# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Master Logic: How a Resin Becomes a Film

Every coating, regardless of resin family, undergoes **film formation**. The mechanism falls into exactly one of three categories — and identifying which one is the most common KTU exam question on this topic.

### Step 1 — Identify the film-formation mechanism
- **Physical Drying (Thermoplastic film):** Solvent evaporates, polymer chains entangle → film. *Examples:* chlorinated rubber, vinyl, solution acrylic, emulsion (after coalescence).
- **Oxidative Curing:** Unsaturated oils/alkyds absorb O₂ from air, form peroxides, crosslink. *Examples:* oil paint, alkyd paint.
- **Co-Reactive Crosslinking:** Two reactive components (e.g., epoxy + amine, NCO + OH) polyadd to build molecular weight *in situ*. *Examples:* epoxy, two-pack PU.

### Step 2 — Connect mechanism to property
| Mechanism | Crosslink Density | Solvent Resistance | Heat Resistance | Re-coat Window |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------|-----------------|----------------|
| Physical drying | Low (linear chains) | Poor–Fair | Poor | Open (any time) |
| Oxidative cure | Medium (C–O–C, C–C) | Good | Good | Closed (after touch-dry) |
| Co-reactive crosslink | High (3-D network) | Excellent | Excellent | Strict pot-life |

### Step 3 — Choose solvent system
- **Water-borne:** Emulsion, water-reducible alkyd, latex acrylic → low VOC, mild odour, easy cleanup.
- **Solvent-borne:** Oil, conventional alkyd, epoxy, PU, silicone, CNR, vinyl → higher VOC, strong solvent power, better film continuity.
- **100 % Solids / Solventless:** Two-pack epoxy, two-pack PU, powder coatings → zero VOC, demanding application.

### Step 4 — Match resin to end-use
The end-use is the **engineering specification** (corrosion, weather, heat, abrasion, chemical). The resin is the **engineering answer**.

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive — Resin-by-Resin Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Emulsion (Latex) Paints

**Chemistry.** A latex paint is a heterogeneous system: a continuous **water phase** (with surfactants, glycols, defoamer, thickener) carrying a discontinuous **polymer-particle phase** (~0.1–0.5 µm spheres of, e.g., poly(methyl methacrylate-co-butyl acrylate)). Pigment is also dispersed (not dissolved) in the water.

**Key constituents (besides binder):**
- **Surfactant / dispersant** — anionic (e.g., sodium lauryl sulfate) or non-ionic (e.g., NP-40) to stabilize latex & pigment.
- **Coalescing solvent** — slow-evaporating glycol ether (e.g., Texanol™) that temporarily plasticizes particles so they fuse below MFT.
- **Freeze-thaw stabilizer** — ethylene glycol.
- **Thickener / rheology modifier** — HEC, HASE, ASE.

**Film-formation theory — the three stages:**

$$ \text{Stage 1: Water evaporation} \rightarrow \text{particles pack} $$

$$ \text{Stage 2: Capillary + surface-tension forces} \rightarrow \text{particles deform} $$

$$ \text{Stage 3: Coalescing solvent + interdiffusion of chains} \rightarrow \text{continuous film} $$

The **Minimum Film Formation Temperature (MFT)** is the lowest temperature at which Stage 2–3 occurs. A good latex is formulated so MFT < 5 °C with coalescent and MFT > 40 °C without (so the dry film is hard, not tacky).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU fact:** Latex paint is a *thermodynamically unstable* two-phase system. The particles are kinetically trapped by surfactant. On drying they fuse, so the dry film is a **continuous polymer phase**, not a dispersion any more.

**Advantages:** Low VOC, fast dry, non-flammable, easy cleanup, breathable (allows water vapour out, resists blistering), good UV resistance, alkyd-compatible.
**Disadvantages:** Sensitive to humidity during cure, poor solvent resistance, lower gloss than solvent-borne, can freeze, sensitive to cold-substrate application.
**Applications:** Interior & exterior architectural wall paints, ceiling paints, primers, elastomeric wall coatings, traffic paints.

---

### 2.2.2 Oil Paints

**Chemistry.** A "drying oil" is a triglyceride of fatty acids containing **conjugated or non-conjugated C=C double bonds**. Linseed oil (≈ 50 % linolenic acid, 18:3) is the classic drying oil. Tung oil (≈ 80 % α-eleostearic acid, conjugated 18:3) is even faster drying.

**Oxidative polymerization pathway:**

$$ \text{R–CH=CH–R'} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{R–CH(OOH)–CH=CH–R'} \quad (\text{hydroperoxide formation}) $$

$$ 2\,\text{R–CH(OOH)–} \rightarrow \text{R–CH(OH)–} + \text{R–C(=O)–} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (\text{peroxide decomposition}) $$

$$ \text{R}^\bullet + \text{R'}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{R–R'} \quad (\text{radical coupling — crosslink}) $$

The drier (cobalt, lead, manganese, zirconium naphthenate or octoate) catalyses the O₂ uptake. The result is a tough, crosslinked film that hardens over 1–7 days.

**Properties:** Excellent wet-edge, good flow & leveling, outstanding brushability, moderate gloss, slow dry, yellowing tendency (linseed), embrittlement with age, weak alkali resistance (saponification by cement/lime substrates).

**Applications:** Traditional house paints, artists' colours, primers on wood, undercoats, varnish (oil + resin), putty.

> [!NOTE]
> **Saponification warning (KTU favourite):** Oil paint films contain ester linkages of the triglyceride. In alkaline environments (fresh plaster, cement, lime), OH⁻ hydrolyses the ester → soap → loss of adhesion. Therefore oil paint is **never** recommended on fresh masonry.

---

### 2.2.3 Alkyd Paints

**Chemistry.** An alkyd resin is synthesized by:

$$ \text{Polybasic acid (phthalic anhydride)} + \text{Polyol (glycerol / pentaerythritol)} + \text{Fatty acid (linseed / soya / coconut)} \rightarrow \text{Alkyd} $$

The reaction proceeds in two conceptual stages:
1. **Polyesterification** of acid + polyol → linear polyester.
2. **Chain termination / modification** by the monobasic fatty acid (the *oil length* determines the % of fatty acid in the resin).

**Oil length classification (very high-yield for KTU):**

$$ \text{Oil length (\%)} = \frac{\text{Mass of oil}}{\text{Total mass of resin (oil + polyol + acid)}} \times 100 $$

| Oil length | % Fatty acid | Solubility in mineral spirit | Typical use |
|------------|--------------|------------------------------|-------------|
| **Short oil** | 30–45 % | Poor (needs strong solvent, e.g., xylene) | Stoving industrial finishes, NC lacquers |
| **Medium oil** | 45–60 % | Good | Air-drying industrial + architectural |
| **Long oil** | 60–80 % | Excellent | Architectural, house paints, primers |

**Curing mechanism:** Identical to oil paint — oxidative crosslinking through the unsaturated fatty acid portion. The polyester backbone supplies hardness, flexibility, gloss, and adhesion; the fatty portion supplies drying ability.

**Modified alkyds (KTU expects awareness):**
- **Silicone-modified alkyd** — superior heat & weather resistance.
- **Urethane-modified alkyd (uralkyd)** — polyisocyanate chain-extension; tougher, faster dry.
- **Acrylic-modified alkyd** — better gloss retention, faster dry.
- **Phenolic-modified alkyd** — superior chemical & moisture resistance.
- **Rosin-modified alkyd** — cheap, hard, but yellows (used in primers, undercoats, varnishes).

**Properties:** Better than oil paint in hardness, gloss, drying, water and alkali resistance. Slower than acrylics/latex. Yellows in dark colours. Excellent wet-edge, brushability.
**Applications:** Architectural trim paints, machinery enamels, automotive refinish (uralkyd), marine topcoats (long-oil alkyd), primers.

---

### 2.2.4 Acrylic Paints

**Chemistry.** Acrylic polymers are built from monomers of the family:

$$ \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH–COOR} \quad (\text{acrylate, when R = H or alkyl}) $$

$$ \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{C(CH}_3\text{)–COOR} \quad (\text{methacrylate, with α-methyl}) $$

By choosing monomers — *hard* (MMA, styrene) vs *soft* (butyl acrylate, 2-EH acrylate) — the **glass-transition temperature, Tg**, is "dialed in":

$$ \frac{1}{T_g} = \sum_i \frac{w_i}{T_{g,i}} \quad (\text{Fox–Flory equation, weight-fraction basis}) $$

where $w_i$ is the weight fraction of monomer $i$ and $T_{g,i}$ its homopolymer Tg in **kelvin**. This is the single most important equation for an acrylic paint formulator.

| Monomer | Homopolymer Tg (°C) | Function in film |
|---------|----------------------|------------------|
| Methyl methacrylate (MMA) | 105 | Hard, weatherable |
| Styrene | 100 | Hard, cheap, yellow-prone |
| Ethyl acrylate | –22 | Soft, flexible |
| Butyl acrylate (BA) | –54 | Soft, hydrophobic, coalescing aid |
| 2-Ethylhexyl acrylate (2-EHA) | –70 | Very soft, internal plasticization |
| Methacrylic acid (MAA) | (high) | Adhesion promoter, neutralizable |
| Acrylic acid (AA) | (high) | Adhesion, thickening on neutralization |
| Hydroxyethyl acrylate (HEA) | — | Crosslinker site for 2K systems |

**Two principal forms:**
1. **Solution acrylic (thermoplastic):** Polymer dissolved in solvent (e.g., toluene, xylene, butyl acetate). Dries by solvent evaporation. Used in automotive refinish, plastics coating.
2. **Latex acrylic (emulsion):** Polymer particles in water. Dries by coalescence. Used in exterior house paint (the "100 % acrylic" premium exterior).

**Properties of 100 % acrylic latex:** outstanding UV & chalk resistance, excellent adhesion to masonry, flexibility, color retention, breathability.
**Applications:** Exterior house paint, automotive OEM & refinish, plastic coatings, high-gloss enamels, can coatings (solution), traffic markings, roof coatings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU trap:** A "vinyl-acrylic" latex is *not* a pure acrylic — it is a copolymer with vinyl acetate (cheaper, less weatherable). A "100 % acrylic" latex is built only from acrylic/methacrylic monomers. Premium exterior requires **pure acrylic**.

---

### 2.2.5 Epoxy Coatings

**Chemistry.** The dominant commercial epoxy is the **diglycidyl ether of bisphenol-A (DGEBA)**, made by:

$$ \text{Bisphenol-A} + 2\,\text{Epichlorohydrin} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH}} \text{DGEBA} + \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

DGEBA carries a **terminal epoxide (oxirane) ring** at each end. The *n* in the formula below is the **degree of polymerization** (n = 0 for liquid resin, n ≈ 0.1 for standard liquid, n = 2–10 for solid resin).

Epoxy equivalent weight (EEW) is the grams of resin containing one mole of epoxide:

$$ \text{EEW} = \frac{\text{Molecular weight of resin unit}}{\text{Number of epoxide groups per molecule}} = \frac{284 + n \times 284}{2} \text{ (for DGEBA)} $$

**Curing (crosslinking) chemistry — the three common types:**

1. **Amine cure (room temperature):**
$$ \text{R–CH–CH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{N–R'} \rightarrow \text{R–CH(OH)–CH}_2\text{–NH–R'} $$

2. **Polyamide cure (room temperature, more flexible):** Long polyamide chains give flexibility, better water resistance, longer pot-life.

3. **Anhydride cure (stoving, 150–200 °C):**
$$ \text{R–CH–CH}_2 + \text{R''CO–O–OCR''} \rightarrow \text{R–CH(OCO–R'')–CH}_2\text{–OCO–R''} $$

**Stoichiometry — KTU high-yield:**

$$ \text{Mass of hardener} = \text{Mass of resin} \times \frac{\text{AHEW (amine hydrogen equivalent weight)}}{\text{EEW}} $$

**Properties:** Excellent adhesion (polar OH groups), outstanding chemical & solvent resistance, low shrinkage, hard, brittle (needs modification for flexibility), poor UV resistance (chalk easily), good corrosion resistance.
**Applications:** Marine primers, pipeline coatings, tank linings (chemical resistance), floor coatings, electronic potting, powder coatings, automotive primers, can coatings (phenolic-modified BPA).

---

### 2.2.6 Polyurethane (PU) Coatings

**Chemistry.** The central reaction is the **polyaddition of an isocyanate with a polyol**:

$$ \text{R–N=C=O} + \text{HO–R'} \rightarrow \text{R–NH–CO–O–R'} \quad (\text{urethane linkage}) $$

The isocyanate group (–NCO) is the reactive handle. With water (moisture cure), the same reaction proceeds via an unstable carbamic acid:

$$ \text{R–NCO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow [\text{R–NH–COOH}] \rightarrow \text{R–NH}_2 + \text{CO}_2 $$

$$ \text{R–NH}_2 + \text{R'–NCO} \rightarrow \text{R–NH–CO–NH–R'} \quad (\text{urea linkage}) $$

**Two-pack vs one-pack:**

| Type | Form | Cure |
|------|------|------|
| **2K PU** | Isocyanate (A) + polyol/acrylic polyol (B) | Mix → polyaddition; pot-life 1–8 h |
| **1K moisture-cure PU** | Prepolymer with free NCO | Atmospheric H₂O |
| **1K blocked-NCO PU** | NCO blocked with MEK-oxime, phenol | Unblocks at 150–180 °C (stoving) |
| **1K dispersion PU** | Aqueous PU dispersion | Water evap → coalescence |
| **1K alkyd-urethane (uralkyd)** | Isocyanate-modified alkyd | Oxidative + urethane formation |

**NCO index (KTU formula):**

$$ \text{NCO index} = \frac{\text{Equivalents of NCO}}{\text{Equivalents of OH}} \times 100 \quad (\%) $$

A typical 2K PU is balanced at index ≈ 100–110 %. Excess NCO → harder, more chemical-resistant, but more brittle and CO₂ gassing possible.

**Properties:** Exceptional abrasion & scratch resistance, flexibility, gloss, chemical resistance, wide hardness range (Shore A 60 to Shore D 80), tunable by polyol choice. Aromatic isocyanates (TDI, MDI) yellow; aliphatic (HDI, IPDI) are light-stable.
**Applications:** Wood floor finishes, aircraft & automotive OEM clearcoats, marine topcoats, sports flooring, leather finishes, anti-graffiti, plastic coatings, sports equipment, industrial maintenance.

---

### 2.2.7 Silicone Coatings

**Chemistry.** The backbone is the **siloxane (–Si–O–Si–)** chain with organic substituents R (methyl, phenyl, vinyl) on silicon. Precursors are chlorosilanes:

$$ \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{Si} \xrightarrow{\text{Cu, 300 °C}} \text{CH}_3\text{SiCl}_3 \text{ (and Me}_2\text{SiCl}_2\text{)} \xrightarrow{\text{hydrolysis}} \text{silicone resin} $$

**Curing:** Room-temperature-vulcanizing (RTV) silicones crosslink by **moisture-induced condensation of silanol (Si–OH) groups**:

$$ \text{≡Si–OH} + \text{HO–Si≡} \rightarrow \text{≡Si–O–Si≡} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

Often a tin catalyst (dibutyltin dilaurate) accelerates the reaction.

**Types of silicone coating resins:**

| Type | Substituents | Property |
|------|--------------|----------|
| **Pure silicone** | R = CH₃, C₆H₅ | Heat, UV, weather, release |
| **Silicone-alkyd** | Alkyd backbone + Si–O–Si grafts | Heat, weather, conventional paint application |
| **Silicone-acrylic** | Acrylic + silicone | Exterior durability, dirt pickup resistance |
| **Silicone-polyester** | Polyester + Si | Coil-coating, high-temperature appliance |
| **Silicone-epoxy** | Epoxy + Si | Heat-resistant industrial maintenance |

**Properties:** Continuous service from –60 °C to +600 °C (silicone-ceramic even higher), outstanding UV/weather/ozone resistance, water repellency, low surface energy (release), electrical insulation.
**Applications:** Chimneys, exhaust pipes, furnaces, ovens, heat exchangers, exterior architectural (silicone-acrylic), release coatings (cookware, baking paper), weather-resistant topcoats for skyscrapers, insulators, anti-graffiti overcoat.

---

### 2.2.8 Chlorinated Rubber Coatings

**Chemistry.** Natural rubber (cis-1,4-polyisoprene) is dissolved in CCl₄ and chlorinated with Cl₂:

$$ (\text{C}_5\text{H}_8)_n + 2n\,\text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow (\text{C}_5\text{H}_6\text{Cl}_4)_n \quad \text{(at ~80–100 °C)} $$

Typical commercial product: **Parlon™** (∼ 65 wt % Cl, viscosity grades 5–125 cP). The high chlorine content imparts:
- **Flame retardancy** (Cl liberates HCl, dilutes O₂).
- **Chemical resistance** (saturated, polar, bulky).
- **Moisture resistance** (low water permeability).
- **Insolubility in mineral oils & aliphatic solvents** (resistant to fuels).

**Film formation:** Pure **physical drying** by solvent evaporation. Thermoplastic, no crosslinking. The polymer is dissolved in aromatic solvents (xylene, toluene) or in solvent blends.

**Properties:** Excellent water & chemical resistance, vapour impermeability, fast dry, recoatable at any time, can be built up to thick films without sagging, **thermoplastic** (re-softens on heating, so not for hot surfaces), **limited UV resistance** (dehydrochlorinates, discolours), low solids at sprayable viscosity.

**Applications:** Swimming pools (over chlorinated rubber primer), chemical-plant steelwork, marine boot-topping, road-marking, anti-slip deck paints, traffic paints, masonry sealers, flame-retardant coatings, primers for plaster.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU note on regulation:** CCl₄ (the original chlorination solvent) is now banned under the Montreal Protocol. Modern manufacture uses **aqueous suspension chlorination** of rubber latex. This is the environmentally correct process expected in KTU 2024 answers.

---

### 2.2.9 Vinyl Coatings

**Chemistry.** Several distinct polymers fall under the "vinyl" umbrella:

| Polymer | Abbreviation | Tg (°C) | Key Property |
|---------|--------------|---------|--------------|
| Poly(vinyl chloride) | PVC | 80 | Hard, chemically resistant, plasticizer-dependent |
| Poly(vinyl acetate) | PVAc | 32 | Tough, transparent, hydrolysis-prone |
| Poly(vinylidene chloride) | PVDC | –19 | Outstanding O₂ & moisture barrier |
| Poly(vinyl formal) | PVF | ≈ 105 | Wire enamel |
| Poly(vinyl butyral) | PVB | ≈ 50 | Tough, adhesion, safety-glass interlayer |
| Vinyl chloride–vinyl acetate copolymer | VC-VAc | 60–75 | Solution coating resin |

The **poly(vinyl chloride)** coating chemistry:

$$ n\,\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CHCl} \xrightarrow{\text{peroxide, heat}} (\text{–CH}_2\text{–CHCl–})_n $$

PVC is too rigid to be a coating binder on its own; it must be **plasticized** (e.g., with phthalate esters) or **copolymerized** (e.g., VC-VAc) to make it film-forming.

**Film formation:** **Physical drying** (solvent evaporation) for solution vinyls; **fusion** for organosols and plastisols (the dispersion of PVC particles in plasticizer gels/fuses at 160–180 °C).

**Properties:** Excellent chemical & water resistance, good weathering, very low fuel permeability, transparent, food-safe, can be formulated as plastisol (no solvent, 100 % solids), thermoplastic, sensitive to heat (HCl liberation above 140 °C — needs heat stabilizers).

**Applications:** Can linings (food & beverage cans — epoxy-modified), marine antifouling, swimming-pool paint (alternative to CNR), tank linings, coil coatings, automotive underbody, paper & foil coatings, fabric coatings, flooring (PVC wear layer), wire & cable insulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU special note — Vinyl vs Acrylic in exterior paint:**
> Vinyl-acrylic latex is cheaper than 100 % acrylic but has *worse* UV resistance and *worse* wet adhesion. For premium exterior, always choose **100 % acrylic**. For interior flat, vinyl-acrylic is fine.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are examinable. Memorize the **form**, the **units**, and the **typical range**.

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|-------------------|---------|----------------------|
| F1 | $\text{Oil length (\%)} = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{m_{\text{resin}}} \times 100$ | Alkyd resin classification | Short ≤ 45, Medium 45–60, Long ≥ 60 % |
| F2 | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \sum_i \dfrac{w_i}{T_{g,i}}$ (K) | Fox–Flory: Tg of acrylic copolymer | Tg in K; $w_i$ weight fraction |
| F3 | $\text{EEW (DGEBA)} = \dfrac{284 + 284\,n}{2}$ | Epoxy equivalent weight | Liquid: 170–190 g/eq |
| F4 | $\text{Loading (phr)} = \dfrac{\text{AHEW}}{\text{EEW}}$ | Grams hardener per 100 g resin (for amines) | phr 30–60 typical |
| F5 | $\text{NCO index (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{eq NCO}}{\text{eq OH}} \times 100$ | Polyurethane stoichiometry | 95–115 % typical |
| F6 | $M_{\text{CO}_2}^{\,\text{from water}} = \dfrac{44 \times m_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}{18}$ | CO₂ released per kg of water reacted in 1K PU | g CO₂ / g water = 2.2 |
| F7 | $\text{MFT} \approx 0.95\,T_g - 25\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Empirical link between latex Tg and MFT | °C |
| F8 | $\rho_{\text{film}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{non-volatiles}}}{V_{\text{wet film}}}$ | Film density for coverage calculation | g/mL |
| F9 | $\text{Spreading rate (m}^2\text{/L)} = \dfrac{10 \times \%\,\text{vol. solids}}{\text{WFT in μm}}$ | Theoretical coverage | m²/L |
| F10 | $\text{CV} = \dfrac{V_{\text{pigment}} + V_{\text{extender}}}{V_{\text{non-volatile}}}$ | Pigment Volume Concentration (CPVC concept) | dimensionless, 0–1 |
| F11 | $\%\,\text{Cl in CNR} \approx 65\ \text{wt \%}$ | Chlorinated-rubber grade check | wt % |
| F12 | $\text{PVC Tg} \approx 80\ ^{\circ}\text{C},\ \text{PEO Tg} \approx -67\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Tg memory values (KTU quick recall) | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical instruction to KTU students:** When using formulas inside any answer, **always state the formula, substitute values, and show the final answer with units**. The board examiner awards partial credit generously only when intermediate steps are visible.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The choice of resin decides *whether the coating is fit for purpose*:
- **Corrosion protection of a ship hull** → epoxy primer + PU or antifouling vinyl topcoat.
- **Chemical plant storage tank** → epoxy-phenolic (high chemical resistance) or chlorinated rubber.
- **High-temperature chimney** → silicone or silicone-ceramic.
- **Domestic interior wall** → PVA or vinyl-acrylic emulsion.
- **Automotive OEM clearcoat** → 2K aliphatic PU.
- **Exterior masonry** → 100 % acrylic latex.
- **Wooden floor** → 2K PU or moisture-cure PU.
- **Food-can lining** → vinyl or epoxy-phenolic.

Every engineering specification (corrosion, weather, heat, abrasion, chemical, hygiene) has a **resin family that is the textbook-correct answer**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Module 2 of KTU PBPOT404 is largely *qualitative descriptive chemistry* rather than *quantitative derivations*. However, three quantitative problems are repeatedly tested: (a) **Fox–Flory Tg calculation for acrylic copolymers**, (b) **Alkyd oil-length calculation**, and (c) **Epoxy stoichiometry / PU NCO-index**. The worked examples below cover all three, plus a chlorinated-rubber chlorine-balance example for completeness.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Fox–Flory Tg of an Acrylic Copolymer

**Problem.** A latex exterior paint is built from a copolymer of 40 wt % methyl methacrylate (MMA, $T_{g1} = 105\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$) and 60 wt % butyl acrylate (BA, $T_{g2} = -54\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$). Calculate the copolymer $T_g$.

**Step 1 — Convert °C to K (Fox–Flory requires absolute temperature).**

$$ T_{g1} = 105 + 273 = 378\ \text{K} $$

$$ T_{g2} = -54 + 273 = 219\ \text{K} $$

**Step 2 — Insert into Fox–Flory.**

$$ \frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g2}} = \frac{0.40}{378} + \frac{0.60}{219} $$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the fractions.**

$$ \frac{0.40}{378} = 1.0582 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ \frac{0.60}{219} = 2.7397 \times 10^{-3} $$

**Step 4 — Sum.**

$$ \frac{1}{T_g} = 1.0582 \times 10^{-3} + 2.7397 \times 10^{-3} = 3.7979 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1} $$

**Step 5 — Take reciprocal.**

$$ T_g = \frac{1}{3.7979 \times 10^{-3}} = 263.3\ \text{K} $$

**Step 6 — Convert back to °C.**

$$ T_g = 263.3 - 273 = -9.7\ ^{\circ}\text{C} $$

**Interpretation.** A Tg of ≈ –10 °C means the film is flexible at room temperature (good) but the latex must be formulated with a coalescing solvent so that the practical **MFT** is well below the expected ambient application temperature (5–40 °C in Kerala). A purely 40/60 MMA/BA latex would have an MFT near Tg and would fail to coalesce in winter.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Alkyd Oil-Length Calculation

**Problem.** A medium-oil alkyd is to be prepared from 380 g linseed oil, 130 g glycerol, and 175 g phthalic anhydride. Determine the oil length and classify the alkyd.

**Step 1 — Total mass of resin.**

$$ m_{\text{total}} = 380 + 130 + 175 = 685\ \text{g} $$

**Step 2 — Apply oil-length formula.**

$$ \text{Oil length} = \frac{380}{685} \times 100 = 55.47\ \% $$

**Step 3 — Classify.**

$$ 45\ \% < 55.47\ \% < 60\ \% \Rightarrow \textbf{Medium-oil alkyd} $$

**Step 4 — Practical consequence.** A medium-oil alkyd is soluble in mineral spirit (white spirit), dries by oxidation, and is the *workhorse* of architectural and air-drying industrial finishes.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Epoxy Stoichiometry (DGEBA + Amine)

**Problem.** A liquid DGEBA resin has EEW = 185 g/eq. The hardener is a polyamide with AHEW = 245 g/eq. Calculate the mass of hardener required per 100 g of resin.

**Step 1 — Write the stoichiometric relationship.**

$$ \text{Loading (phr)} = \frac{\text{AHEW}}{\text{EEW}} \times 100 = \frac{245}{185} \times 100 = 132.4\ \text{phr} $$

**Step 2 — Convert to mass.**

$$ m_{\text{hardener}} = 1.324 \times 100\ \text{g resin} = 132.4\ \text{g hardener} $$

**Step 3 — Interpret.** Roughly **1.32 kg of polyamide hardener is required per 1 kg of DGEBA resin**. This is typical — polyamides are large molecules and contribute flexibility, impact resistance, and moisture tolerance to the cured film.

**Step 4 — Pot-life consideration.** A 2K epoxy mixed with polyamide typically has a pot-life of 30 min – 4 h at 25 °C. The student should always state the **molar ratio and the equivalent ratio** in a KTU answer, not just "phr".

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Polyurethane NCO Index

**Problem.** A 2K PU wood-coating clearcoat is formulated from an acrylic polyol (OH value = 110 mg KOH/g, equivalent weight = 510 g/eq) and an HDI trimer (NCO content = 23.0 %, equivalent weight = 183 g/eq). For an NCO index of 105 %, calculate the mass of isocyanate per 100 g of polyol.

**Step 1 — Compute equivalents in 100 g of polyol.**

$$ \text{eq}_{OH} = \frac{100\ \text{g}}{510\ \text{g/eq}} = 0.1961\ \text{eq} $$

**Step 2 — Required equivalents of NCO at 105 % index.**

$$ \text{eq}_{NCO} = 0.1961 \times 1.05 = 0.2059\ \text{eq} $$

**Step 3 — Mass of isocyanate.**

$$ m_{NCO} = 0.2059 \times 183\ \text{g/eq} = 37.7\ \text{g} $$

**Step 4 — Mix ratio (by mass).**

$$ \text{Polyol : Isocyanate} = 100 : 37.7 \approx 2.65 : 1\ \text{(by mass)} $$

**Step 5 — Cross-check OH value consistency.** Verify: 56 100 ÷ OH value = 56 100 ÷ 110 = 510 g/eq ✓.

**Interpretation.** The mix ratio is convenient (about 2.65 parts polyol to 1 part HDI trimer), and the small excess (5 %) ensures complete reaction of all OH groups and a slight "self-cure" with atmospheric moisture for surface hardness.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Chlorine Content of Chlorinated Rubber

**Problem.** 1000 g of natural rubber (C₅H₈, $M_0$ = 68 g/mol repeating unit) is chlorinated. After the reaction, the polymer has the empirical formula C₅H₆Cl₄. Calculate (a) the mass of Cl₂ consumed, and (b) the wt % Cl in the product.

**Step 1 — Moles of repeat unit.**

$$ n = \frac{1000}{68} = 14.706\ \text{mol} $$

**Step 2 — Cl atoms added per repeat unit.**

$$ \text{From C}_5\text{H}_8 \text{ to C}_5\text{H}_6\text{Cl}_4 \Rightarrow 4\ \text{Cl atoms gained per unit} $$

**Step 3 — Mass of Cl added.**

$$ m_{\text{Cl}} = 14.706 \times 4 \times 35.45 = 2085\ \text{g} $$

**Step 4 — Total mass of product.**

$$ m_{\text{prod}} = 1000 + 2085 = 3085\ \text{g} $$

**Step 5 — Mass of Cl₂ consumed.**

$$ m_{\text{Cl}_2} = \frac{2085}{0.709} = 2941\ \text{g} \quad (\text{since 1 mol Cl}_2 = 70.9\ \text{g provides 70.9 g Cl}) $$

**Step 6 — wt % Cl in product.**

$$ \%\,\text{Cl} = \frac{2085}{3085} \times 100 = 67.6\ \% $$

**Interpretation.** This matches the commercial spec (≈ 65 wt % Cl). The result is a thermoplastic binder with excellent chemical & flame resistance but a tendency to dehydrochlorinate on UV exposure — hence chlorinated-rubber paints are usually top-coated with a UV-resistant alkyd or are pigmented with hiding pigments to protect the binder.

---

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Resin Family vs. Property

The following matrix is the single most-reproduced table in KTU board answers for Module 2. It is **strongly recommended** to reproduce it (or a version of it) in any 14-mark question.

| Property | Emulsion | Oil | Alkyd | Acrylic | Epoxy | PU | Silicone | CNR | Vinyl |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Film mechanism** | Coalescence | Oxidative | Oxidative | Coalescence/evap. | Crosslink | Polyaddition | Condensation | Evap. | Evap./fusion |
| **Solvent base** | Water | Solvent | Solvent | Water or solv. | Solvent / 100 % | Solvent / 100 % | Solvent | Solvent | Solvent / plastisol |
| **Gloss (high)** | Low–Med | Low–Med | Med–High | Med–High | Med–High | Very high | Med | Med | Med |
| **UV resistance** | Excellent | Fair (yellows) | Fair (yellows) | Excellent | Poor (chalks) | Excellent (aliph.) | Excellent | Poor–Fair | Good |
| **Chemical resistance** | Poor | Poor–Fair | Fair | Fair | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Heat resistance** | < 100 °C | < 120 °C | < 130 °C | < 130 °C | < 180 °C | < 150 °C | **< 600 °C** | < 80 °C | < 100 °C |
| **Water resistance** | Good (breathes) | Fair | Good | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Alkali resistance** | Excellent | **Poor** | Fair | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Good | Good |
| **Flexibility** | Excellent | Med | Med | Excellent | Brittle* | Tunable | Good | Fair | Fair |
| **VOC** | Very low | Medium | Medium | Low–Med | Med–High | Med–High | Med | Med | Low (plastisol 0) |
| **Cost (binder)** | Low | Low–Med | Low–Med | Med–High | Med–High | High | High | Med | Low–Med |
| **Cure time (RT)** | Hours | Days | Hours–Days | Hours | Hours–Days | Hours–Days | Hours–Days | Minutes | Minutes–Hours |

*Epoxy is rigid unless flexibilized (e.g., with polyamide, long-chain diamines, or rubber toughening).

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 model]** Define an *emulsion paint*. Explain the role of the coalescing solvent and the concept of Minimum Film Formation Temperature (MFT).

**Model Answer (10 lines):**
An emulsion paint is a water-borne coating in which the binder is a synthetic polymer (typically acrylic, vinyl-acrylic, or styrene-acrylic) supplied as discrete ~0.1–0.5 µm particles dispersed in water with the help of surfactants. Film formation occurs by coalescence, *not* by solvent evaporation. The **coalescing solvent** (e.g., Texanol, butyl carbitol) is a slow-evaporating, high-boiling, partially water-soluble glycol ether that temporarily softens the polymer particles, allowing them to deform and fuse into a continuous film. The **MFT** is the lowest temperature at which this fusion is complete; it is typically 5–25 °C below the polymer Tg. After film formation, the coalescent slowly evaporates, and the final film is hard, glossy, and water-resistant.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 model]** Distinguish between *short-oil*, *medium-oil* and *long-oil* alkyd resins. Give one example application of each.

**Model Answer (8 lines):**
Alkyd resins are polyesters of polybasic acids (phthalic anhydride) and polyhydric alcohols (glycerol, pentaerythritol) modified with drying-oil fatty acids. The **oil length** is the percentage of fatty acid in the total resin mass.
- **Short oil (30–45 %):** Highly aromatic-soluble, fast drying, hard, used in **stoving industrial finishes and NC lacquers**.
- **Medium oil (45–60 %):** The workhorse, soluble in mineral spirit, used in **air-drying industrial and architectural enamels**.
- **Long oil (60–80 %):** Highly flexible, exterior-durable, used in **house paints, primers, and exterior trim enamels**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU ESE, each module is examined by a 14-mark question with internal choice (i.e., the student picks one of two). Below, **Question A and Question B** are alternative independent choices on the same module. Sub-parts (a) and (b) are 7 marks each.

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) **[7 marks]** Discuss the chemistry, curing mechanism, and key properties of **epoxy coatings**. Explain the role of EEW and AHEW in two-pack epoxy formulation.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Chemistry (2 marks).** Epoxy coatings are based on the diglycidyl ether of bisphenol-A (DGEBA), synthesized by reacting bisphenol-A with epichlorohydrin in the presence of NaOH. The structure carries terminal epoxide (oxirane) rings:

$$ \text{CH}_2\text{–CH–CH}_2\text{–O–(C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–C(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–O–)}_n\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH–CH}_2 $$

The value of $n$ (degree of polymerization) ranges from 0 (liquid resin, EEW ≈ 170) to 2–10 (solid resin, EEW ≈ 500–2000).

2. **Curing mechanism (2 marks).** The terminal epoxide ring reacts with amine (–NH₂) and amide (–CONH–) groups of polyamine or polyamide hardeners through a **nucleophilic ring-opening** at room temperature:

$$ \text{R–NH}_2 + \text{Epoxide} \rightarrow \text{R–NH–CH}_2\text{–CH(OH)–} $$

The secondary amine formed can react with a second epoxide, building a **three-dimensional network** that is densely crosslinked. With polyamides, the long aliphatic chains provide flexibility.

3. **EEW and AHEW (2 marks).**
- **EEW (Epoxy Equivalent Weight):** mass of resin containing one mole of epoxide, g/eq. For DGEBA, $\text{EEW} = (284 + 284n)/2$.
- **AHEW (Amine Hydrogen Equivalent Weight):** mass of hardener containing one mole of active NH hydrogen, g/eq.
- **Stoichiometry:** $\text{Mass hardener per 100 g resin} = \dfrac{\text{AHEW}}{\text{EEW}} \times 100\ \text{phr}$.

4. **Key properties (1 mark).** Excellent adhesion, very high chemical and solvent resistance, low shrinkage, high mechanical strength, but **brittle and UV-sensitive** (chalking). Applications: marine primers, tank linings, floor coatings, pipeline coatings, electronic potting.

#### (b) **[7 marks]** Compare and contrast **chlorinated rubber** and **vinyl** coatings. Discuss their chemistry, film-formation mechanism, and the engineering applications where each is preferred.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Chlorinated Rubber | Vinyl (PVC/PVAc/PVDC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Monomer/polymer** | Cis-1,4-polyisoprene chlorinated to ≈ 65 wt % Cl | Vinyl chloride → PVC; vinyl acetate → PVAc; vinylidene chloride → PVDC |
| **Synthesis** | Natural rubber latex chlorinated (modern aqueous process, not CCl₄) | Free-radical polymerization of monomer with peroxide initiator |
| **Backbone polarity** | C–C with C–Cl side groups; saturated | C–C with C–Cl / C–OCOCH₃ side groups; saturated |
| **Film mechanism** | Pure physical drying (solvent evaporation); thermoplastic | Pure physical drying or fusion (plastisol); thermoplastic |
| **Solvent** | Aromatic (xylene, toluene) | Aromatic or ketone (for solution); plastisol needs none |
| **Water resistance** | Excellent; vapour-impermeable | Excellent |
| **Chemical resistance** | Excellent to acids/alkalis | Excellent to acids, salts, fuels |
| **UV resistance** | Poor (dehydrochlorinates, discolours) | Good (PVC), but needs heat stabilizer above 140 °C |
| **Heat limit** | Softens ≈ 60–80 °C | PVC softens ≈ 80 °C (unplasticized) |
| **Re-coat** | Open (any time) | Open |
| **Application 1** | Swimming pools, chemical-plant steelwork | Can linings (food cans) |
| **Application 2** | Road markings, traffic paint, marine boot-topping | Marine antifouling, flooring wear layers |
| **Application 3** | Masonry sealer, flame-retardant primer | Wire & cable insulation, paper/foil coating |
| **Health/environment** | Modern non-CCl₄ process preferred | Plasticizer migration (phthalates) being phased out |

**Conclusion (½ mark).** Chlorinated rubber is preferred where **flame retardancy, fast air-dry, and chemical resistance** are needed. Vinyl coatings are preferred where **food contact safety, plastisol build-up, or fuel/oil resistance** are needed. Both are thermoplastic and hence unsuitable for high-temperature service.

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) **[7 marks]** Explain the **chemistry and curing mechanism of two-pack polyurethane coatings**. Describe the NCO index and its effect on film properties.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Chemistry (2 marks).** The central reaction is the polyaddition of a **polyisocyanate (–N=C=O)** with a **polyol (–OH)** to form a **urethane (carbamate) linkage**:

$$ \text{R–N=C=O} + \text{HO–R'} \rightarrow \text{R–NH–CO–O–R'} $$

In a two-pack (2K) PU coating, Part A is the polyisocyanate (commonly HDI trimer for light-stable clearcoats, or TDI/MDI for primers) and Part B is the polyol (acrylic polyol, polyester polyol, or polyether polyol).

2. **Curing mechanism (2 marks).** On mixing at room temperature, the isocyanate and hydroxyl groups polyadd to build molecular weight *in situ*, ultimately forming a densely crosslinked **polyurethane–polyurea network**. The reaction is catalysed by **dibutyltin dilaurate (DBTDL)** or tertiary amines (DABCO). In 1K moisture-cure PU, free isocyanate groups react with atmospheric water:

$$ \text{R–NCO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{R–NH}_2 + \text{CO}_2 $$

$$ \text{R–NH}_2 + \text{R'–NCO} \rightarrow \text{R–NH–CO–NH–R'} \quad (\text{urea}) $$

3. **NCO index and stoichiometry (2 marks).** The NCO index is the equivalent ratio expressed as a percentage:

$$ \text{NCO index (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{equivalents of NCO}}{\text{equivalents of OH}} \times 100 $$

- Index ≈ **100 %** → stoichiometric, balanced film, tough and flexible.
- Index **> 100 %** (105–115 %) → excess NCO; harder, more chemical-resistant film; also reacts with moisture to give urea crosslinks.
- Index **< 100 %** → excess OH; softer, more flexible, but under-cured.

4. **Effect on properties (1 mark).** Higher NCO index improves hardness, solvent resistance, and crosslink density, but reduces flexibility and may cause CO₂ gassing (pinholes) if moisture is present. The choice of polyol (acrylic vs polyester vs polyether) controls the balance of hardness, flexibility, and outdoor durability.

5. **Applications (½ mark).** Wood floor finishes, automotive OEM clearcoats (aliphatic HDI), aircraft topcoats, marine topcoats, sports flooring, anti-graffiti coatings.

#### (b) **[7 marks]** Write short notes on:
   1. **Silicone coatings** (3 marks)
   2. **Vinyl coatings** (2 marks)
   3. **Modified alkyds** (2 marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Silicone coatings (3 marks).** Silicone coatings are based on **polysiloxane (–Si–O–Si–)** backbones, prepared by hydrolysis and condensation of organochlorosilanes (e.g., methyltrichlorosilane, dimethyldichlorosilane, phenyltrichlorosilane). The inorganic Si–O–Si chain gives exceptional **heat resistance (up to 600 °C for pure silicones)**, **UV/ozone/weather resistance**, **low surface energy (release)**, and **flexibility at low temperature (–60 °C)**. Cure occurs by **moisture-induced condensation of silanol (Si–OH) groups**, often catalysed by tin (dibutyltin dilaurate). The principal variants are **silicone-alkyd**, **silicone-acrylic**, **silicone-polyester** and **silicone-epoxy**, which combine the heat/UV resistance of silicone with the application ease of conventional resins. Applications: chimneys, exhaust manifolds, ovens, electrical insulation, weather-resistant architectural topcoats, release coatings for cookware, anti-graffiti overcoats.

2. **Vinyl coatings (2 marks).** Vinyl coatings are based on polymers of vinyl chloride, vinyl acetate, or vinylidene chloride. PVC is rigid (Tg ≈ 80 °C) and requires plasticization or copolymerization (e.g., VC–VAc) to form a film. They dry purely by **solvent evaporation (physical drying)**; in the form of **plastisols** they fuse at 160–180 °C. They have excellent water, chemical, fuel and abrasion resistance, very low vapour permeability, and are widely used in **food-can linings, marine antifouling paints, swimming-pool paints, flooring wear layers, wire and cable insulation, and paper/foil coatings**. They yellow on UV exposure and need heat stabilizers above 140 °C.

3. **Modified alkyds (2 marks).** Alkyds are often chemically modified to improve specific properties:
   - **Urethane-modified alkyd (uralkyd):** isocyanate chain extension; faster dry, tougher, better abrasion.
   - **Silicone-modified alkyd:** superior heat and UV resistance; used in marine and exterior maintenance.
   - **Acrylic-modified alkyd:** better gloss retention, faster dry, better colour.
   - **Phenolic-modified alkyd:** superior chemical, moisture, and saponification resistance; used in can coatings and marine primers.
   - **Rosin-modified alkyd:** cheap, hard, faster dry; used in primers, undercoats, and varnishes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic (Module 2):**
> 1. **Confusing emulsion with solution paint.** Emulsion = water-borne, discrete particles, film by coalescence. Solution = solvent-borne, polymer dissolved, film by solvent evaporation. Examiners repeatedly deduct 2 marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of driers in oil and alkyd paints.** Cobalt/manganese/zirconium naphthenates are not optional — they are essential catalysts of the oxidative cure. Always name them.
> 3. **Citing "oil paint is alkali-resistant".** This is FALSE — oil paints saponify on alkaline substrates. Many students lose marks here.
> 4. **Calling PU "polymerized urethane".** The correct word is "polyaddition" of NCO + OH.
> 5. **Confusing EEW and AHEW in epoxy stoichiometry.** The formula is *phr = AHEW/EEW × 100*, not the reverse.
> 6. **Skipping the role of chlorinated rubber's chlorine.** The 65 wt % Cl is what gives the chemical and flame resistance. Without mentioning it, the answer is incomplete.
> 7. **Calling silicone "silicon paint".** It is **silicone** (the polymer), not silicon (the element).
> 8. **Forgetting that NCO index > 100 leads to urea crosslinks via atmospheric moisture.** This is the basis of "over-indexed" PU clearcoats.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this page. Memorize before the KTU exam.**

- [ ] **Emulsion paint** = water-borne, polymer particles 0.1–0.5 µm, film by coalescence, needs coalescing solvent (Texanol), key concept is MFT.
- [ ] **Oil paint** = drying oil (linseed/tung), oxidative polymerization, needs driers (Co/Mn/Zr), saponifies on alkali substrates.
- [ ] **Alkyd** = polyester + fatty acid. Oil-length: short (≤ 45), medium (45–60), long (≥ 60 %).
- [ ] **Acrylic** = polymers of acrylate/methacrylate esters. Tg tuned by Fox–Flory: $\frac{1}{T_g} = \sum \frac{w_i}{T_{g,i}}$.
- [ ] **Epoxy** = DGEBA + amine/polyamide. Stoichiometry: phr = AHEW/EEW × 100. Best for chemical resistance. Brittle & UV-sensitive.
- [ ] **Polyurethane** = NCO + OH → urethane. NCO index = (eq NCO / eq OH) × 100. Aromatic = yellows, aliphatic = light-stable. Best abrasion resistance.
- [ ] **Silicone** = polysiloxane (–Si–O–Si–). Heat up to 600 °C, UV-stable, moisture-cure. Cures by Si–OH + HO–Si → Si–O–Si + H₂O.
- [ ] **Chlorinated rubber** = natural rubber + 65 wt % Cl. Film by solvent evaporation only. Chemical & flame resistance.
- [ ] **Vinyl** = PVC, PVAc, PVDC. Thermoplastic; rigid PVC plasticized or copolymerized. Food-can, antifouling, flooring.
- [ ] **Film-formation types:** (i) Physical drying (CNR, vinyl, solution acrylic); (ii) Oxidative (oil, alkyd); (iii) Crosslinking (epoxy, PU); (iv) Coalescence (emulsion, acrylic latex).
- [ ] **Alkali resistance ranking (best→worst):** Silicone ≈ Epoxy ≈ 100% Acrylic > Vinyl > CNR > PU > Alkyd > Oil.
- [ ] **Heat resistance ranking (best→worst):** Silicone (600 °C) > Silicone-ceramic > Epoxy (180) > Alkyd (130) > PU (150) > Acrylic (130) > Oil (120) > Vinyl (100) > CNR (80) > Emulsion (< 100).
- [ ] **For premium exterior house paint in Kerala climate:** 100 % acrylic latex (UV, alkali, monsoon).
- [ ] **For chemical plant storage tank interior:** epoxy-phenolic or CNR.
- [ ] **For swimming pool:** CNR or epoxy (over CNR-compatible primer).
- [ ] **For high-temperature chimney:** silicone or silicone-ceramic.
- [ ] **For wooden sports floor:** 2K aliphatic PU.
- [ ] **For food-can interior:** epoxy-phenolic or organosol vinyl.
- [ ] **For aircraft topcoat:** 2K aliphatic PU (HDI trimer + acrylic polyol).
- [ ] **Remember three KTU "examiner's darling" equations:** Fox–Flory Tg; Oil length; Epoxy phr = AHEW/EEW × 100.
- [ ] **Remember the KTU 2024 environment angle:** VOC reduction → water-borne and 100 % solids; CCl₄-free chlorination; low-VOC PU; modern emulsion paints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 2 Notes — PBPOT404 Paints & Surface Coatings — KTU 2024 Scheme.**
> Practice the worked examples, reproduce the comparative matrix, and draw the classification tree from memory in the exam. These three artefacts together cover 70–80 % of the marks available on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification Based on Application: Appliance Finishes, Automotive Finishes, Coil Coatings, Can Coatings, Marine Coatings, Aircraft Finishes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT404 - PAINTS & SURFACE COATINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification Based on Application: Appliance Finishes, Automotive Finishes, Coil Coatings, Can Coatings, Marine Coatings, Aircraft Finishes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Application-Based Classification of Surface Coatings

In the field of surface coatings technology, classification based on **application** refers to the systematic categorization of paints and coatings according to the **end-use industry** or the **specific substrate-environment combination** they are engineered to protect and decorate. This classification is fundamentally distinct from classification by binder chemistry (e.g., alkyds, epoxies, polyurethanes) or by physical form (e.g., solvent-borne, waterborne, powder). Instead, it groups coatings by the **performance envelope** they must satisfy in real-world service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2):**
> "Surface coatings can be classified based on their end-use application. Each application demands a unique combination of physical, chemical, mechanical, and aesthetic properties. The six major industrial application categories are Appliance, Automotive, Coil, Can, Marine, and Aircraft finishes."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Suit of Armour" Intuition

Think of a surface coating as a **suit of armour** tailor-made for a knight going into a specific battle:

- A **chef's apron** (appliance finish) must resist kitchen splatters and frequent wiping.
- A **race-car driver's suit** (automotive finish) must withstand high speeds, sun, rain, fuel splashes, and look stunning.
- A **prefabricated roof tile** (coil coating) is painted in a factory and then exposed to decades of weather.
- A **tin can** (can coating) must protect acidic foods without contaminating the contents.
- A **ship's hull** (marine coating) fights the relentless battle against salt water and biological growth.
- A **jet fighter** (aircraft finish) endures extreme altitude, freezing temperatures, hydraulic fluid, and supersonic raindrops.

Each "suit" uses a different combination of materials, thicknesses, and application methods — yet the principle is the same: **form follows function**.

### 1.3 The Six Application Categories — A Glance

| Sl. No. | Application Category | Typical Substrate | Defining Performance Demand |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Appliance Finishes** | Cold-rolled steel, pre-treated steel | Gloss, stain resistance, scratch resistance, moderate bake |
| 2 | **Automotive Finishes** | Phosphated steel, galvanized steel, plastics | Weatherability, color, gloss, OEM multi-layer system |
| 3 | **Coil Coatings** | Galvanized steel, aluminum coils | Continuous high-speed application, extreme flexibility |
| 4 | **Can Coatings** | Tin-plated steel, aluminum | Food contact safety, sterilization resistance, flexibility |
| 5 | **Marine Coatings** | Steel hulls, superstructures | Salt-water corrosion, anti-fouling, long service life |
| 6 | **Aircraft Finishes** | Aluminum alloys, titanium, composites | Hydraulic fluid resistance, -50 °C to +50 °C service, weight savings |

> [!NOTE]
> The **end-use environment** dictates the choice of **binder resin, pigment system, additive package, application method, and curing schedule**. Hence, understanding application-based classification is essential for KTU students aspiring to work in formulation R&D, industrial sales, or quality control in the paints industry.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Appliance Finishes — Detailed Engineering Analysis

**Definition:** Coatings applied to white goods (refrigerators, washing machines, microwave ovens, dishwashers, dryers, air-conditioners) primarily to provide a tough, glossy, stain-resistant, and aesthetically pleasing skin over a pre-treated metal substrate.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Substrate Preparation:** Cold-rolled steel is degreased, phosphated (iron or zinc phosphate), and chromate-passivated to ensure paint adhesion.
2. **Primer Application:** A corrosion-resistant primer (often an epoxy or polyester) is roller-coated or spray-applied at a dry film thickness (**DFT**) of approximately **5–10 µm**.
3. **Topcoat Application:** A polyester or alkyd-melamine baking enamel is applied via electrostatic spray at a DFT of **20–30 µm**.
4. **Curing:** The wet film is cured in a **convection oven** at **160–180 °C** for 15–20 minutes (metal temperature). This is termed a **"bake"** schedule.
5. **Inspection:** Gloss is measured at **60°** using a gloss meter (typical reading: **>85 GU** — Gloss Units). Pencil hardness should be **≥ 2H**. Cross-hatch adhesion must be **ASTM 5B** (no flaking).

**Why these choices?**
- **Polyester resins** give excellent flexibility and overbake yellowing resistance.
- **Alkyd-melamine** systems give a deep, "wet-look" gloss prized in refrigerators.
- **Powder coatings** (epoxy-polyester hybrid) are now preferred for environmental reasons (zero **VOC** — Volatile Organic Compounds).

**KTU Formula & Performance Cheat Sheet (Appliance Finishes):**

| Parameter | Symbol / Method | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gloss at 60° | $G_{60}$ | $\geq 85$ | GU (Gloss Units) |
| Pencil Hardness | $H_p$ | $\geq 2H$ | — |
| DFT of topcoat | $d_t$ | $20 - 30$ | $\mu m$ |
| Total DFT (primer + top) | $d_{total}$ | $30 - 40$ | $\mu m$ |
| MEK Double Rub | $N_{rub}$ | $\geq 100$ | rubs |
| Curing Temperature | $T_{cure}$ | $160 - 180$ | $^\circ C$ |
| Curing Time | $t_{cure}$ | $15 - 20$ | minutes |

> [!NOTE]
> **MEK (Methyl Ethyl Ketone) Double Rub Test (ASTM D4752):** A cotton pad soaked in MEK is rubbed back-and-forth on the coating; the number of rubs required to expose the substrate indicates **cure density**. A value $\geq 100$ rubs is a hallmark of a properly cured appliance finish.

---

### 2.2 Automotive Finishes — Detailed Engineering Analysis

**Definition:** The most demanding and technologically sophisticated decorative-protective coating system, applied to passenger cars, commercial vehicles, and two-wheelers. It must satisfy aesthetics, durability, corrosion resistance, and increasingly stringent environmental regulations.

**The Five-Layer OEM Architecture:**

1. **Cathodic Electrodeposition (CED) Primer:** Deposited by immersing the car body in a paint bath and using electricity. The body is the **cathode**, attracting anionic epoxy-amine resins. DFT: **18–25 µm**. Provides outstanding **creep-back corrosion resistance** (i.e., corrosion does not propagate under a stone chip).
2. **Anti-Chip Primer / Primer Surfacer:** Applied by bell-atomizer spray, often a PVC plastisol. DFT: **30–40 µm**. Absorbs stone impacts.
3. **Basecoat:** Provides color and effects (metallic, pearlescent, solid). Today, predominantly **waterborne** to meet VOC regulations. DFT: **12–20 µm**.
4. **Clearcoat:** A transparent layer providing gloss, UV resistance, and chemical resistance. Most commonly a **2-component (2K) acrylic-polyurethane**. DFT: **35–45 µm**.
5. **Wax and Cavity Treatments:** Applied to hidden areas for additional corrosion protection.

**Refinish Paints (Body-Shop Repaints):** These are lower-bake, air-dry, or infrared-cure systems. The four layers are: primer-surfacer, basecoat, clearcoat. VOC rules have driven the adoption of **waterborne basecoats** (e.g., Standox, Spies Hecker, PPG Envirobase).

**Key Performance Metrics — Automotive:**

| Parameter | OEM Target | Refinish Target | Test Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gloss at 20° | $\geq 90$ GU | $\geq 85$ GU | ASTM D523 |
| DOI (Distinctness of Image) | $\geq 90$ | $\geq 80$ | ASTM D5767 |
| Xenon Arc Weathering | $\geq 3000$ hr (Florida) | $\geq 2000$ hr | SAE J1960 |
| Cross-hatch Adhesion | 5B | 5B | ASTM D3359 |
| Stone Chip Resistance | $\leq 2$ (rating) | — | ASTM D3170 |
| Acid Spot Resistance | Pass | Pass | OEM Internal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DOI (Distinctness of Image)** is a critical visual metric in automotive finishes. It measures how sharply the coating reflects a defined image. Modern luxury cars demand **DOI > 95**. DOI is computed from the sharpness of a reflected light-dark boundary.

**Mathematical Approximation for DOI Measurement:**

$$
DOI = 100 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{\sigma_{slope}}{K_{ref}}\right)
$$

where $\sigma_{slope}$ is the standard deviation of the slope of the reflected image edge, and $K_{ref}$ is a reference constant defined by the instrument.

---

### 2.3 Coil Coatings — Detailed Engineering Analysis

**Definition:** A high-speed, continuous industrial process in which a metal coil (galvanized steel, Galvalume®, aluminum) is pre-painted in a coil-coating line before being fabricated into roofing, wall cladding, gutters, garage doors, and white-goods panels.

**The Continuous Process Flow:**

1. **Entry Section:** Coil is unwound and the edges are stitched.
2. **Cleaning:** Alkaline degrease removes rolling oils and contaminants.
3. **Pretreatment:** Chromate, no-rinse chrome (e.g., **Henkel Alodine**), or zirconium-based conversion coating.
4. **Primer Application:** Roller-coater applies a polyester or epoxy primer at **5–8 µm** DFT.
5. **Topcoat Application:** Roller-coater or curtain-coater applies a polyester, **PVDF (Polyvinylidene Fluoride — Kynar 500®)**, silicone-modified polyester, or plastisol topcoat at **15–25 µm** DFT.
6. **Backing Coat:** Optional protective coat on the reverse side.
7. **Curing:** Single or dual ovens with peak metal temperatures of **PMT 216 °C** (for polyester) to **249 °C** (for PVDF).
8. **Cooling & Recoiling:** Water quench, dry, and rewind.

**Line Speeds:** Modern lines run at **60–200 m/min**. The total cycle time from cleaning to recoiling is often less than **2 minutes**.

**Coil Coating Baked-Film Standards (ASTM D3794 / AAMA 2604-2605):**

| Performance Property | Polyester | PVDF (Kynar 500®) |
|---|---|---|
| Outdoor Durability (years) | $10 - 15$ | $25 - 35$ |
| Chalk Resistance | Good | Excellent |
| Color Retention | Good | Excellent |
| Cost | Moderate | High (3–5× polyester) |
| Flexibility (T-bend) | 2T – 3T | 0T – 1T |
| Solvent Resistance | Good | Excellent |

> [!NOTE]
> **T-Bend Test (ASTM D4145):** The painted metal is bent around itself a number of times (e.g., 1T = one metal-thickness bend). A "1T" rating means the coating survives a tight 1-thickness bend without cracking — critical for roll-forming into roofing profiles.

---

### 2.4 Can Coatings — Detailed Engineering Analysis

**Definition:** Specialized coatings applied to the inside and outside of metal food cans, beverage cans, aerosol cans, and general-line containers. The interior coating must be **food-contact safe** (FDA 21 CFR 175.300 / EU 1935/2004 compliant), while the exterior must resist printing inks, scratching, and sterilization cycles.

**Two Types of Cans:**

- **Three-Piece Cans (3PC):** Body blank, two ends. Steel body welded and side-striped.
- **Two-Piece Cans (2PC):** Either drawn-and-ironed (D&I) steel/aluminum beverage cans, or deep-drawn (DRD) food cans.

**Coating Architecture:**

1. **Inside (Body) Spray or Roller Coat:** A **gold-colored epoxy-phenolic** resin (Bisphenol-A epoxy + phenolic resin) is the workhorse — historically called the "gold lacquer." It resists acidic foods (tomato, fruit) and retort sterilization up to **121 °C** for 90 minutes.
2. **Inside End-Lining Compound:** A water-based latex dispersion (often a **vinyl organosol** or butadiene-styrene) extruded onto the can end for gasket formation.
3. **Outside Base Coat (WBC — White Base Coat):** A polyester or alkyd roller-coated for printability.
4. **Inks:** Reverse-offset, dry-offset, or digital printing inks.
5. **Outside Overvarnish (Canned Beverage Can):** An epoxy-ester or acrylic varnish for gloss and abrasion resistance.

> [!WARNING]
> **BPA Migration Concern:** Bisphenol-A-based epoxy has come under regulatory pressure due to endocrine-disruption concerns. Modern alternatives include:
> - **Vinyl organosols** (used in beer and carbonated beverage cans)
> - **Acrylic dispersions** (e.g., PPG ISOLITE®, Valspar VALPure®)
> - **Polyester resins** (BPA-NI — "BPA Non-Intent")
> This is a **high-frequency KTU 2024 viva question**.

**Key Performance Metrics — Can Coatings:**

| Test | Method | Typical Pass Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| Wedge Bend Flexibility | ASTM D3281 | No cracks at $90\%$ elongation |
| Retort Resistance | $121^\circ C / 90 \text{ min}$ | No softening, blistering, or flavor change |
| Enamel Rater (Inside) | Industrial test | Porosity $< 5$ mA |
| Fabric Lining Integrity | Cross-hatch + solvent | 5B adhesion |
| Global Migration | EU 10/2011 | $< 10 \text{ mg/dm}^2$ |

**Enamel Rater Logic:** The can is filled with an electrolyte (1% NaCl), and a low DC voltage is applied. Current flow indicates **porosity** in the coating. Lower mA = better.

---

### 2.5 Marine Coatings — Detailed Engineering Analysis

**Definition:** Coatings formulated to protect ships, offshore platforms, and port structures from the most aggressive corrosion environment — **seawater immersion, salt-spray atmosphere, UV radiation, mechanical abrasion, and biofouling**.

**Ship Hull Zone Classification:**

- **Topside:** Above the waterline, exposed to sun, salt spray, and weather.
- **Boottop:** Transition zone (waterline ± 0.5 m), alternating wet/dry.
- **Bottom (Underwater):** Continuously immersed, requires anti-fouling.
- **Superstructure:** Above main deck, decorative and camouflage.

**Multi-Layer Marine System:**

1. **Shop Primer / Blast Primer:** Zinc-silicate or zinc-rich epoxy, DFT **20–40 µm**, applied at the shipyard before plate erection.
2. **Anti-Corrosive Primer:** High-build epoxy (often **coal-tar epoxy** historically; today pure epoxy or epoxy-polyamide), DFT **125–200 µm** per coat. Two or three coats may be applied.
3. **Tie Coat:** A vinyl or epoxy tie coat to bond anti-corrosive to anti-fouling, DFT **50 µm**.
4. **Anti-Fouling (AF) Topcoat:** The only coating layer exposed to seawater continuously. DFT **100–200 µm**.

**Anti-Fouling Chemistry — Evolution:**

| Generation | Type | Active Biocide | Service Life |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1st | **Conventional / Free-association** | Cuprous oxide ($Cu_2O$) | $12 - 24$ months |
| 2nd | **TBT Self-Polishing Copolymer (SPC)** | Tributyltin acrylate | $36 - 60$ months |
| 3rd | **Tin-free SPC (TF-SPC)** | $Cu_2O$ + booster biocides | $36 - 60$ months |
| 4th | **Fouling-Release Silicones** | None — slippery surface | $36 - 60$ months |
| 5th | **Biomimetic / Hydrogels** | None or natural products | Emerging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IMO Anti-Fouling Systems (AFS) Convention (2001):** Banned **TBT (Tributyltin)** from 2008 onwards due to severe impact on marine life (imposex in sea snails). Modern marine coatings use **$Cu_2O$ + organic biocides** (e.g., zinc pyrithione, tralopyril) or **fouling-release silicone elastomers** (e.g., Intersleek®).

**Self-Polishing Copolymer (SPC) Reaction — Simplified Chemistry:**

$$
\text{Polymer-Cu-Carboxylate} + \text{Na}^+ + \text{Cl}^- \xrightarrow{\text{seawater}} \text{Polymer-COO}^-\text{Na}^+ + CuCl
$$

The sodium ions in seawater exchange with copper in the polymer matrix, slowly dissolving the polymer surface and releasing biocides. The surface is **continuously renewed** — a self-polishing mechanism.

**Cathodic Protection Compatibility:** Anti-fouling paints are designed to be compatible with **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** or **sacrificial zinc anodes**. The potential on the hull is typically maintained at **–850 mV (Ag/AgCl reference)**.

---

### 2.6 Aircraft Finishes — Detailed Engineering Analysis

**Definition:** A specialized class of high-performance coatings engineered for civilian and military aircraft. These must operate across a punishing environmental envelope: **–50 °C to +50 °C**, supersonic rain erosion, UV at high altitude, hydraulic fluid (Skydrol® LD-4, phosphate ester), fuel (JP-8, Jet A-1), de-icing fluids, and ozone.

**The Standard Aircraft Painting System:**

1. **Surface Pretreatment:** Solvent wipe, alkaline clean, acid etch (for aluminum), conversion coating (**Alodine 1200**, chromate; or non-chrome trivalent chromium).
2. **Primer:** **Epoxy-polyamide primer** pigmented with **strontium chromate** (corrosion inhibitor) — traditional. Modern formulations use **zinc phosphate or non-chrome inhibitors**. DFT **15–25 µm**.
3. **Topcoat:** **Aliphatic polyurethane** (2K, isocyanate-cured polyester or acrylic polyol). Provides weatherability, flexibility, and gloss. DFT **50–75 µm**.
4. **Markings / Livery:** Polyurethane stencil inks and decals.

**Total Aircraft Film Weight Budget:** Typically **300–500 g/m²** wet — even a 1 g/m² saving on a Boeing 747 saves **~250 kg** over the airframe, equivalent to substantial fuel savings.

**Aircraft-Specific Tests & Targets:**

| Test | Method | Pass Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| Skydrol® Resistance | 7 days @ 70 °C | No softening, blistering, color change |
| Heat Resistance | 1000 hr @ 120 °C | $\Delta E \leq 2$ |
| Low-Temperature Flexibility | 24 hr @ –54 °C | No cracking (mandrel bend 1/4") |
| Rain Erosion (Whirling Arm) | 30 min @ 200 m/s | Erosion rate $\leq 25 \mu m$/min |
| UV/Weathering (Xenon) | 2000 hr | $\Delta E \leq 2$, gloss retention $\geq 90\%$ |
| Fluid Resistance | Jet A-1, hydraulic fluid | No degradation |

**Rain Erosion Physics:** At supersonic tip speeds (helicopter rotor blades) or high-velocity flight, water droplets impact at **>150 m/s** and cause cumulative coating loss. A simple hydrodynamic impact model approximates the erosion rate:

$$
\dot{e} = K \cdot \rho_{drop} \cdot v_{imp}^{n}
$$

where $\dot{e}$ is the erosion rate ($\mu m$/s), $K$ is a material constant, $\rho_{drop}$ is water density, $v_{imp}$ is the impact velocity, and $n \approx 5\text{–}6$ for polymer coatings.

**Camouflage Pigments:** Military aircraft use special **IR-reflective pigments** so the aircraft signature is reduced in night-vision scopes. Coatings are also tested for **radar-absorbing** properties when combined with composite substrates.

---

### 2.7 Master Comparison — "What Each Application Needs Most"

| Application | #1 Critical Property | #2 Critical Property | #3 Critical Property | Substrate | Cure |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Appliance | Stain resistance | Gloss | Hardness | Steel | Bake |
| Automotive | Weatherability | DOI / Color match | Stone-chip resistance | Phosphated steel | Multi-bake |
| Coil | Formability | UV resistance | Line speed | Galv. steel / Al | Continuous bake |
| Can | Food safety | Flexibility | Sterilization | Tin-plate / Al | Bake / Sterilize |
| Marine | Anti-fouling | Salt corrosion | Long life | Steel | Ambient / Force-cure |
| Aircraft | Hydraulic fluid resistance | Flexibility | Weight | Al / Composites | Ambient or low bake |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Estimating Total Coating Weight per Aircraft

**Problem:** Estimate the dry coating weight per square meter of a typical narrow-body aircraft (e.g., Airbus A320) given the following layer structure:

- Epoxy Primer: DFT = 20 µm, density = 1.45 g/cm³
- Polyurethane Topcoat: DFT = 60 µm, density = 1.20 g/cm³

**Goal:** Compute the total dry film weight per unit area (g/m²).

**Step 1 — Convert DFT to meters:**

$$
d_{primer} = 20 \,\mu m = 20 \times 10^{-6} \, m
$$

$$
d_{top} = 60 \,\mu m = 60 \times 10^{-6} \, m
$$

**Step 2 — Recall the relationship between mass per area, density, and thickness:**

$$
W = \rho \times d
$$

where $W$ is weight per unit area (g/m²), $\rho$ is density (g/m³), and $d$ is thickness (m).

**Step 3 — Convert densities from g/cm³ to g/m³:**

$$
\rho_{primer} = 1.45 \,\frac{g}{cm^3} = 1.45 \times 10^6 \,\frac{g}{m^3}
$$

$$
\rho_{top} = 1.20 \,\frac{g}{cm^3} = 1.20 \times 10^6 \,\frac{g}{m^3}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute weight per unit area for each layer:**

$$
W_{primer} = 1.45 \times 10^6 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} = 29.0 \,\frac{g}{m^2}
$$

$$
W_{top} = 1.20 \times 10^6 \times 60 \times 10^{-6} = 72.0 \,\frac{g}{m^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum the layers:**

$$
W_{total} = 29.0 + 72.0 = 101.0 \,\frac{g}{m^2}
$$

**Result:** The total dry coating weight is approximately **101 g/m²**. For a typical narrow-body aircraft with a painted area of ~1000 m², the total paint mass is around **100 kg** — illustrating why aircraft coatings must be engineered for minimal weight while still surviving extreme conditions.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Estimating the Theoretical Coverage of a Coil Coating Line

**Problem:** A coil-coating line processes galvanized steel coils of width **1.2 m**, line speed **100 m/min**, primer roller applies **7 µm** of primer, and topcoat roller applies **20 µm** of polyester. Calculate the **volume of paint consumed per hour** by each roller.

**Step 1 — Wet film thickness (assume 100% solids for the roller process):**

$$
d_{primer} = 7 \,\mu m = 7 \times 10^{-6} \, m
$$

$$
d_{top} = 20 \,\mu m = 20 \times 10^{-6} \, m
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the painted area per minute:**

$$
A_{min} = w \times v = 1.2 \, m \times 100 \, \frac{m}{min} = 120 \,\frac{m^2}{min}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to volume per minute:**

$$
V_{primer/min} = A_{min} \times d_{primer} = 120 \times 7 \times 10^{-6} = 8.4 \times 10^{-4} \,\frac{m^3}{min}
$$

$$
V_{top/min} = A_{min} \times d_{top} = 120 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} = 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \,\frac{m^3}{min}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to volume per hour (multiply by 60):**

$$
V_{primer/hr} = 8.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 60 = 5.04 \times 10^{-2} \,\frac{m^3}{hr} = 50.4 \,\frac{L}{hr}
$$

$$
V_{top/hr} = 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 = 1.44 \times 10^{-1} \,\frac{m^3}{hr} = 144 \,\frac{L}{hr}
$$

**Result:** The line consumes approximately **50.4 L/hr of primer** and **144 L/hr of topcoat**. Such calculations are the foundation of **production planning and cost estimation** in coil-coating factories.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Calculating the Galvanic Compatibility of Marine Cathodic Protection

**Problem:** A steel hull is protected by a zinc sacrificial anode. The exposed surface area of the hull is **A = 1000 m²**. The required protection current density is **$i = 0.2 \, A/m^2$** (for an epoxy-coated hull in tropical seawater). How much **zinc** (in kg) is consumed per year?

**Step 1 — Total protection current required:**

$$
I_{total} = i \times A = 0.2 \times 1000 = 200 \, A
$$

**Step 2 — Faraday's Law of Electrolysis:**

$$
m = \dfrac{M \times I \times t}{n \times F}
$$

where $m$ is the mass dissolved (g), $M$ is the molar mass of zinc (**65.4 g/mol**), $n$ is the valency (**2** for Zn $\to$ Zn$^{2+}$), and $F$ is Faraday's constant (**96,485 C/mol**).

**Step 3 — Compute seconds in 1 year:**

$$
t = 365 \times 24 \times 3600 = 31,536,000 \, s
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$
m = \dfrac{65.4 \times 200 \times 31{,}536{,}000}{2 \times 96{,}485}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate numerator:**

$$
65.4 \times 200 = 13{,}080
$$

$$
13{,}080 \times 31{,}536{,}000 = 4.125 \times 10^{11} \, g \cdot C / mol
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate denominator:**

$$
2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970
$$

**Step 7 — Divide:**

$$
m = \dfrac{4.125 \times 10^{11}}{192{,}970} \approx 2.138 \times 10^{6} \, g \approx 2138 \, kg
$$

**Result:** Approximately **2,138 kg of zinc per year** must dissolve to protect the hull. In practice, ICCP (Impressed Current) systems are used for large vessels; sacrificial anodes are practical only for small boats.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Properties vs. Application

| Property \ Application | Appliance | Automotive | Coil | Can | Marine | Aircraft |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gloss (60°) | High | Ultra-high | Moderate | High | Moderate | High |
| UV Resistance | Low | Very High | High | — | Very High | Ultra-High |
| Chemical Resistance | Low | High | Moderate | High | High | Ultra-High |
| Flexibility | Low | Moderate | Ultra-high | Ultra-high | Moderate | Ultra-high |
| Hardness | High | High | Moderate | High | Moderate | High |
| Heat Resistance | 180 °C | 160 °C | 250 °C | 130 °C | 80 °C | 120 °C |
| FDA / Food Safe | No | No | No | **YES** | No | No |
| Salt Spray (hr) | 240 | 1000 | 1000 | 240 | >5000 | 1000 |

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix above is a **high-yield revision tool** for the KTU 2024 ESE. Examiners frequently set "comparison" questions asking students to differentiate between two of these application categories.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Decision Logic for Application Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Substrate & End-Use Identified] --> B{What is the Service Environment?}
    B -->|Indoor household| C[Appliance Finish]
    B -->|Outdoor road vehicle| D[Automotive Finish]
    B -->|Pre-fab metal sheet| E[Coil Coating]

    B -->|Ship / offshore / seawater| G[Marine Coating]
    B -->|Aircraft / aerospace| H[Aircraft Finish]
    C --> C1[Polyester / Alkyd-Melamine bake]
    D --> D1[5-Layer OEM: CED + Primer + Base + Clear]
    E --> E1[Polyester / PVDF roller + continuous bake]
    F --> F1[Epoxy-Phenolic inside + WBC + Varnish]
    G --> G1[Zinc-rich Epoxy + Tie + SPC Anti-fouling]

```

### 4.2 Layer Architecture — Automotive OEM vs. Aircraft Finish

```mermaid
    A0[Substrate Phosphated Steel] --> A1[1 CED Epoxy Primer 25um]

```

### 4.3 Process Flow — Marine Coating System (Bottom + Topside)

```mermaid
    M3 --> M4[Anti-Corrosive Epoxy Coat 2]
```
