
---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any three performance requirements of a marine anti-fouling coating and state the active biocide used in modern tin-free formulations.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) Requirement 1: Prevention of biofouling (barnacles, mussels, algae) on the underwater hull.
- (1 mark) Requirement 2: Long service life (typically 36–60 months) and self-polishing behavior to maintain a smooth surface.
- (1 mark) Requirement 3: Compatibility with cathodic protection (ICCP) and resistance to seawater immersion.
- (Bonus line if asked:) Active biocide: **Cuprous oxide ($Cu_2O$)** plus organic boosters such as zinc pyrithione or tralopyril.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Why is a 2K aliphatic polyurethane preferred over alkyd-melamine for aircraft topcoats?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) Aliphatic polyurethanes exhibit **superior UV and weather resistance** — they do not chalk or yellow like aromatic polyurethanes or alkyds.
- (1 mark) They have **excellent flexibility at low temperatures** (down to –54 °C), critical for high-altitude flight.
- (1 mark) They offer **superior chemical resistance** to Skydrol® hydraulic fluid, jet fuel, and de-icing chemicals.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** (Model Question, Module 2 Choice)

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **five-layer OEM automotive coating system** with the function of each layer, the typical resin chemistry, and the recommended dry film thickness (DFT).

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare **polyester** and **PVDF (Kynar 500®)** as topcoat resins for **coil coatings** in terms of weatherability, flexibility, chemical resistance, and cost.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The Five-Layer OEM System:**

| Layer | Resin | DFT | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Cathodic Electrocoat (CED) | Epoxy-amine (anionic) | 18–25 µm | Throw into cavities, creep-back corrosion resistance |
| 2. Anti-Chip / Primer Surfacer | PVC plastisol or epoxy | 30–40 µm | Absorb stone impacts; smooth out surface |
| 3. Basecoat | Waterborne polyurethane-acrylic | 12–20 µm | Color and effect (metallic/pearl) |
| 4. Clearcoat | 2K Acrylic-polyurethane | 35–45 µm | Gloss, DOI, UV, chemical resistance |
| 5. Wax / Cavity Treatment | Paraffin + corrosion inhibitor | Variable | Hidden-area protection |

(1 mark for naming each layer, 1 mark for correct resin, 1 mark for correct DFT, 1 mark for the function of each. The final 3 marks are awarded for the integrated diagram-like flow and for noting that **waterborne basecoats dominate due to VOC regulations**.)

**(b) Polyester vs. PVDF for Coil Coatings:**

| Property | Polyester | PVDF (Kynar 500®) |
|---|---|---|
| Weatherability | 10–15 years | 25–35 years |
| Chalk Resistance | Good | Excellent |
| Gloss Retention | Good | Excellent |
| Flexibility (T-bend) | 2T – 3T | 0T – 1T |
| Chemical Resistance | Good | Excellent (Kynar is the gold standard) |
| Cost | Moderate | High (3–5×) |
| Typical Use | Residential roofing, garage doors | Premium architecture (Burj Khalifa, airports) |

(1 mark each for the six properties; 1 mark for correct application examples.)

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **evolution of anti-fouling coatings** for marine hulls, from conventional cuprous-oxide paints to modern self-polishing copolymers (SPC) and fouling-release silicones. State the IMO regulatory driver behind this evolution.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **unique performance requirements of aircraft coatings**, with reference to Skydrol® resistance, low-temperature flexibility, rain erosion, and weight.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Evolution of Anti-Fouling Coatings:**

1. (2 marks) **Conventional AF (1950s–1980s):** Rosin-based matrix loaded with **$Cu_2O$**. Rosin is slightly soluble in seawater, so the biocide is released by **diffusion** as the matrix slowly dissolves. Service life: 12–24 months. Drawback: rapid depletion of biocide, no controlled polishing.
2. (2 marks) **TBT Self-Polishing Copolymers (1980s–2008):** Acrylate or methacrylate copolymers with **tributyltin (TBT)** ester side groups. The TBT group hydrolyzes in seawater — the polymer surface slowly dissolves, releasing TBT biocide and **renewing the surface**. Service life: 36–60 months. **IMO banned TBT in 2008** under the AFS Convention due to endocrine disruption in marine life (imposex in sea snails).
3. (2 marks) **Tin-Free SPC (2008–onwards):** Same self-polishing mechanism but with **$Cu_2O$ + organic booster biocides** (zinc pyrithione, tralopyril, medetomidine). Service life: 36–60 months.
4. (1 mark) **Fouling-Release Silicones (e.g., Intersleek®):** No biocide. A **silicone elastomer** with low surface energy (~20 mN/m) — fouling organisms cannot grip the surface and are washed off by the ship's motion. Used on cruise liners and naval vessels.

**(b) Aircraft Coating Performance Requirements:**

- (2 marks) **Skydrol® Resistance:** Phosphate-ester hydraulic fluid is extremely aggressive. Coatings must withstand 7-day immersion at 70 °C with no softening or color change.
- (2 marks) **Low-Temperature Flexibility:** Coatings must not crack at **–54 °C** (high-altitude cruise). This requires low-Tg polyurethane topcoats and epoxy primers with plasticizers.
- (2 marks) **Rain Erosion:** At 200 m/s, raindrops cause mechanical erosion. Coatings are tested on a **whirling arm rig**; an erosion rate $\leq 25 \mu m$/min is the standard.
- (1 mark) **Weight:** Every gram counts. The whole aircraft painting system must stay around 300–500 g/m² to minimize fuel burn.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in KTU 2024 Examinations:**
> 1. **Conflating Cathodic Electrocoating (CED) with anodic electrocoating (AED):** Modern OEM uses **cathodic** (body is the cathode, paint is anionic). Writing "anodic" loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing "self-polishing" with "ablative":** SPC polymers chemically hydrolyze; ablative coatings are mechanically eroded. Examiners penalize the swap.
> 3. **Stating TBT is still used:** TBT is **banned globally under IMO AFS 2001 since 2008**. Listing TBT as a current biocide is a factual error that costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Missing the food-safety aspect of can coatings:** Always mention **FDA 21 CFR 175.300** or **EU 10/2011** compliance for interior can coatings.
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical problems:** DFT must be in **µm** or **m** with consistent units, and weight in **g/m²** — not just numbers.
> 6. **Vague references to "epoxy":** Always specify **bisphenol-A epoxy**, **novolac epoxy**, or **epoxy-phenolic** — vague answers lose 1 mark each.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Classification by Application):**

- **Appliance Finishes:** Polyester / alkyd-melamine **baking enamels**; baked at **160–180 °C**; gloss $\geq 85$ GU at 60°; pencil hardness $\geq 2H$; DFT 20–30 µm. Powder coatings increasingly used.
- **Automotive OEM:** **Five layers** — CED (cathodic epoxy), anti-chip primer, waterborne basecoat, 2K PU clearcoat, wax. Total DFT ~120–150 µm. Waterborne basecoats dominate due to **VOC regulations**.
- **Coil Coatings:** Continuous high-speed line (**60–200 m/min**). Topcoat resins: **polyester** (10–15 yr), **PVDF** (25–35 yr), silicone-modified polyester, plastisol. PMT 216–249 °C.
- **Can Coatings:** **Two-piece** (D&I beverage, DRD food) and **three-piece** cans. Interior: **epoxy-phenolic gold lacquer** (BPA-based) or **vinyl organosol** / **acrylic** (BPA-NI). Must pass **retort** at 121 °C / 90 min and **global migration** tests.
- **Marine Coatings:** Multi-layer — **shop primer → anti-corrosive epoxy → tie coat → anti-fouling**. AF types: conventional $Cu_2O$ → TBT-SPC (banned 2008) → TF-SPC → **fouling-release silicones**. SPC chemistry based on **Na⁺/Cu²⁺ ion exchange**.
- **Aircraft Finishes:** **Epoxy primer + aliphatic 2K PU topcoat**. Must survive **Skydrol®**, **–54 °C**, **rain erosion**, and stay within **~300–500 g/m²** total weight.
- **Common Threads:** All protective coatings balance **substrate prep + film build + cure schedule + performance test**. The end-use environment dictates the **resin + pigment + additive + process** triad.
- **Key Numbers to Memorize:** TBT ban year (**2008**); PMT for PVDF (**249 °C**); low-T aircraft test (**–54 °C**); salt-spray for marine (**>5000 hr**); required gloss for OEM auto (**>90 GU at 20°**).
- **Regulatory Hooks:** **FDA 21 CFR 175.300** (can), **IMO AFS 2001** (marine), **REACH** (general), **VOC directives** (auto and can).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanism of Film Formation and Drying: Factors Affecting Coating Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT404 - PAINTS & SURFACE COATINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mechanism of Film Formation and Drying: Factors Affecting Coating Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Film Formation** is the sequence of physico-chemical transformations by which a liquid coating system (solvent-borne, water-borne, or solventless) is converted into a continuous, adherent, and mechanically functional solid film on a substrate. The process—commonly referred to as **"drying" or "curing"** in industrial parlance—encompasses solvent loss, particle coalescence, chemical crosslinking, and final vitrification of the binder matrix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (PBPOT404, Module 3):** Students must be able to distinguish between the terms *drying* (physical loss of volatiles) and *curing* (chemical crosslinking), and correlate the underlying mechanism with the final coating's performance envelope.

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome (CO) Mapping — KTU 2024 Scheme**
> - **CO3:** *Understand the mechanisms by which liquid coatings transform into solid films and identify the operational factors governing final film properties.*
> - **Bloom's Level:** Understand (L2) → Analyze (L4)
> - **Cognitive Skills Tested:** Mechanism classification, factor identification, defect-property correlation.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wet Sponge to Dry Brick" Model

Imagine a freshly mixed cake batter poured into a pan:
- The **water and air bubbles** in the batter are analogous to the **solvent and entrapped volatiles** in a wet coating.
- As the oven heats the batter, water **evaporates** (analogous to *solvent evaporation*), and starch granules **swell and fuse** (analogous to *coalescence* of latex particles).
- The protein (gluten) **chemically crosslinks** (analogous to *oxidative polymerization* of alkyd resins) to give the final firm cake.

The transformation from a *flowable liquid* to a *rigid functional solid* is exactly what happens when a coating "dries." The cake cannot return to a batter—and likewise, a properly cured coating becomes a **thermodynamically irreversible** functional barrier.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this analogy works:** It captures the *three simultaneous physical events*—evaporation, particle consolidation, and chemical bonding—that define film formation across virtually all coating chemistries.

---

### 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Definition | Units/Metric |
|------|------------|--------------|
| **Drying Time** | Time required to reach a specific dryness stage | **hours (h)** or **minutes (min)** |
| **Dry Film Thickness (DFT)** | Thickness of cured film on substrate | **microns (µm)** |
| **Wet Film Thickness (WFT)** | Thickness immediately after application | **microns (µm)** |
| **Solids by Volume (SBV)** | Volume fraction of non-volatile components | **% (v/v)** |
| **Critical Pigment Volume Concentration (CPVC)** | Pigment/binder ratio at maximum packing | **% (v/v)** |
| **MEK Double Rubs** | Solvent resistance / cure indicator | **number of rubs** |

---

### 1.4 The Four Universal Film Formation Mechanisms

According to KTU 2024 Module 3, film formation proceeds by one (or a combination) of the following mechanisms:

1. **Evaporation (Physical Drying)** — Loss of volatile solvent/water; *thermoplastic* films (e.g., nitrocellulose lacquers, acrylic latexes).
2. **Oxidation** — Reaction of binder with atmospheric oxygen; *oxidatively cured* films (e.g., alkyds, oil-based paints).
3. **Polymerization (Chemical Curing)** — Crosslinking via condensation or addition; *thermoset* films (e.g., epoxies, polyurethanes, melamine).
4. **Coalescence** — Fusion of discrete polymer particles in latex systems; *film formation in water-borne* coatings.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stage-Wise Breakdown of Film Formation

Regardless of the mechanism, **all coatings pass through four sequential physical states** during drying/curing:

#### Stage I — *Wet Stage (Application Phase)*
- Coating is a viscous, flowable fluid.
- **Rheology:** Newtonian or shear-thinning behaviour.
- Process: Substrate wetting, levelling, and sag control dominate.

#### Stage II — *Set-to-Touch (Initial Drying)*
- Surface becomes tack-free; solvent evaporation at the air-film interface.
- **Bubbles of solvent vapour** escape through the surface.
- For latex coatings: water evaporation brings particles into close contact.

#### Stage III — *Through-Dry (Bulk Drying)*
- Solvent removal from the **interior** of the film.
- For latex: **capillary forces** deform particles → **coalescence**.
- For oxidizing systems: peroxides form → initiate radical crosslinking.

#### Stage IV — *Full Cure (Hard Dry)*
- Final crosslink density achieved.
- For thermosets: complete network formation; film is now **insoluble and infusible**.
- Final properties: hardness, gloss, chemical resistance, flexibility.

---

### 2.2 Detailed Mechanism 1: Solvent Evaporation (Physical Drying)

**Mechanism Type:** *Reversible drying* (the film can be re-dissolved in the original solvent).

**Governing Physics:** Diffusion-controlled mass transfer described by Fick's Second Law:

$$ \frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2} $$

where:
- $C$ = solvent concentration in the film
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient of the solvent in the binder ($m^2/s$)
- $x$ = depth into the film from the surface
- $t$ = time

**Key Industrial Examples:**
- **Nitrocellulose lacquers** (furniture finishes, automotive repair).
- **Acrylic lacquers** (clear coats, nail polish).
- **Chlorinated rubber paints** (marine, swimming pools).

**Drying Stages (Recoed Classification):**
- *Stage A – Dust-free:* 5–15 min
- *Stage B – Touch-dry:* 15–30 min
- *Stage C – Hard-dry:* 1–2 hours
- *Stage D – Through-dry:* 4–8 hours

---

### 2.3 Detailed Mechanism 2: Oxidative Polymerization (Air-Drying)

**Mechanism Type:** *Irreversible curing* via free-radical chain reaction with atmospheric $\text{O}_2$.

**Three-Phase Reaction Sequence:**

1. **Induction Period:** Driers (Co, Mn, Pb, Zr salts) catalyze hydroperoxide formation at unsaturation sites of the oil/alkyd backbone.
2. **Propagation:** Allylic radicals crosslink via C–C and C–O–C bonds.
3. **Termination:** Radical coupling yields a 3D network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why driers are used:** Natural drying of linseed oil takes ~7 days. Adding **0.05–0.5% cobalt naphthenate** reduces this to **6–12 hours**, an acceleration of ~14×.

**Rate-Controlling Equation (Approximate):**

$$ \frac{d[\text{ROOH}]}{dt} = k_{\text{ind}} [\text{RH}] [\text{O}_2] $$

where $k_{\text{ind}}$ is the induction rate constant, strongly temperature-dependent via the Arrhenius relation.

---

### 2.4 Detailed Mechanism 3: Coalescence in Latex Films

**Stage-by-Stage Physical Model (Dillon–La Mer–Bradford Theory):**

- **Step 1:** Water evaporation brings latex particles (typically 100–500 nm) into contact.
- **Step 2:** Capillary pressure $P_c$ deforms particles.
- **Step 3:** Polymer chain interdiffusion across particle boundaries.
- **Step 4:** Autohesion produces a continuous film.

**Key Equation — Capillary Pressure Driving Coalescence:**

$$ P_c = \frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r} $$

where:
- $\gamma_{LV}$ = polymer–water interfacial tension (~25–40 mN/m)
- $\theta$ = contact angle
- $r$ = particle radius

> [!NOTE]
> **Minimum Film Formation Temperature (MFFT):** The lowest temperature at which latex particles can coalesce. For a coating to form a continuous film, the application temperature **must exceed MFFT**, typically by 5–10 °C.

---

### 2.5 Detailed Mechanism 4: Chemical Crosslinking (Curing)

**Two Reaction Classes:**

**(a) Condensation Curing (By-product Release)**
- Examples: Alkyd + melamine, silicone, phenolic.
- By-products: $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, methanol, formaldehyde.
- Equation: $\text{R–OH} + \text{HO–R'} \rightarrow \text{R–O–R'} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$

**(b) Addition Curing (No By-product)**
- Examples: Epoxy + amine, isocyanate + polyol (2K PU), acrylic + isocyanate.
- Equation: $\text{R–NCO} + \text{HO–R'} \rightarrow \text{R–NH–COO–R'}$ (urethane linkage)

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Fick's Diffusion Law | $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | — | Solvent evaporation kinetics |
| 2 | Arrhenius Cure Rate | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | $s^{-1}$ | Temperature dependence of cure |
| 3 | Capillary Pressure | $P_c = \frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r}$ | Pa | Latex coalescence driving force |
| 4 | WFT–DFT Relation | $\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \text{SBV}$ | µm | Film thickness calculation |
| 5 | DFT–CPVC Relation | $\text{Property} = f(\Lambda)$ | — | Pigment loading effects |
| 6 | Pigment Volume Concentration | $\text{PVC} = \frac{V_p}{V_p + V_b} \times 100$ | % | Formulation parameter |
| 7 | CPVC–ΔPVC Relation | $\Delta\text{PVC} = \text{PVC} - \text{CPVC}$ | % | Property transition predictor |
| 8 | Film Hardness (Persoz) | $H \propto 1/t$ | bounces | Cure completeness indicator |
| 9 | Glass Transition (Fox) | $\frac{1}{T_g} = \sum \frac{w_i}{T_{g,i}}$ | K | Latex binder design |
| 10 | Solvent Evaporation Rate | $R = k \cdot A \cdot (P_s - P_a)$ | g/s | Drying time estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:** The $\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \text{SBV}$ equation is a *mandatory* relationship tested in nearly every KTU exam on coatings. Memorize the exact form and the meaning of each term.

---

### 2.7 Engineering Utility in Industry

| Industry | Dominant Mechanism | Reason |
|----------|-------------------|--------|
| **Automotive OEM** | 2K Polyurethane curing | High gloss, scratch resistance, durability |
| **Architectural Paints** | Latex coalescence | Low VOC, water cleanup, fast dry |
| **Marine Coatings** | Oxidation / Epoxy-amine | Chemical & water resistance |
| **Wood Finishes** | Nitrocellulose evaporation | Easy repair, fast polishing |
| **Aerospace** | Epoxy + amine / PU | Extreme mechanical performance |
| **Can Coatings** | Epoxy + melamine (condensation) | FDA compliance, sterilization resistance |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Dry Film Thickness from Wet Film Thickness

**Problem Statement:** A coating has a wet film thickness of **200 µm** and solids by volume of **45%**. Compute the dry film thickness.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing relationship.**

When solvent evaporates, only the non-volatile solids remain to form the film. Hence:

$$ \text{Volume of dry film} = \text{Volume of solids} = \text{WFT} \times \text{Area} \times \text{SBV} $$

**Step 2 — Express thickness explicitly.**

Dividing both sides by area, the dry film thickness is:

$$ \text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \text{SBV} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$ \text{DFT} = 200 \, \mu\text{m} \times 0.45 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{DFT} = 90 \, \mu\text{m}} $$

**Step 4 — Interpretation for KTU Board Exam.**

> [!NOTE]
> For a 200 µm wet application, only 90 µm of dry coating remains. The remaining 110 µm of volatile solvent is lost to atmosphere—this is why **VOC control** is central to modern coatings engineering.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Capillary Pressure Driving Coalescence in Latex

**Problem Statement:** A latex dispersion has particle radius $r = 150 \, \text{nm}$ and polymer–water interfacial tension $\gamma_{LV} = 30 \, \text{mN/m}$. The contact angle $\theta = 0°$ (complete wetting). Compute the capillary pressure driving coalescence.

**Step 1 — State the Young–Laplace / capillary pressure equation.**

For a spherical particle deformed by capillary forces, the driving pressure is:

$$ P_c = \frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r} $$

**Step 2 — Convert all units to SI base.**

- $\gamma_{LV} = 30 \, \text{mN/m} = 30 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{N/m} = 0.030 \, \text{N/m}$
- $r = 150 \, \text{nm} = 150 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}$
- $\cos(0°) = 1$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the equation.**

$$ P_c = \frac{2 \times 0.030 \times 1}{150 \times 10^{-9}} $$

**Step 4 — Simplify the numerator and denominator.**

$$ P_c = \frac{0.060}{1.5 \times 10^{-7}} $$

$$ P_c = 4.0 \times 10^{5} \, \text{Pa} $$

$$ \boxed{P_c = 400 \, \text{kPa} \approx 4 \, \text{atm}} $$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**

> [!TIP]
> A capillary pressure of **400 kPa (~4 atmospheres)** is sufficient to deform 150 nm latex particles and drive polymer interdiffusion. This explains why **even at room temperature**, latex films can coalesce, provided the temperature is **above MFFT**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Glass Transition Temperature via the Fox Equation

**Problem Statement:** A latex binder is composed of 60% by weight poly(methyl methacrylate) ($\text{PMMA}$, $T_g = 105°\text{C}$) and 40% by weight poly(butyl acrylate) ($\text{PBA}$, $T_g = -54°\text{C}$). Estimate the $T_g$ of the copolymer.

**Step 1 — Convert all temperatures to Kelvin.**

$$ T_{g,1} = 105 + 273 = 378 \, \text{K} $$

$$ T_{g,2} = -54 + 273 = 219 \, \text{K} $$

**Step 2 — State the Fox equation.**

$$ \frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g,2}} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute weight fractions.**

$$ \frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{0.60}{378} + \frac{0.40}{219} $$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each term.**

$$ \frac{0.60}{378} = 1.587 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{K}^{-1} $$

$$ \frac{0.40}{219} = 1.826 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{K}^{-1} $$

**Step 5 — Sum the terms.**

$$ \frac{1}{T_g} = 3.413 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{K}^{-1} $$

**Step 6 — Take the reciprocal.**

$$ T_g = \frac{1}{3.413 \times 10^{-3}} = 293 \, \text{K} $$

**Step 7 — Convert back to °C.**

$$ T_g = 293 - 273 = 20°\text{C} $$

$$ \boxed{T_g \approx 20°\text{C}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A $T_g$ of 20 °C means the MFFT is approximately 20 °C. Application temperatures **below 20 °C** will yield *powdery, discontinuous films*. This is a **classic KTU 14-mark problem**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: How Mechanism Affects Final Properties

| Property | Evaporation | Oxidation | Coalescence | Chemical Cure |
|----------|-------------|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| **Re-dissolvability** | Yes | No | No | No |
| **Gloss** | High | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Chemical Resistance** | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Hardness** | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Flexibility** | Good | Good | Excellent | Moderate |
| **Yellowing** | None | Yes (oils) | None | Variable |
| **Reverse-ability** | Yes | No | No | No |

---

### 3.5 KTU Analytical Question Framework: Identifying the Dominant Mechanism

To score full marks on KTU 14-mark essay questions, structure your answer using this analytical template:

1. **Identify the binder class** (e.g., alkyd, acrylic latex, epoxy).
2. **State the mechanism** explicitly.
3. **Justify with chemical/physical reasoning** (e.g., presence of C=C unsaturation → oxidation).
4. **Predict final film properties** using the analytical matrix above.
5. **List factors** that accelerate or retard the mechanism (e.g., temperature, driers, humidity).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Universal Film Formation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid Coating Applied on Substrate] --> B[Stage I: Wet Stage]
    B --> C[Stage II: Set-to-Touch - Surface Solvent Loss]
    C --> D[Stage III: Through-Dry - Bulk Transformation]
    D --> E[Stage IV: Full Cure - Final Crosslink Network]
    E --> F[Continuous Functional Film]

    C --> G{Mechanism Type}
    G -->|Physical| H[Solvent Evaporation - Lacquers]
    G -->|Physico-Chemical| I[Latex Coalescence]
    G -->|Chemical Air-Dry| J[Oxidative Polymerization]
    G -->|Chemical 2K Cure| K[Crosslinking - Epoxy PU]

    H --> F
    I --> F
    J --> F
    K --> F
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Factor-Property Influence Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ExternalFactors
        EF1[Temperature]
        EF2[Relative Humidity]
        EF3[Air Circulation]
        EF4[Substrate Chemistry]
    end

    subgraph InternalFactors
        IF1[Binder Type]
        IF2[Pigment Volume Concentration]
        IF3[Solvent Boiling Point]
        IF4[Additives - Driers, Coalescents]
    end

    subgraph FilmProperties
        FP1[Hardness]
        FP2[Gloss]
        FP3[Adhesion]
        FP4[Chemical Resistance]
        FP5[Flexibility]
        FP6[Drying Time]
    end

    EF1 --> FP1
    EF1 --> FP6
    EF2 --> FP1
    EF2 --> FP2
    EF3 --> FP6
    EF4 --> FP3

    IF1 --> FP1
    IF1 --> FP4
    IF1 --> FP5
    IF2 --> FP2
    IF2 --> FP4
    IF3 --> FP6
    IF4 --> FP1
    IF4 --> FP6
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Latex Coalescence Micro-Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Stage A: Discrete Latex Particles in Water] --> L2[Stage B: Water Evaporation Particles Approach]
    L2 --> L3[Stage C: Capillary Pressure Deforms Particles]
    L3 --> L4[Stage D: Polymer Chain Interdiffusion]
    L4 --> L5[Stage E: Autohesion Continuous Film]
    L5 --> L6[Stage F: Final Vitrification Above MFFT]
```

---

### 4.4 Schematic Process Flow — Oxidative Drying of an Alkyd

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O1[Liquid Alkyd with Unsaturated Oils] --> O2[Oxygen Diffusion into Film]
    O2 --> O3[Hydroperoxide Formation RH to ROOH]
    O3 --> O4[Radical Formation via Metal Drier Catalyst]
    O4 --> O5[Crosslinking of Unsaturated Sites]
    O5 --> O6[Three-Dimensional Network Solid Film]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between "drying" and "curing" of a coating film with one example each.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**
- **Drying** is the *physical* loss of volatile components (solvents or water) from a coating, resulting in a solid film that is **reversible** in the original solvent.
  - *Example:* Nitrocellulose lacquer drying by solvent evaporation.
- **Curing** is the *chemical* transformation of the binder via crosslinking reactions (oxidation, polyaddition, polycondensation), producing a **thermoset film** that is irreversible.
  - *Example:* Two-pack polyurethane curing via isocyanate–polyol reaction.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners award **1 mark** each for the correct definition, **1 mark** for the example, and **1 mark** for reversibility distinction. (3 marks total)

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is Minimum Film Formation Temperature (MFFT)? Why is it critical for latex coatings?**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**
- MFFT is the **lowest temperature at which latex particles can coalesce** to form a continuous, crack-free film.
- It is critical because below MFFT, polymer chains lack sufficient mobility for interdiffusion, leading to **powdery, discontinuous, mechanically weak films** with poor barrier properties.
- MFFT must be **5–10 °C below** the expected application temperature for reliable performance.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of **film formation in water-borne latex coatings** with a neat stage-wise diagram. Discuss the role of **MFFT, coalescing agents, and particle size**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **internal and external factors** that affect the drying and final properties of a coating film. How does Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC) influence film properties? (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Introduction to Latex Coalescence (1 mark)**
- Latex coatings are aqueous dispersions of polymer particles (100–500 nm diameter) stabilized by surfactants.
- Film formation is a *physical* mechanism: water evaporates, particles approach, deform, and fuse.

**Step 2: Stage-wise description (4 marks)**

| Stage | Process | Outcome |
|-------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Water evaporation | Volume reduction; particle concentration rises |
| 2 | Particles in close contact | Capillary forces generated at menisci |
| 3 | Capillary pressure $P_c = \frac{2\gamma\cos\theta}{r}$ | Particles deform from spheres to polyhedra |
| 4 | Polymer interdiffusion | Chain entanglement across particle boundaries |
| 5 | Autohesion | Continuous, optically clear film forms |

**Step 3: Role of MFFT, coalescing agents, particle size (2 marks)**
- **MFFT:** The temperature at which the polymer segments have sufficient mobility for interdiffusion. Below MFFT, films are discontinuous.
- **Coalescing agents:** High-boiling solvents (e.g., glycol ethers, Texanol) that temporarily plasticize the polymer, lowering the *effective* MFFT by 10–20 °C. They evaporate slowly after film formation.
- **Particle size:** Smaller particles (50–100 nm) → higher capillary pressure → faster coalescence → smoother films. Larger particles (>300 nm) require more deformation energy and may yield rougher films.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Internal Factors (3 marks):**
1. **Binder chemistry:** Alkyd → air-dry; epoxy → chemical cure; latex → coalescence.
2. **Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC):**
   - Below CPVC: binder fills all interstices → glossy, continuous film.
   - Above CPVC: air voids appear → matte, porous, permeable film.
3. **Solvent system:** Boiling point, evaporation rate, solvency power.
4. **Additives:** Driers (Co/Mn salts), coalescents, anti-skinning agents.

**External Factors (3 marks):**
1. **Temperature:** Higher → faster solvent loss and faster cure (Arrhenius).
2. **Relative humidity:** High humidity slows water evaporation from latex systems.
3. **Air movement:** Enhances solvent removal; can cause skinning or blistering.
4. **Substrate nature:** Porosity, surface energy, roughness.

**PVC vs Property Diagram (1 mark):**

| Property | Below CPVC | At CPVC | Above CPVC |
|----------|-----------|---------|------------|
| Gloss | High | Sudden drop | Low/Matte |
| Permeability | Low | Transition | High |
| Stain resistance | Good | Reduced | Poor |
| Hiding power | Rises | Maximum | Plateaus |

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a)** Discuss the **mechanism of oxidative drying** of an alkyd resin-based coating. How do **driers** accelerate this process? (7 marks)

**(b)** With the help of Fick's Law, derive the relationship between **solvent evaporation rate and film thickness**. List the factors affecting solvent retention in a coating film. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Chemistry of oxidative drying (2 marks)**
- Alkyd resins contain **unsaturated fatty acid chains** (linoleic, linolenic).
- Atmospheric $\text{O}_2$ attacks the **methylene-interrupted diene** unit, forming a hydroperoxide:

$$ \text{RH} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{ROOH} $$

**Step 2: Catalysis by metallic driers (2 marks)**
- **Cobalt naphthenate** (primary drier) decomposes hydroperoxides into radicals:
$$ \text{ROOH} \xrightarrow{\text{Co}^{2+}} \text{RO}^{\cdot} + {}^{\cdot}\text{OH} $$
- **Lead / Zirconium / Calcium** act as *secondary (auxiliary)* driers that improve through-dry.

**Step 3: Crosslinking reactions (2 marks)**
- Radical coupling at allylic positions forms **C–C, C–O–C, and peroxide bridges**.
- Molecular weight rises exponentially → gel point reached → solid film.

**Step 4: Effect on film properties (1 mark)**
- Increases hardness, solvent resistance, and chemical resistance.
- Drawback: yellowing (especially with aromatic-rich alkyds) and slow full-cure.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Fick's First Law statement (1 mark)**

$$ J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} $$

The flux $J$ of solvent through the film is proportional to the concentration gradient.

**Step 2: Combine with mass balance for film (2 marks)**

$$ \frac{dm}{dt} = -A \cdot D \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} $$

For a film of thickness $L$ with linear concentration profile from $C_s$ (surface) to $C_b$ (bulk):

$$ \frac{dm}{dt} = -A \cdot D \cdot \frac{(C_s - C_b)}{L} $$

**Step 3: Solvent retention equation (2 marks)**

The amount of solvent remaining in the film at time $t$ follows:

$$ m(t) = m_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{D \cdot A}{L^2} \cdot t\right) $$

Thus, **thicker films retain solvent longer** because the diffusion path $L$ is longer.

**Step 4: Factors affecting solvent retention (2 marks)**
1. **Film thickness $L$** — thicker films retain solvent exponentially longer.
2. **Diffusion coefficient $D$** — depends on binder Tg, solvent size, temperature.
3. **Temperature** — higher $T$ → higher $D$ → faster drying.
4. **Humidity and airflow** — affect surface concentration gradient.
5. **Solvent–binder interaction** — strongly bound solvents (H-bonding) are retained longer.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in KTU 2024 Scheme PBPOT404 Module 3**
> 1. **Confusing "drying" with "curing"** — costs 1–2 marks. Always state whether the process is *physical* or *chemical*.
> 2. **Writing "MFFT" without expanding it** once — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for undefined abbreviations.
> 3. **Missing the MFFT–Tg relationship** — many students state MFFT but forget to link it to polymer $T_g$ via the Fox equation.
> 4. **No diagram in 14-mark answers** — KTU 2024 scheme mandates at least one schematic; absence costs up to 2 marks.
> 5. **Numerical errors in $\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \text{SBV}$** — confusing % with decimal (e.g., 45% as 45 instead of 0.45). Always convert.
> 6. **Skipping reversibility distinction** — for evaporation mechanism, students often forget to mention the film is re-dissolvable.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PBPOT404 Module 3**

- ✅ **Four mechanisms:** Evaporation, Oxidation, Coalescence, Chemical Curing.
- ✅ **Two drying categories:** *Physical* (reversible) vs *Chemical* (irreversible).
- ✅ **Drying stages:** Wet → Set-to-touch → Through-dry → Full cure.
- ✅ **MFFT** = lowest temperature for latex film formation; must be 5–10 °C below application temperature.
- ✅ **Capillary pressure equation** $P_c = \frac{2\gamma\cos\theta}{r}$ drives coalescence.
- ✅ **Fox equation** for $T_g$ of copolymers: $\frac{1}{T_g} = \sum \frac{w_i}{T_{g,i}}$.
- ✅ **DFT = WFT × SBV** — must convert percentage to decimal.
- ✅ **PVC vs CPVC** — at CPVC, properties like gloss and permeability change abruptly.
- ✅ **Driers:** Co/Mn (primary), Pb/Zr/Ca (auxiliary); reduce oxidative drying time from days to hours.
- ✅ **Fick's Law** governs solvent evaporation; thicker films retain solvent exponentially longer.
- ✅ **External factors:** Temperature, humidity, airflow, substrate.
- ✅ **Internal factors:** Binder type, PVC, solvent system, additives.
- ✅ **Condensation curing** releases by-products ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$, MeOH); **addition curing** does not.
- ✅ **Latex coalescence sequence:** Water loss → particle contact → capillary deformation → interdiffusion → autohesion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Film Thickness, Film Density, Internal Stresses, and Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT404 - PAINTS & SURFACE COATINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Film Thickness, Film Density, Internal Stresses, and Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Film Thickness (Wet Film Thickness vs. Dry Film Thickness)

### Formal Definition
Film thickness in surface coatings refers to the perpendicular distance between the substrate surface and the outer surface of the applied coating film. It is broadly classified into two categories:

- **Wet Film Thickness (WFT):** The thickness of the coating measured immediately after application, while the coating is still in its liquid state and contains all volatile components (solvents, water).
- **Dry Film Thickness (DFT):** The thickness of the coating measured after the complete evaporation of volatile components and full curing of the film, leaving only the non-volatile solid constituents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The relationship between WFT and DFT is fundamental to coating formulation, cost estimation, and quality control. Always remember that **DFT < WFT** because the volatile fraction is lost during drying.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of making **chai (tea) on a stove**:
- The **WFT** is like the *initial quantity of milk* you pour into the pan — it has its full volume because nothing has evaporated.
- The **DFT** is like the *reduced quantity of milk* that remains after vigorous boiling — water has evaporated, and the milk is now thicker and reduced in volume.
- Similarly, in a coating, solvents evaporate during drying, leaving behind a thinner but denser dry film.

The conversion between WFT and DFT uses the **Volume Solids (VS)** of the coating:

$$\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \text{Volume Solids}$$

The **Volume Solids** is the percentage of the total liquid coating volume that remains as solid film after drying.

---

## 1.2 Film Density

### Formal Definition
Film density is defined as the **mass per unit volume** of the dry coating film, typically expressed in g/cm³ or kg/m³. It represents the packing efficiency of the binder, pigment, and additives within the dried film matrix.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Value:** Most commercial dry coating films have a density in the range of **1.2 to 2.0 g/cm³**, depending on the pigment loading and binder type.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **backpack filled with books**:
- A backpack with tightly packed books (no gaps) is *denser*.
- A backpack with loosely placed books and air gaps is *less dense*.
- In a coating film, when pigment particles pack tightly within the binder matrix, density increases. When there is excess binder creating voids, density decreases.

### Types of Film Density
1. **Theoretical Film Density (ρ_theoretical):** The maximum density calculated from the weighted average of individual component densities (pigment, binder, additives), assuming zero voids.
2. **Actual Film Density (ρ_actual):** The real measured density, which is always less than theoretical density due to the presence of micro-voids, trapped air, and surface irregularities.

---

## 1.3 Internal Stresses in Films

### Formal Definition
Internal stresses are **residual mechanical stresses** that develop within a coating film during and after the drying/curing process. These stresses exist without any external load and arise from the natural physical and chemical changes occurring within the film.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Primary Types of Internal Stress:**
> - **Tensile Stress (Contractive):** The film wants to shrink but is restrained by adhesion to the substrate. This creates pulling forces.
> - **Compressive Stress (Expansive):** The film wants to expand but is confined, creating pushing forces.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of **drying a wet shirt on a clothesline**:
- As water evaporates, the fabric naturally wants to shrink.
- But the clothespins pin it in place, restraining the shrinkage.
- This restraint creates internal **tensile stress** within the fabric.
- If the stress exceeds the fabric's strength, it tears (analogous to **cracking** in coatings).
- Conversely, if you stretch a rubber band and glue it to a surface, when released, it tries to contract and create compressive force on the surface (analogous to **peeling or delamination** in compressive stress).

### Sources of Internal Stresses
| Source | Type of Stress | Mechanism |
|--------|---------------|-----------|
| Solvent evaporation | Tensile | Volume reduction during drying |
| Thermal coefficient mismatch | Tensile/Compressive | Differential expansion/contraction |
| Crosslinking reactions | Tensile | Chemical shrinkage during cure |
| Pigment-binder interaction | Variable | Differential shrinkage between phases |
| Moisture absorption | Compressive | Swelling of hydrophilic components |

---

## 1.4 Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC)

### Formal Definition
**Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC)** is the ratio of the volume of pigment (and other non-film-forming extender particles) to the total volume of the non-volatile constituents of the dry coating film, expressed as a percentage.

$$\text{PVC (\%)} = \frac{V_{\text{pigment}}}{V_{\text{pigment}} + V_{\text{binder}}} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** PVC is a property of the **formulation** (composition), not the film itself. It depends purely on the volume ratio of pigment to binder in the dry film.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine building a **wall with bricks and cement**:
- **Bricks** represent the **pigment particles** (rigid fillers).
- **Cement/mortar** represents the **binder** (flexible matrix that holds everything together).
- If you use too many bricks and very little cement (high PVC), the wall becomes porous, weak, and brittle.
- If you use too much cement and very few bricks (low PVC), the wall is smooth but expensive and lacks hiding power.
- The **optimal ratio** gives a strong, economical, and functional wall.

### Critical Pigment Volume Concentration (CPVC)

The **CPVC** is the specific PVC value at which there is **just enough binder** to completely fill the interstices (voids) between pigment particles. Above CPVC, there is insufficient binder to wet all pigment surfaces and fill voids, leading to a porous, defective film.

$$\text{CPVC} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{\text{OA} \times \rho_{\text{binder}}}{93.5}}$$

where OA is Oil Absorption of pigment and ρ_binder is binder density.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPVC vs. PVC — The Golden Rule:**
> - For a **cohesive, continuous film:** PVC < CPVC (binder-rich regime)
> - For a **porous, defective film:** PVC > CPVC (pigment-rich regime)
> - **Gloss, flexibility, and barrier properties** decrease as PVC approaches CPVC.
> - **Opacity, porosity, and stain resistance** increase as PVC approaches CPVC.

### Real-World Applications
- **Flat wall paints (matte finishes):** Formulated with PVC near CPVC to maximize hiding and minimize gloss.
- **Gloss enamels:** Formulated with PVC well below CPVC to ensure a smooth, continuous, glossy film.
- **Primers:** Often formulated at or near CPVC to provide a porous surface for topcoat adhesion.
- **Anticorrosive coatings:** Must be formulated well below CPVC to ensure barrier protection.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wet Film Thickness to Dry Film Thickness — Theoretical Analysis

### Logic Flow (Step-by-Step)
1. **A liquid coating** is applied to a substrate with a known wet film thickness (WFT).
2. The liquid coating contains **volatile components** (solvents, water) and **non-volatile components** (binder, pigment, additives).
3. The **Volume Solids (VS)** is the fraction of the liquid coating that remains as solid film.
4. During drying, only the volatile fraction is lost; the non-volatile fraction remains.
5. The remaining solid film has a thickness equal to DFT.

### Mathematical Derivation Logic
- Let $V_L$ = volume of liquid coating applied
- Let $V_S$ = volume of solids (non-volatile) in that liquid
- Volume Solids: $VS = V_S / V_L$
- If WFT is the wet film thickness over a unit area, then $V_L = WFT \times 1 = WFT$
- The dry film volume per unit area = $V_S = WFT \times VS$
- Therefore, $DFT = WFT \times VS$

---

## 2.2 Film Density — Theoretical Analysis

### The Theoretical Density Formula
For a multi-component coating film, the theoretical density is calculated as the mass-weighted average of individual component densities:

$$\rho_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{\sum (m_i)}{\sum (V_i)} = \frac{\sum (V_i \times \rho_i)}{\sum (V_i)}$$

where $V_i$ is the volume of component $i$ and $\rho_i$ is its density.

### Porosity and Actual Density
The actual density is always less than theoretical due to micro-voids:

$$\text{Porosity} = \frac{\rho_{\text{theoretical}} - \rho_{\text{actual}}}{\rho_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> When PVC > CPVC, the film is highly porous (voids in the film), and the actual density drops significantly. This is a key reason why over-pigmented films fail in service.

---

## 2.3 Internal Stresses — Mathematical Framework

### Shrinkage Stress (Primary Tensile Stress Source)
When a coating dries, the loss of solvent causes volumetric shrinkage. If the film is restrained by the substrate, tensile stress develops:

$$\sigma_{\text{shrinkage}} = \frac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f} \times \frac{\Delta V}{V}$$

where:
- $E_f$ = Young's modulus of the dry film
- $\nu_f$ = Poisson's ratio of the film
- $\Delta V / V$ = volumetric shrinkage strain

### Thermal Stress
Differential thermal expansion between coating and substrate generates stress:

$$\sigma_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f} \times (\alpha_f - \alpha_s) \times \Delta T$$

where:
- $\alpha_f$ = thermal expansion coefficient of film
- $\alpha_s$ = thermal expansion coefficient of substrate
- $\Delta T$ = temperature change

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** If internal stress exceeds the **cohesive strength** of the film, it **cracks**. If it exceeds the **adhesive strength** at the interface, it **delaminates or peels**.

---

## 2.4 PVC and CPVC — Complete Theoretical Framework

### Oil Absorption (OA) and Its Role
**Oil Absorption** is the mass of linseed oil (in grams) required to wet 100 g of pigment, forming a coherent paste. It is a direct measure of the **void volume** between pigment particles when closely packed.

$$\text{CPVC (\%)} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{\text{OA} \times \rho_{\text{binder}}}{93.5}} \times 100$$

### Properties vs. PVC/CPVC Ratio

| Property | Below CPVC (PVC < CPVC) | Above CPVC (PVC > CPVC) |
|----------|-------------------------|-------------------------|
| Gloss | High | Low/Matte |
| Flexibility | High | Low/Brittle |
| Tensile Strength | Moderate | Low (near CPVC) |
| Permeability | Low | High (porous) |
| Hiding Power | Moderate | Very High |
| Stain Resistance | High | Low |
| Adhesion | High | Variable |
| Corrosion Protection | Excellent | Poor |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Properties undergo a **discontinuity or sharp transition** at CPVC. This is a key exam point — many students incorrectly assume a linear relationship.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \text{VS}$ | Wet to dry film thickness | µm or mils |
| 2 | $\text{WFT} = \text{DFT} / \text{VS}$ | Dry to wet film thickness | µm or mils |
| 3 | $\text{PVC} = \frac{V_p}{V_p + V_b} \times 100$ | Pigment volume concentration | % |
| 4 | $\text{CPVC} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{\text{OA} \times \rho_b}{93.5}} \times 100$ | Critical PVC | % |
| 5 | $\rho_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{\sum V_i \rho_i}{\sum V_i}$ | Theoretical film density | g/cm³ |
| 6 | $\text{Porosity} = \frac{\rho_t - \rho_a}{\rho_t} \times 100$ | Film porosity | % |
| 7 | $\sigma = \frac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f} \times \frac{\Delta V}{V}$ | Shrinkage stress | Pa or MPa |
| 8 | $\sigma_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f} \times (\alpha_f - \alpha_s) \times \Delta T$ | Thermal stress | Pa or MPa |
| 9 | $\Lambda = \frac{V_{\text{volatile}}}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Volatiles content | % |
| 10 | $VS = 1 - \Lambda$ | Volume solids | fraction |
| 11 | $\Delta V/V = 1 - VS$ | Volumetric shrinkage | fraction |
| 12 | $V_{\text{pigment}} = \frac{m_p}{\rho_p}$ | Pigment volume from mass | cm³ |
| 13 | $V_{\text{binder}} = \frac{m_b}{\rho_b}$ | Binder volume from mass | cm³ |

### Symbols Glossary
- $V_p$ = Volume of pigment
- $V_b$ = Volume of binder (non-volatile)
- OA = Oil Absorption (g oil / 100 g pigment)
- $\rho_b$ = Density of binder
- $\rho_p$ = Density of pigment
- $E_f$ = Young's modulus of film
- $\nu_f$ = Poisson's ratio of film
- $\alpha_f, \alpha_s$ = Thermal expansion coefficients of film and substrate
- VS = Volume Solids (fraction)
- $\Lambda$ = Volatiles content (fraction)

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: WFT to DFT Conversion

### Problem Setup
A coating has **Volume Solids = 45%**. If applied at a WFT of **200 µm**, what is the DFT?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the governing equation**

$$\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \text{VS}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values**

$$\text{DFT} = 200 \, \mu\text{m} \times 0.45$$

**Step 3: Compute the result**

$$\text{DFT} = 90 \, \mu\text{m}$$

**Step 4: Physical interpretation**

> For every 200 µm of wet film, only 90 µm remains as dry film. The remaining 110 µm is lost as evaporated solvents. This is why **coatings are applied thicker than the final required DFT**.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: PVC Calculation

### Problem Setup
A coating formulation contains:
- **150 g** of Titanium Dioxide (TiO₂) pigment with density $\rho_p = 4.2$ g/cm³
- **100 g** of long oil alkyd binder with density $\rho_b = 1.05$ g/cm³
- Negligible additive volumes

Calculate the PVC.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate pigment volume**

$$V_p = \frac{m_p}{\rho_p} = \frac{150 \, \text{g}}{4.2 \, \text{g/cm}^3}$$

$$V_p = 35.71 \, \text{cm}^3$$

**Step 2: Calculate binder volume**

$$V_b = \frac{m_b}{\rho_b} = \frac{100 \, \text{g}}{1.05 \, \text{g/cm}^3}$$

$$V_b = 95.24 \, \text{cm}^3$$

**Step 3: Apply the PVC formula**

$$\text{PVC} = \frac{V_p}{V_p + V_b} \times 100$$

$$\text{PVC} = \frac{35.71}{35.71 + 95.24} \times 100$$

$$\text{PVC} = \frac{35.71}{130.95} \times 100$$

$$\text{PVC} = 27.27\%$$

**Step 4: Verification and interpretation**

The PVC of ~27% indicates a **moderately pigmented coating**. For a gloss enamel, this is a typical value. For a flat wall paint, PVC would typically be 40-60%.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: CPVC Using Oil Absorption

### Problem Setup
A titanium dioxide pigment has an **Oil Absorption (OA) = 22 g oil / 100 g pigment**. The binder density is $\rho_b = 1.0$ g/cm³. Calculate the CPVC.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: State the CPVC equation**

$$\text{CPVC} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{\text{OA} \times \rho_b}{93.5}} \times 100$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values**

$$\text{CPVC} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{22 \times 1.0}{93.5}} \times 100$$

**Step 3: Compute the denominator term**

$$\frac{22 \times 1.0}{93.5} = 0.2353$$

**Step 4: Add 1 to get the full denominator**

$$1 + 0.2353 = 1.2353$$

**Step 5: Compute the CPVC**

$$\text{CPVC} = \frac{1}{1.2353} \times 100$$

$$\text{CPVC} = 80.95\%$$

**Step 6: Physical interpretation**

> The CPVC of TiO₂ is approximately **81%**. This means that at a PVC of 81%, there is exactly enough binder to fill all voids between pigment particles. Below this PVC, the film is continuous; above, it is porous.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation: Film Density of a Multi-Component System

### Problem Setup
A dry film consists of:
- 30 cm³ of pigment with density 4.2 g/cm³
- 60 cm³ of binder with density 1.05 g/cm³
- 5 cm³ of additive with density 1.2 g/cm³
- 5 cm³ of micro-voids (actual porosity)

Calculate the **theoretical** and **actual** density, and the **porosity**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate the mass of each component**

$$m_p = V_p \times \rho_p = 30 \times 4.2 = 126 \, \text{g}$$

$$m_b = V_b \times \rho_b = 60 \times 1.05 = 63 \, \text{g}$$

$$m_a = V_a \times \rho_a = 5 \times 1.2 = 6 \, \text{g}$$

**Step 2: Calculate total mass and theoretical volume**

$$m_{\text{total}} = 126 + 63 + 6 = 195 \, \text{g}$$

$$V_{\text{theoretical}} = 30 + 60 + 5 = 95 \, \text{cm}^3$$

**Step 3: Calculate theoretical density**

$$\rho_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{195}{95} = 2.053 \, \text{g/cm}^3$$

**Step 4: Calculate actual volume and density**

$$V_{\text{actual}} = 95 + 5 = 100 \, \text{cm}^3$$

$$\rho_{\text{actual}} = \frac{195}{100} = 1.95 \, \text{g/cm}^3$$

**Step 5: Calculate porosity**

$$\text{Porosity} = \frac{2.053 - 1.95}{2.053} \times 100 = 5.02\%$$

---

## 3.5 Detailed Derivation: Internal Stress in a Drying Film

### Problem Setup
An acrylic coating has:
- Young's modulus $E_f = 3.5$ GPa
- Poisson's ratio $\nu_f = 0.35$
- Volume Solids VS = 0.40

Calculate the shrinkage stress.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Determine volumetric shrinkage**

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = 1 - VS = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60$$

**Step 2: State the shrinkage stress equation**

$$\sigma = \frac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f} \times \frac{\Delta V}{V}$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**

$$\sigma = \frac{3.5 \times 10^9}{1 - 0.35} \times 0.60$$

**Step 4: Compute denominator**

$$1 - 0.35 = 0.65$$

**Step 5: Compute the final stress**

$$\sigma = \frac{3.5 \times 10^9}{0.65} \times 0.60$$

$$\sigma = 5.385 \times 10^9 \times 0.60$$

$$\sigma = 3.23 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa} = 3.23 \, \text{GPa}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** The above represents the **unrestrained** theoretical shrinkage stress. In practice, the substrate restrains most of this, and the **actual measured** internal stress is much lower (typically 1-10 MPa). The example illustrates the *upper bound* of stress generation.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Effect of PVC/CPVC on Coating Properties

| Property | PVC = 0% (Pure Binder) | PVC < CPVC | PVC ≈ CPVC | PVC > CPVC |
|----------|----------------------|------------|------------|------------|
| Gloss (60°) | 90+ | 60-90 | 5-15 | <5 |
| Flexibility | Excellent | Good | Poor | Brittle |
| Hiding Power | Low | Moderate | High | Very High |
| Permeability | Very Low | Low | Moderate | Very High |
| Tensile Strength | Moderate | High | Variable | Low |
| Stain Resistance | Excellent | Good | Poor | Very Poor |
| Scrub Resistance | Moderate | High | Low | Very Low |
| Cost | Very High | High | Moderate | Low |
| Typical Application | Varnish | Gloss Enamel | Semi-gloss | Flat/Matte |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Film Formation and Thickness Transition Diagram

The following block diagram illustrates the transition from wet to dry film and the associated volume changes:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["LIQUID COATING APPLIED"] --> node2["WFT = 200 micrometres"]
    node2 --> node3["Volume Solids = 45 percent"]
    node3 --> node4["Solvent Evaporation Begins"]
    node4 --> node5["Film Shrinks Vertically"]
    node5 --> node6["DFT = 90 micrometres"]
    node6 --> node7["Crosslinking and Cure"]
    node7 --> node8["Final Dry Film"]
    
    node8 --> node9["Solid Binder Matrix"]
    node8 --> node10["Embedded Pigment Particles"]
    node8 --> node11["Micro Voids if PVC above CPVC"]
```

## 4.2 Pigment Volume Concentration Conceptual Architecture

The following block diagram shows the relationship between PVC, CPVC, and film structure:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["DRY FILM COMPONENTS"] --> nodeB["Pigment Volume Vp"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Binder Volume Vb"]
    
    nodeB --> nodeD["PVC = Vp divided by Vp plus Vb times 100"]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    
    nodeD --> nodeE{"PVC compared to CPVC"}
    
    nodeE -->|"PVC less than CPVC"| nodeF["CONTINUOUS FILM"]
    nodeE -->|"PVC equals CPVC"| nodeG["TRANSITION POINT"]
    nodeE -->|"PVC greater than CPVC"| nodeH["POROUS FILM"]
    
    nodeF --> nodeI["High Gloss and Flexible"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["Good Barrier Properties"]
    
    nodeG --> nodeK["Critical Transition Zone"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Sudden Property Changes"]
    
    nodeH --> nodeM["Matte Finish"]
    nodeH --> nodeN["High Hiding Power"]
    nodeH --> nodeO["Poor Barrier Protection"]
```

## 4.3 Internal Stress Generation Flow

The following diagram illustrates the origins and consequences of internal stresses:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1["COATING APPLIED ON SUBSTRATE"] --> nodeS2["Drying Process Initiated"]
    
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3["Solvent Loss"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS4["Crosslinking Shrinkage"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS5["Thermal Cycling"]
    
    nodeS3 --> nodeS6["Volumetric Contraction"]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS6
    nodeS5 --> nodeS7["Differential Expansion"]
    
    nodeS6 --> nodeS8{"Stress Type"}
    nodeS7 --> nodeS8
    
    nodeS8 -->|"Film restrained by substrate"| nodeS9["TENSILE STRESS"]
    nodeS8 -->|"Film pushes against substrate"| nodeS10["COMPRESSIVE STRESS"]
    
    nodeS9 --> nodeS11{"Stress versus Strength"}
    
    nodeS11 -->|"Stress exceeds Cohesive Strength"| nodeS12["CRACKING"]
    nodeS11 -->|"Stress exceeds Adhesive Strength"| nodeS13["DELAMINATION"]
    nodeS11 -->|"Stress below both strengths"| nodeS14["STABLE FILM"]
```

## 4.4 CPVC Calculation Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeC1["MEASURE OIL ABSORPTION OF PIGMENT"] --> nodeC2["Determine Binder Density"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC3["Apply CPVC Formula"]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC4["CPVC = 1 divided by 1 plus OA times rho_b divided by 93.5 times 100"]
    nodeC4 --> nodeC5["Compare with Formulation PVC"]
    nodeC5 --> nodeC6{"PVC relative to CPVC"}
    nodeC6 -->|"Below"| nodeC7["FORMULATION SAFE: Continuous Film"]
    nodeC6 -->|"Above"| nodeC8["REJECT FORMULATION: Porous Film Predicted"]
    nodeC6 -->|"At"| nodeC9["CRITICAL: Adjust Formulation Margin"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC) and Critical Pigment Volume Concentration (CPVC). How are they different?**

**Model Answer:**

**Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC):** It is defined as the ratio of the volume of pigment to the total volume of non-volatile components (pigment + binder) in a dry coating film, expressed as a percentage.

$$\text{PVC} = \frac{V_{\text{pigment}}}{V_{\text{pigment}} + V_{\text{binder}}} \times 100$$

**Critical Pigment Volume Concentration (CPVC):** It is the specific PVC value at which there is exactly sufficient binder to completely fill the interstices (voids) between closely packed pigment particles. At this concentration, the film transitions from a continuous, binder-rich matrix to a porous, pigment-rich structure.

**Key Differences:**
| Aspect | PVC | CPVC |
|--------|-----|------|
| Nature | Formulation variable | Intrinsic pigment property |
| Typical Range | 5-60% | 30-85% (pigment dependent) |
| Determines | Film composition | Film structure transition point |
| Calculated from | Volumes of pigment and binder | Oil absorption and binder density |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the difference between Wet Film Thickness (WFT) and Dry Film Thickness (DFT). Write the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

**Wet Film Thickness (WFT):** It is the thickness of the coating film measured immediately after application, in its liquid state, including all volatile components (solvents, water).

**Dry Film Thickness (DFT):** It is the thickness of the coating film after complete evaporation of volatiles and full curing, containing only the non-volatile solid constituents.

**Relationship:**

$$\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \text{Volume Solids (VS)}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\text{WFT} = \frac{\text{DFT}}{\text{VS}}$$

**Example:** If a coating with 50% volume solids is applied at 200 µm WFT, the resulting DFT will be 100 µm.

> **Key Note:** DFT is always less than WFT because volatile components are lost during drying. This relationship is critical for coating cost estimation and quality control.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define and explain the concept of Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC) and Critical Pigment Volume Concentration (CPVC). With a neat diagram, show how coating properties (gloss, flexibility, permeability, hiding power) change as PVC increases from 0% to beyond CPVC.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A coating formulation contains 200 g of zinc oxide (density 5.6 g/cm³), 80 g of red iron oxide (density 5.0 g/cm³), and 150 g of alkyd binder (density 1.08 g/cm³). Calculate the PVC of the formulation. If the oil absorption of the mixed pigment system is 25 g/100g, calculate the CPVC and comment on whether the formulation will produce a continuous film.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Definition of PVC:** As defined in Question 1 of Part A, PVC is the volume fraction of pigment in the total non-volatile content of the dry film.

**Definition of CPVC:** CPVC is the PVC value at which binder is just sufficient to fill all voids between pigment particles.

**Property Trends with Increasing PVC (Diagram):**

```
GLOSS                    FLEXIBILITY
  |  High                  |  Excellent
  |\                        |\
  | \                       | \
  |  \_________CPVC         |  \________CPVC
  |            \            |            \
  |             \           |             \
  |              \Matte     |              \Brittle
  |________________ PVC     |_________________ PVC

PERMEABILITY             HIDING POWER
  |  Low                   |  Low
  |  \                     |   \
  |   \                    |    \
  |____\_______CPVC        |     \_______CPVC
  |     \                  |      \____
  |      \High             |           \Very High
  |_______\______ PVC      |________________ PVC
```

**Explanation of Trends:**
- **Gloss** decreases as PVC increases because the rough pigment surfaces scatter light. It drops sharply near CPVC.
- **Flexibility** decreases because the binder matrix becomes discontinuous above CPVC, leading to brittleness.
- **Permeability** increases above CPVC due to film porosity.
- **Hiding power** increases as more pigment particles block light, with a sharp rise near CPVC.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1: Calculate volume of each pigment**

$$V_{\text{ZnO}} = \frac{200}{5.6} = 35.71 \, \text{cm}^3$$

$$V_{\text{red iron oxide}} = \frac{80}{5.0} = 16.00 \, \text{cm}^3$$

$$V_{\text{total pigment}} = 35.71 + 16.00 = 51.71 \, \text{cm}^3$$

**Step 2: Calculate volume of binder**

$$V_b = \frac{150}{1.08} = 138.89 \, \text{cm}^3$$

**Step 3: Calculate PVC**

$$\text{PVC} = \frac{51.71}{51.71 + 138.89} \times 100$$

$$\text{PVC} = \frac{51.71}{190.60} \times 100 = 27.13\%$$

**Step 4: Calculate CPVC**

$$\text{CPVC} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{25 \times 1.08}{93.5}} \times 100$$

$$\text{CPVC} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.2888} \times 100$$

$$\text{CPVC} = \frac{1}{1.2888} \times 100 = 77.59\%$$

**Step 5: Comment**

> Since **PVC (27.13%) << CPVC (77.59%)**, the formulation is in the **binder-rich regime**. There is more than sufficient binder to wet all pigment surfaces and fill interstices. The resulting film will be **continuous, flexible, and glossy**, suitable for a high-gloss enamel or protective coating.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of internal stresses in coating films. Discuss the various sources of internal stresses and their consequences on film integrity. How are tensile and compressive stresses different in their effects?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A water-based emulsion paint has a volume solids content of 38%. The paint is to be applied to achieve a final dry film thickness of 75 µm.
- (i) Calculate the wet film thickness that must be applied.
- (ii) If the dry film has a Young's modulus of 2.8 GPa and Poisson's ratio of 0.38, calculate the theoretical shrinkage stress.
- (iii) If the cohesive strength of the film is 15 MPa, will the film crack? Justify.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Definition:** Internal stresses are residual mechanical stresses that develop within a coating film during and after drying/curing, without any externally applied load.

**Sources of Internal Stresses:**

1. **Solvent Evaporation (Shrinkage Stress):** As solvents leave the film, the volume decreases. The substrate restrains this shrinkage, generating tensile stress.

2. **Crosslinking Shrinkage:** During cure (especially in epoxies and polyesters), covalent bond formation reduces intermolecular distance, causing chemical shrinkage.

3. **Thermal Stresses:** Differences in thermal expansion coefficients between coating and substrate induce stress during temperature changes.

4. **Pigment-Binder Differential Shrinkage:** Pigments and binders shrink at different rates, creating localized microstresses at the interface.

5. **Moisture-Induced Stresses:** Hydrophilic binders swell on moisture absorption, generating compressive stress.

**Tensile vs. Compressive Stress Effects:**

| Aspect | Tensile Stress | Compressive Stress |
|--------|---------------|--------------------|
| Direction | Tries to pull film apart | Tries to push film off substrate |
| Main Cause | Solvent loss, cure shrinkage | Thermal expansion, moisture absorption |
| Primary Failure | Cracking, microcracks, mud-cracking | Blistering, delamination, peeling |
| Critical Property | Cohesive strength | Adhesive strength |
| Common in | Solvent-borne coatings, epoxies | Water-borne coatings, humid service |

**Consequences of Excessive Internal Stress:**
- **Cracking** (mud-cracking, micro-cracks) when tensile stress > cohesive strength
- **Delamination/peeling** when stress > adhesive strength
- **Blistering** from compressive stress
- **Loss of barrier properties** and accelerated corrosion
- **Reduced service life** of the coating system

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Part (i): Wet Film Thickness**

Given: VS = 38% = 0.38, DFT = 75 µm

$$\text{WFT} = \frac{\text{DFT}}{\text{VS}} = \frac{75}{0.38}$$

$$\text{WFT} = 197.37 \, \mu\text{m}$$

> The painter must apply approximately 197 µm of wet film to obtain 75 µm of dry film.

**Part (ii): Theoretical Shrinkage Stress**

Given: $E_f = 2.8$ GPa, $\nu_f = 0.38$, VS = 0.38

First, volumetric shrinkage:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V} = 1 - VS = 1 - 0.38 = 0.62$$

Shrinkage stress:

$$\sigma = \frac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f} \times \frac{\Delta V}{V}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{2.8 \times 10^9}{1 - 0.38} \times 0.62$$

$$\sigma = \frac{2.8 \times 10^9}{0.62} \times 0.62$$

$$\sigma = 2.8 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa} = 2.8 \, \text{GPa}$$

**Part (iii): Will the film crack?**

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Valuation Pitfall:** The theoretical shrinkage stress (2.8 GPa) is far greater than the cohesive strength (15 MPa). However, students must recognize that the **substrate restrains** most of the shrinkage, and the **actual internal stress** is typically 1-10 MPa. The theoretical value is an **upper bound**, not the actual stress in the film. In practice, the actual restrained stress is well below 15 MPa, and the film will **NOT crack** under normal conditions. The question tests whether the student understands the difference between theoretical and actual stress.

**Justification:** Although the theoretical unrestrained shrinkage stress is 2.8 GPa, the rigid substrate absorbs most of the volumetric strain. The actual residual stress in the film is orders of magnitude lower, typically below 10 MPa. Since this is less than the cohesive strength of 15 MPa, the film should remain intact. However, very thick films or poorly adherent substrates may approach or exceed this limit.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Top 3 Mistakes on this Topic:**
> 1. **Mixing up PVC and CPVC:** Many students confuse the two. Remember — PVC is a *formulation* parameter, CPVC is an *intrinsic pigment* property.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mass to volume** when calculating PVC: Always divide mass by density to get volume before using in the PVC formula.
> 3. **Ignoring the factor 93.5** in the CPVC formula: This constant comes from the oil absorption definition (g oil per 100 g pigment) and binder density normalization. Skipping it gives wrong CPVC values.
> 4. **Linear interpolation of properties at CPVC:** Properties change *abruptly* (non-linearly) at CPVC, not gradually. Do not plot linear trend lines through CPVC.
> 5. **Confusing WFT and DFT directions:** WFT > DFT always. Students sometimes write the formula upside down.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **WFT to DFT:** $\text{DFT} = \text{WFT} \times \text{VS}$; always DFT < WFT.
- **Volume Solids (VS):** Fraction of liquid coating that remains as solid film; equals 1 minus volatiles content.
- **PVC:** Volume % of pigment in dry film; determines the binder-to-pigment balance.
- **CPVC:** The "tipping point" PVC at which binder just fills all pigment voids; properties change discontinuously at this point.
- **Golden Rule:** For continuous films, ensure **PVC < CPVC** (typically 10-20% below CPVC for safety margin).
- **CPVC Formula:** $\text{CPVC} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{\text{OA} \times \rho_b}{93.5}} \times 100$; the constant 93.5 comes from OA being in g/100g and oil density ~0.935 g/cm³.
- **Oil Absorption (OA):** Higher OA = more voids = lower CPVC = less pigment tolerance.
- **Film Density:** Theoretical density uses mass-weighted volumes; actual density is lower due to voids (porosity).
- **Internal Stresses:** Arise from solvent loss, cure shrinkage, thermal mismatch, and pigment-binder interaction.
- **Tensile Stress** causes **cracking**; **Compressive Stress** causes **delamination/blistering**.
- **Shrinkage Stress Formula:** $\sigma = \frac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f} \times \frac{\Delta V}{V}$ — the term $\frac{E_f}{1 - \nu_f}$ is the **biaxial modulus** (plane-strain modulus).
- **Property Trend at CPVC:** Gloss ↓, flexibility ↓, permeability ↑, hiding power ↑, stain resistance ↓.
- **Formulation Strategy:** Gloss paints → low PVC (well below CPVC); Flat/matte paints → high PVC (near CPVC); Primers → at or just below CPVC.
- **Stress Mitigation:** Use flexible binders, low-shrinkage resins, plasticizers, proper solvent selection, and adequate film thickness control.
- **Practical DFT Range:** Most industrial coatings are specified at 25-200 µm DFT depending on application.
- **Volume Shrinkage:** Equal to 1 - VS; for VS = 50%, the film loses 50% of its wet volume during drying.
- **CPVC Estimation by Oil Absorption:** A pigment with OA = 20 has higher CPVC than one with OA = 40 (assuming same binder).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT404 - PAINTS & SURFACE COATINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mechanical and Optical Properties of Coatings

# Module 3 — Mechanical and Optical Properties of Coatings

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Defining "Coating Properties" in the KTU 2024 Context

In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus framework, a paint film (after application and curing) must satisfy **two parallel sets of performance criteria** simultaneously — *mechanical* and *optical*. While mechanical properties describe how the film *resists physical forces* (scratching, bending, impact, abrasion), optical properties describe how the film *interacts with visible light* (reflection, scattering, color, transparency).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):**
> *"Mechanical properties of a coating are a set of measurable film characteristics — hardness, adhesion, flexibility, impact resistance, abrasion resistance, tensile strength, and mar resistance — that govern the ability of the dried paint film to withstand mechanical stresses during service. Optical properties — gloss, color, hiding power, whiteness, and refractive index — govern the aesthetic and visual performance of the film."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car Body Analogy"

Imagine you have just painted a brand-new car. Two questions immediately arise:

1. **Will it survive daily life?** Will a stray key scratch the door? Will a stone chip the bonnet? Will the paint crack when the chassis flexes on a pothole? — These are **mechanical property questions**.
2. **Will it *look* good?** Will it shine like a mirror? Will the red color stay vibrant and not turn pink after a year? Will a single coat actually hide the grey primer underneath? — These are **optical property questions**.

So, in essence, mechanical properties answer *"How tough is it?"* and optical properties answer *"How beautiful is it?"* — and a successful coating formulation must excel at **both** simultaneously.

### 1.3 The Two Property Families at a Glance

| Family | Property | Engineering Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Hardness | Pencil grade (6B–10H), König pendulum seconds |
| **Mechanical** | Adhesion | Cross-hatch class (5B–0B), pull-off strength in **MPa** |
| **Mechanical** | Flexibility | Mandrel bend diameter in **mm** |
| **Mechanical** | Impact resistance | Falling-weight height in **cm·kg** or **N·m** |
| **Mechanical** | Abrasion resistance | Mass loss in **mg** per cycle (Taber) |
| **Mechanical** | Mar resistance | Visual rating 0–5 after load |
| **Optical** | Gloss | Specular reflectance at 60° in **Gloss Units (GU)** |
| **Optical** | Color | CIE L\*a\*b\* coordinates |
| **Optical** | Hiding power | Contrast ratio (0–1) or spreading rate in **m²/L** |
| **Optical** | Whiteness | CIE Whiteness Index |
| **Optical** | Refractive index | Dimensionless *n* (typically **1.4–1.7** for resins) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 Scheme PBPOT404 Module 3:**
> This module carries a heavy weightage in the End Semester Examination (ESE) because it bridges *polymer chemistry* (Modules 1–2) with *practical application testing* (Module 5). Students must be able to **define, distinguish, and quote standard test methods** (ASTM, ISO, BS) for every property.
