## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanical Properties — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Hardness

**Definition**: Hardness of a coating is the *resistance of the film surface to indentation, scratching, or penetration by a harder body*.

**Why it matters**: Hardness directly controls scratch resistance, mar resistance, and the ability of the coating to resist dirt pickup.

**Test Methods (High-Yield for KTU)**:

1. **Pencil Hardness Test (ASTM D3363)** — The most commonly quoted in KTU exams.
   * Pencils of known graphite hardness (6B, 5B, 4B, 3B, 2B, B, HB, F, H, 2H, 3H, 4H, 5H, 6H, 7H, 8H, 9H, 10H) are pushed at **45°** against the coated panel under a **1 kg load**.
   * The hardest pencil that does *not* scratch the film defines its hardness.

2. **König Pendulum Hardness (ASTM D4366, ISO 1522)**
   * Measures *damping time* of a pendulum oscillating on the coating surface.
   * Harder films damp the pendulum less, so higher **seconds = harder film**.

3. **Wolff-Wilborn (Scratch) Hardness**
   * Uses pencil leads similar to ASTM D3363 but is the European (ISO 15184) convention.

#### 2.1.2 Adhesion

**Definition**: The force required to separate the dried coating film from the substrate, measured as force per unit area.

**Test Methods**:

1. **Cross-Cut / Cross-Hatch Tape Test (ASTM D3359)** — The KTU favourite.
   * A lattice pattern of cuts is made through the film to the substrate using a sharp blade, pressure-sensitive tape is applied and pulled off at **180°** in **0.5–1 second**.
   * Classification: **5B (no removal) → 4B → 3B → 2B → 1B → 0B (>65% removal)**.

2. **Pull-Off Adhesion Test (ASTM D4541)**
   * A dolly is glued to the coating, and tensile force is applied perpendicular to the surface until failure.
   * Result expressed in **MPa** or **psi**.

#### 2.1.3 Flexibility & Elongation

**Definition**: The ability of the coating film to bend without cracking or losing adhesion.

**Test Method — Mandrel Bend Test (ASTM D522, ISO 6860)**:
* Coated panels are bent around cylindrical mandrels of *decreasing diameter* (typically 25.4 mm, 19.1 mm, 12.7 mm, 9.5 mm, 6.4 mm, 3.2 mm).
* The smallest mandrel diameter around which the film does *not* crack defines flexibility.

**Cupping Test (ISO 1520)**: A coated panel is deformed by a hemispherical punch; depth at first crack (in **mm**) is reported.

#### 2.1.4 Impact Resistance

**Definition**: Ability of the coating to resist cracking, chipping, or detachment when subjected to a sudden mechanical impact.

**Test Methods**:

1. **Falling-Weight (Drop) Impact (ASTM D2794)**
   * A weight of mass **m** falls from height **h** onto the coated side.
   * Impact energy: **E = mgh** in **J** (or **N·m**).
   * Reported as the maximum height the weight can fall without cracking.

2. **Inverse Impact** — Weight strikes the *uncoated side*, testing the coating's ability to stretch with the substrate.

#### 2.1.5 Abrasion Resistance

**Definition**: Resistance of the coating to wearing away by rubbing, scraping, or frictional contact.

**Test Method — Taber Abraser (ASTM D4060)**:
* A coated panel rotates on a turntable while two abrasive wheels (CS-10, CS-17, H-18, H-22) apply a **500 g / 1000 g load**.
* Mass loss is measured after a specified number of cycles (typically **500 or 1000**).
* Result: **Taber Wear Index** = mass loss (mg) per 1000 cycles.

#### 2.1.6 Mar Resistance

**Definition**: Resistance of the coating to *superficial damage* (lighter than scratch) — typically a slight indentation or surface mark that does not penetrate to the substrate.

**Test Method — Balanced Beam Mar Tester (ASTM D5178, D6037)**:
* A stylus scratches the coating under controlled load; the minimum load (in **grams**) that produces a visible mar is the mar resistance.

#### 2.1.7 Tensile Strength & Elongation at Break (ASTM D2370)

* **Tensile Strength (σ)**: Maximum stress the free film can sustain before breaking, in **MPa**.
* **Elongation (%E)**: Percentage increase in length of the film at break.

$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A} \quad ; \quad \%E = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100
$$

where $F$ is the breaking force, $A$ is the cross-sectional area, $L_0$ is the original gauge length, and $L_f$ is the length at break.

---

### 2.2 Optical Properties — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Gloss

**Definition**: Gloss is the *ability of a surface to reflect light specularly* (mirror-like), as opposed to diffusely.

**KTU Standard Angles of Measurement (ASTM D523, ISO 2813)**:

| Angle | Use Case | Typical Gloss Range |
|---|---|---|
| **20°** | High-gloss finishes | > 70 GU |
| **60°** | Semi-gloss and general comparison | 10–70 GU |
| **85°** | Matte / low-gloss finishes | < 10 GU |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 60° reading is the **default reference angle**. If 60° > 70 GU → use 20°. If 60° < 10 GU → use 85°.

**Physical Principle** — Fresnel Reflection:
* At normal incidence, the specular reflectance depends on the **refractive index** of the film:
$$
R_s = \left( \frac{n - 1}{n + 1} \right)^2
$$
where $R_s$ is the specular reflectance (as a fraction) and $n$ is the refractive index of the coating.

* For a typical resin with **n ≈ 1.5**: $R_s \approx 0.04$, i.e. 4% — corresponding to ~ 40 GU on a 60° gloss meter calibrated with a black glass standard of $n = 1.567$.

#### 2.2.2 Color (CIE L*a*b* System — ASTM D1729, D2244)

Color is measured using the **CIE 1976 L\*a\*b\*** uniform color space:

| Coordinate | Range | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **L\*** | 0 (black) → 100 (white) | Lightness |
| **a\*** | −a (green) → +a (red) | Red-green axis |
| **b\*** | −b (blue) → +b (yellow) | Yellow-blue axis |

**Total Color Difference**:
$$
\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> $\Delta E^* < 1$ is generally considered *imperceptible to the human eye*; $\Delta E^* > 3$ is a *clearly visible* difference.

#### 2.2.3 Hiding Power (Opacity)

**Definition**: Hiding power is the *ability of a coating to obscure a substrate of contrasting color*, typically expressed via **Contrast Ratio**.

**Contrast Ratio (ASTM D2805)**:
$$
\text{CR} = \frac{Y_{\text{black}}}{Y_{\text{white}}}
$$
where $Y_{\text{black}}$ is the reflectance of the film over a black substrate, and $Y_{\text{white}}$ is the reflectance over a white substrate.

> CR = 1 → completely opaque (perfect hiding)
> CR = 0 → completely transparent

**Spreading Rate** (practical engineering metric):
$$
\text{Spreading Rate (m}^2\text{/L)} = \frac{\text{Volume of paint (L)}}{\text{Area hidden to CR} = 0.98 \text{ (m}^2\text{)}}
$$

#### 2.2.4 Kubelka-Munk Theory (Two-Flux Model)

For pigmented coatings, light is absorbed and scattered by pigment particles. The **Kubelka-Munk equation** relates the reflectance $R_\infty$ of an *infinitely thick* film to the ratio of absorption coefficient $K$ to scattering coefficient $S$:

$$
\frac{K}{S} = \frac{(1 - R_\infty)^2}{2 R_\infty}
$$

The contrast ratio of a film of thickness $h$ is then:
$$
\text{CR} = \frac{1 - R_\infty \cdot (a - b \cdot \coth(b \cdot S \cdot h))}{a - R_\infty + b \cdot \coth(b \cdot S \cdot h)}
$$
with $a = 1 + K/S$ and $b = \sqrt{a^2 - 1}$.

#### 2.2.5 Whiteness Index (ASTM E313)

$$
WI = 100 - \sqrt{(100 - L^*)^2 + a^{*2} + b^{*2}}
$$

A higher WI indicates a *whiter* and *less yellow* appearance.

#### 2.2.6 Yellowness Index (ASTM E313, D1925)

$$
YI = \frac{100 \cdot (1.3013 X - 1.1498 Z)}{Y}
$$
where $X$, $Y$, $Z$ are CIE XYZ tristimulus values. Lower YI = less yellow (more "fresh").

#### 2.2.7 Haze

Haze is the *ratio of diffuse transmission (or reflection) just outside the specular beam* to the *total transmission*. It is reported as a percentage and is critical for clear coats and automotive topcoats.

$$
\text{Haze } (\%) = \frac{\text{Diffuse Luminescence}}{\text{Total Luminescence}} \times 100
$$

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for ESE)

| # | Property | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tensile Strength | $\sigma = F / A$ | $\sigma$ [MPa], $F$ [N], $A$ [mm²] | ASTM D2370 |
| 2 | Elongation at Break | $\%E = (L_f - L_0)/L_0 \times 100$ | Lengths in mm | — |
| 3 | Impact Energy | $E = m g h$ | $E$ [J], $m$ [kg], $h$ [m], $g$ = **9.81 m/s²** | ASTM D2794 |
| 4 | Fresnel Reflectance | $R_s = ((n-1)/(n+1))^2$ | $n$ dimensionless | At normal incidence |
| 5 | Contrast Ratio | $\text{CR} = Y_{\text{black}} / Y_{\text{white}}$ | Dimensionless, 0–1 | ASTM D2805 |
| 6 | Kubelka-Munk | $K/S = (1 - R_\infty)^2 / (2 R_\infty)$ | $K, S$ in **m⁻¹** (or cm⁻¹) | Infinite-thickness film |
| 7 | CIE Color Difference | $\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$ | All unitless | ASTM D2244 |
| 8 | Whiteness Index | $WI = 100 - \sqrt{(100-L^*)^2 + a^{*2} + b^{*2}}$ | ASTM E313 | — |
| 9 | Taber Wear Index | $TWI = \Delta m \times 1000 / N$ | $\Delta m$ [mg], $N$ = cycles | ASTM D4060 |
| 10 | Spreading Rate | $SR = V / A$ | $V$ in **L**, $A$ in **m²** → **m²/L** | Practical hiding |
| 11 | Mar Resistance Load | $L_{mar}$ in **grams** | Min. load to mar | ASTM D5178 |
| 12 | Pull-off Strength | $\tau = F / A_{dolly}$ | $\tau$ [MPa] | ASTM D4541 |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Property Critical | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive OEM clear coats** | Gloss, DOI, Mar resistance | Showroom shine + car-wash survival |
| **Marine anti-fouling** | Adhesion, Flexibility, Impact | Hull flexes with waves |
| **Aerospace topcoats** | Hardness, Flexibility, Solvent resistance | Extreme service conditions |
| **Architectural exterior** | Hiding power, Color retention, Whiteness | Aesthetics over decades |
| **Can coatings (food)** | Adhesion, Flexibility, Chemical resistance | Stamping + sterilization |
| **Wood finishes** | Hardness, Mar resistance, Scratch | Daily touch and abrasion |
| **Industrial maintenance** | Abrasion, Impact, Chemical | Harsh service floors |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation — Gloss from Refractive Index

**Problem:** A coating has a refractive index *n* = 1.55. Calculate the specular reflectance at normal incidence, and convert it to **Gloss Units (GU)** when measured at 60° against a black-glass standard of *n* = 1.567.

**Step 1 — Fresnel Equation at Normal Incidence**

$$
R_s = \left( \frac{n_{\text{film}} - n_{\text{air}}}{n_{\text{film}} + n_{\text{air}}} \right)^2
$$

**Step 2 — Insert Values**

$$
R_s = \left( \frac{1.55 - 1.00}{1.55 + 1.00} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{0.55}{2.55} \right)^2
$$

$$
R_s = (0.2157)^2 = 0.0465
$$

So the specular reflectance is **4.65 %**.

**Step 3 — Black-Glass Standard Reflectance (Calibration)**

$$
R_{s,\text{std}} = \left( \frac{1.567 - 1}{1.567 + 1} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{0.567}{2.567} \right)^2 = (0.2209)^2 = 0.0488
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Gloss Units**

The gloss meter is calibrated so that the black-glass standard reads **100 GU at 60°** (and **100 GU at 20° and 85°** too, by convention). Therefore:

$$
\text{GU} = \frac{R_s}{R_{s,\text{std}}} \times 100 = \frac{0.0465}{0.0488} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{GU} = 95.3
$$

**Result:** The coating reads approximately **95 GU at 60°**, which classifies as **high gloss** (> 70 GU).

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation — Impact Energy from Drop Weight

**Problem:** A 1 kg steel weight falls from 50 cm onto the coated side of a panel (ASTM D2794 direct impact). Calculate the impact energy in joules.

**Step 1 — Define the formula**

$$
E = m g h
$$

**Step 2 — Convert units to SI**

$$
m = 1 \text{ kg}, \quad g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2, \quad h = 50 \text{ cm} = 0.50 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**

$$
E = 1 \times 9.81 \times 0.50
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate**

$$
E = 4.905 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to N·m (equivalent since J = N·m)**

$$
E = 4.905 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Result:** The impact energy is **≈ 4.9 J**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation — Contrast Ratio and Hiding Power

**Problem:** A white architectural latex paint is applied at 100 µm dry-film thickness over black and white contrast cards. Reflectance over black = 78%, over white = 95%. Compute (a) Contrast Ratio, (b) classify hiding, (c) estimate the Kubelka-Munk K/S ratio.

**Step 1 — Contrast Ratio**

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{Y_b}{Y_w} = \frac{78}{95} = 0.821
$$

**Step 2 — Classify Hiding**

CR = 0.821 < 0.98 → **Incomplete hiding** (translucent).

**Step 3 — Estimate $R_\infty$**

For an opaque (infinitely thick) film, $R_\infty$ is approximately the reflectance of a thick layer. A practical estimate uses the formula:

$$
R_\infty = a - \sqrt{a^2 - 1}, \quad a = \frac{1}{2}\left(R + \frac{1}{R}\right) \text{ where } R = \frac{Y_w}{Y_b}
$$

With $R = 95/78 = 1.2179$:

$$
a = \frac{1}{2}\left(1.2179 + \frac{1}{1.2179}\right) = \frac{1}{2}(1.2179 + 0.8211) = \frac{1}{2}(2.039) = 1.0195
$$

$$
R_\infty = 1.0195 - \sqrt{(1.0195)^2 - 1} = 1.0195 - \sqrt{0.0394} = 1.0195 - 0.1985 = 0.821
$$

**Step 4 — Kubelka-Munk K/S**

$$
\frac{K}{S} = \frac{(1 - R_\infty)^2}{2 R_\infty} = \frac{(1 - 0.821)^2}{2 \times 0.821}
$$

$$
\frac{K}{S} = \frac{(0.179)^2}{1.642} = \frac{0.0320}{1.642} = 0.0195
$$

**Result:** $K/S \approx 0.02$ — typical of a high-quality white pigment (TiO₂).

---

### 3.4 Worked Derivation — Total Color Difference

**Problem:** A coating's reference color is (L\*₁, a\*₁, b\*₁) = (90, +2, −5). After 1000 hours of QUV weathering, the measured values are (L\*₂, a\*₂, b\*₂) = (88, +4, −2). Calculate $\Delta E^*$.

**Step 1 — Component differences**

$$
\Delta L^* = L^*_2 - L^*_1 = 88 - 90 = -2
$$

$$
\Delta a^* = a^*_2 - a^*_1 = 4 - 2 = +2
$$

$$
\Delta b^* = b^*_2 - b^*_1 = -2 - (-5) = +3
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula**

$$
\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(-2)^2 + (2)^2 + (3)^2}
$$

$$
\Delta E^* = \sqrt{4 + 4 + 9} = \sqrt{17}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate**

$$
\Delta E^* = 4.123
$$

**Interpretation**: $\Delta E^* > 3$ → clearly visible color shift. Likely due to pigment degradation or resin yellowing.

---

### 3.5 Worked Derivation — Taber Wear Index

**Problem:** A floor coating loses 18 mg after 1500 cycles on a Taber Abraser with CS-17 wheels under 1000 g load. Compute the Taber Wear Index.

**Step 1 — Formula**

$$
\text{TWI} = \frac{\Delta m \times 1000}{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert Values**

$$
\text{TWI} = \frac{18 \times 1000}{1500} = 12
$$

**Result:** TWI = **12 mg per 1000 cycles**. Lower TWI = better abrasion resistance.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
Define **contrast ratio** of a paint film. Mention the standard test method and classify the hiding power if CR = 0.85.

**Model Answer**:
Contrast ratio is defined as the ratio of the reflectance of a coating film applied over a black substrate to its reflectance over a white substrate, both measured under identical conditions:

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{Y_b}{Y_w}
$$

**Standard method**: ASTM D2805 / ISO 2814.
For CR = 0.85 (less than 0.98) → **incomplete hiding**; the substrate pattern would still be partially visible. The film is classified as **translucent** for the given thickness.

*(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for standard, 1 for interpretation)*

---

#### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]**
Differentiate between **hardness** and **mar resistance** of a coating film. State one standard test method for each.

**Model Answer**:

| Aspect | Hardness | Mar Resistance |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Resistance of the film to *penetration or scratching* by a harder object | Resistance to *light surface marking* that does not cut through the film |
| Severity | Complete cut / deep scratch | Superficial deformation only |
| Standard test | Pencil hardness ASTM D3363 / König ASTM D4366 | Balanced beam ASTM D5178 / Crockmeter |
| Reporting | Pencil grade (e.g., 2H) or König seconds | Minimum marring load (g) or visual rating |

*(3 marks: 1 for each definition, 1 for differentiating the test methods)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module-Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — Module 3 Choice A]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **CIE L\*a\*b\*** color space. Define **total color difference** $\Delta E^*$ and state its significance in coating quality control.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* The reference colour of a coating is (L\*=85, a\*=−2, b\*=+3). After accelerated weathering, the measured colour is (L\*=82, a\*=0, b\*=+5). Calculate $\Delta E^*$ and comment on the visible colour change.

**Model Solution**:

**(a) — CIE L\*a\*b\* Explanation** *(7 marks)*:

The **CIE L\*a\*b\*** (or CIELAB) color space, defined in 1976 by the Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage, is a *device-independent*, *uniform-perceptual* three-dimensional color model. It is widely used in the coatings industry for *color matching*, *quality control*, and *batch-to-batch comparison*.

* **L\* coordinate** (Lightness): Ranges from **0 (pure black)** to **100 (pure white)**. Represents the *vertical* (achromatic) axis.
* **a\* coordinate** (Red–Green axis): Positive values indicate **red**, negative values indicate **green**.
* **b\* coordinate** (Yellow–Blue axis): Positive values indicate **yellow**, negative values indicate **blue**.

The model is *perceptually uniform*, meaning a numerical change of 1 unit in any direction corresponds to roughly the same perceived color difference.

**Total color difference** $\Delta E^*$ between two samples (1 and 2) is given by the Euclidean distance in this 3-D space:

$$
\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}
$$

where $\Delta L^* = L^*_2 - L^*_1$, etc.

**Significance in QC**:
* $\Delta E^* < 1$ → *imperceptible* to the average human eye (acceptable for most commercial applications).
* $1 \leq \Delta E^* < 2$ → perceptible only on close inspection.
* $2 \leq \Delta E^* < 3$ → *perceptible at a glance* but tolerable.
* $\Delta E^* \geq 3$ → *clearly visible*; requires corrective action in production.

The instrument used is typically a **spectrophotometer** (e.g., X-Rite, Konica Minolta) under standardized D65 illuminant / 10° observer geometry.

---

**(b) — Worked Calculation** *(7 marks)*:

**Step 1 — Compute component differences**

$$
\Delta L^* = 82 - 85 = -3
$$

$$
\Delta a^* = 0 - (-2) = +2
$$

$$
\Delta b^* = 5 - 3 = +2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the $\Delta E^*$ formula**

$$
\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(-3)^2 + (2)^2 + (2)^2}
$$

$$
\Delta E^* = \sqrt{9 + 4 + 4} = \sqrt{17}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate**

$$
\Delta E^* \approx 4.123
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

Since $\Delta E^* \approx 4.1 > 3$, the colour shift is **clearly visible** to the human eye. The film has:
* **Darkened** ($\Delta L^* = -3$),
* **Lost green hue** (a\* shifted toward red by +2),
* **Gained yellow hue** (b\* shifted positive by +2).

This is consistent with **pigment degradation and resin yellowing** under accelerated weathering. Production batch should be **rejected** or the formulation reviewed.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — Module 3 Choice B]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the **mechanical properties** of coatings: hardness, adhesion, flexibility, impact resistance, and abrasion resistance. For each, name the standard test and the unit/classification of the result.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A 1.5 kg falling weight is dropped from 80 cm onto a coated panel (ASTM D2794). Calculate the impact energy in joules and N·m. If the Taber Abraser test (CS-17 wheels, 1000 cycles) records a mass loss of 12 mg, calculate the Taber Wear Index.

**Model Solution**:

**(a) — Discussion of Five Mechanical Properties** *(7 marks)*:

1. **Hardness** (1.5 marks): The film's resistance to indentation or scratching.
   * Test: **Pencil Hardness (ASTM D3363)** — pencils 6B to 10H pushed at 45° under 1 kg load.
   * Result: Highest pencil grade that does **not** scratch the film (e.g., "3H").
   * Alternate: König pendulum (ASTM D4366) — measured in **seconds** of damping.

2. **Adhesion** (1.5 marks): Force required to detach the film from the substrate.
   * Test: **Cross-hatch tape test (ASTM D3359)** — cuts + tape pull.
   * Result: Classified from **5B (none removed)** to **0B (>65% removed)**.
   * Alternate: Pull-off (ASTM D4541) — measured in **MPa**.

3. **Flexibility** (1.5 marks): Ability of the film to bend without cracking.
   * Test: **Mandrel bend (ASTM D522)**.
   * Result: Smallest mandrel diameter (in **mm**) around which the film does not crack (e.g., 6.4 mm).

4. **Impact Resistance** (1.5 marks): Resistance to sudden impact loads.
   * Test: **Falling-weight impact (ASTM D2794)**.
   * Result: Maximum impact energy in **J** or **N·m**, or maximum drop height in **cm·kg** without cracking.

5. **Abrasion Resistance** (1 mark): Resistance to wear by friction.
   * Test: **Taber Abraser (ASTM D4060)**.
   * Result: **Taber Wear Index (TWI)** in **mg / 1000 cycles** — mass loss of film.

---

**(b) — Numerical Solutions** *(7 marks)*:

**Step 1 — Impact Energy Calculation**

Formula:

$$
E = m g h
$$

Unit conversion:

$$
h = 80 \text{ cm} = 0.80 \text{ m}
$$

Substitute:

$$
E = 1.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.80
$$

Evaluate:

$$
E = 11.772 \text{ J}
$$

Since $1 \text{ J} = 1 \text{ N·m}$:

$$
E = 11.772 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Taber Wear Index Calculation**

Formula:

$$
\text{TWI} = \frac{\Delta m \times 1000}{N}
$$

Substitute $\Delta m = 12$ mg, $N = 1000$ cycles:

$$
\text{TWI} = \frac{12 \times 1000}{1000} = 12
$$

**Result**: $\text{TWI} = 12 \text{ mg}/1000 \text{ cycles}$.

**Step 3 — Interpretation**

The film withstands ~ 11.8 J of impact energy and loses only 12 mg per 1000 abrasion cycles — indicating a **robust coating system**, suitable for industrial or high-traffic applications.

---

### 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks in PBPOT404 Module 3 questions:**
>
> 1. **Wrong standard numbers** — Quoting "IS 101" instead of **ASTM D3363** for pencil hardness or **ASTM D3359** for cross-hatch adhesion. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for missing or wrong standard.
> 2. **Confusing CR with hiding power** — Students frequently write "CR = 1 means perfect hiding" but forget to mention that the practical acceptance threshold is **CR ≥ 0.98**.
> 3. **Skipping units** — Gloss is always reported in **Gloss Units (GU)**, hardness in **pencil grade**, impact in **J / N·m**. Missing units = **−1 mark each**.
> 4. **Wrong angle for gloss** — Many students incorrectly state 90° as the standard; correct is **60°** with **20°** and **85°** as extremes.
> 5. **Mixing up $\Delta E^*$ components** — Forgetting to *subtract in the correct order* ($\Delta a^* = a^*_2 - a^*_1$) — sign errors frequently cost **1–2 marks**.
> 6. **No interpretation** — Numerical answers without a concluding statement ("Clearly visible", "Within tolerance", etc.) lose **1 mark** for *lack of engineering judgment*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 3 — Mechanical & Optical Properties**

**Mechanical Properties**:
- Hardness — Pencil (ASTM D3363), König (ASTM D4366), in **pencil grade** or **seconds**.
- Adhesion — Cross-hatch (ASTM D3359) → **5B to 0B**; Pull-off (ASTM D4541) → **MPa**.
- Flexibility — Mandrel bend (ASTM D522) → smallest **mm** diameter without crack.
- Impact — Falling-weight (ASTM D2794) → **E = mgh** in **J** or **N·m**.
- Abrasion — Taber (ASTM D4060) → **TWI in mg/1000 cycles**; lower is better.
- Mar — Balanced beam (ASTM D5178) → minimum mar load in **g**.
- Tensile — ASTM D2370 → **σ in MPa**, **%E in %**.
- König pendulum uses **seconds** of damping — *higher = harder*.
- Pencil grades: **6B (softest) → 10H (hardest)**; HB is the middle reference.

**Optical Properties**:
- Gloss — ASTM D523, **60°** default angle; high gloss > 70 GU at 60°.
- Fresnel reflection at normal incidence: $R_s = ((n-1)/(n+1))^2$ → determines the *upper theoretical gloss limit* of any smooth coating.
- Color — CIE L\*a\*b\* (ASTM D2244); $\Delta E^*$ is Euclidean distance; **< 1 imperceptible**, **> 3 clearly visible**.
- Hiding Power — ASTM D2805; **CR = Y_black / Y_white**; **≥ 0.98** = full hiding.
- Kubelka-Munk: $K/S = (1 - R_\infty)^2 / (2 R_\infty)$ — relates pigment *absorption* to *scattering*.
- Whiteness — ASTM E313; higher WI = whiter.
- Haze — ASTM D4039; reported in **%**; critical for clear coats.
- Spreading rate — practical engineering metric, in **m²/L**.

**Key Constants & Values**:
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Refractive index range for typical resins: **1.4 – 1.7**
- TiO₂ K/S ≈ **0.02** (efficient hiding)
- Black-glass standard: $n = 1.567$ → reflectance ≈ **4.9 %** at normal incidence (calibrates 100 GU).

**Cross-Linking Tips**:
- Every formula on the exam needs: **(a) the equation, (b) the substituted values, (c) the unit of the answer, (d) the interpretation**.
- ASTM and ISO standards are the *language* of the examiner — quoting them shows command of the subject.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ageing Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT404 - PAINTS & SURFACE COATINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ageing Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ageing Properties** of a paint or surface coating refer to the **time-dependent changes in the physical, chemical, optical, and mechanical characteristics** of a dried/cured coating film when subjected to prolonged exposure to one or more environmental stressors such as ultraviolet (UV) radiation, heat, moisture, oxygen, and chemical pollutants. They are collectively a measure of the **long-term durability and service life** of a coating.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, ageing properties encompass all the **resistance characteristics** that determine how well a coating retains its functional and aesthetic performance over its intended service life. These properties are evaluated through both **natural (outdoor) weathering** and **accelerated laboratory ageing tests**.

### Key Ageing Characteristics (KTU Syllabus Glossary)

| Ageing Phenomenon | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Weathering** | Overall deterioration of a coating due to combined outdoor exposure |
| **Chalking** | Formation of a powdery, friable surface layer due to binder degradation |
| **Cracking** | Development of fissures in the film as it loses elasticity |
| **Peeling / Flaking** | Loss of adhesion causing the film to detach in pieces |
| **Blistering** | Formation of dome-shaped raised areas due to trapped moisture/osmotic pressure |
| **Yellowing** | Progressive discoloration (especially in alkyds, epoxies) due to chromophore formation |
| **Gloss Reduction** | Loss of surface specular reflectance as roughness increases |
| **Fading / Color Change** | Alteration in pigment due to UV, chemical, or thermal attack |
| **Erosion** | Gradual wearing away of the film thickness |
| **Checking** | Fine surface cracks that do not penetrate the full film thickness |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Ageing properties are evaluated under the broader umbrella of **"Durability Testing"** in Module 3. A coating's ageing behavior is the cumulative result of three simultaneous processes: **Photo-oxidation**, **Hydrolytic Degradation**, and **Thermal Degradation**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a paint film as **human skin** exposed to the sun, wind, and rain for years.

- **Sunlight (UV)** is like the sun burning your skin — it breaks down the polymer "collagen" (the binder) in the paint, causing wrinkles (cracks) and tanning (yellowing).
- **Rain and humidity** are like water on a wound — they seep under the film, cause swelling, and create blisters.
- **Heat** is like a sauna — it accelerates every chemical reaction, including the breakdown of the polymer.
- **Oxygen in the air** is like rust on iron — it slowly oxidizes the binder, making it brittle.
- **Pollutants (SO₂, NOₓ)** are like harsh soaps — they chemically attack the film.

Just as sunscreen (UV stabilizers), moisturizers (plasticizers), and antioxidants extend skin life, paint formulators add **UV absorbers, HALS (Hindered Amine Light Stabilizers), antioxidants, and anti-chalking pigments** to extend coating life.

### Real-World Engineering Example

> [!TIP]
> A marine epoxy coating on a ship hull is expected to last **5–10 years** in tropical seawater. Its ageing properties — blister resistance, cathodic disbondment resistance, and salt-spray resistance — are what determine if the coating survives or fails prematurely, costing lakhs in repainting and corrosion damage.

### Standard Metrics (Highlighted for KTU Board Exams)

- **Service Life**: typically **5–20 years** depending on resin chemistry
- **QUUV Exposure**: measured in **hours** (e.g., 1000 h, 2000 h, 5000 h)
- **Xenon Arc**: measured in **kJ/m²** of total radiant exposure
- **Gloss Retention**: expressed as **% of original 60° gloss**
- **ΔE (Color Change)**: measured in **CIELAB units** (acceptable limit: **ΔE < 2** for most applications)
- **Salt Spray (ASTM B117)**: results in **hours** before failure (e.g., 1000 h, 3000 h)

---

## 1.3 Why Ageing Properties Matter in KTU Curriculum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> From an **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** perspective, ageing properties tie directly to:
> - **CO3**: *Evaluate the performance and durability of paint films under various service conditions.*
> - **Bloom's Level**: Analyze (L4) and Evaluate (L5)
> - **Engineering Significance**: A coating that fails prematurely is not just an aesthetic loss — it is a **functional failure** exposing the substrate to corrosion, weathering, and chemical attack.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanisms of Ageing — The Three Pillars

A coating ages through **three simultaneous, often synergistic mechanisms**. Understanding these is critical for KTU 14-mark derivations.

### Pillar 1: Photo-Oxidation (UV-Driven Degradation)

This is the **most dominant** ageing mechanism for exterior coatings.

**Step-by-step mechanism:**

1. **UV Absorption**: A chromophore (C=O, C=C, aromatic ring, or impurity) in the binder absorbs a UV photon.
2. **Excited State Formation**: The molecule is promoted to an excited singlet state $S_1$ or triplet state $T_1$.

$$ \text{Binder} \xrightarrow{h\nu \; (290-400 \text{ nm})} \text{Binder}^* $$

3. **Chain Scission (Norrish Type I and II)**: The excited bond cleaves, generating free radicals.

$$ \text{R}-\text{CO}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{R}' \xrightarrow{h\nu} \text{R}-\text{CO}^\bullet + {}^\bullet\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{R}' $$

4. **Radical Propagation with O₂**: The radicals react with atmospheric oxygen to form peroxy radicals.

$$ \text{R}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{ROO}^\bullet $$

5. **Hydroperoxide Formation & Decomposition**:

$$ \text{ROO}^\bullet + \text{RH} \longrightarrow \text{ROOH} + \text{R}^\bullet $$

$$ \text{ROOH} \longrightarrow \text{RO}^\bullet + {}^\bullet\text{OH} $$

6. **Termination**: Crosslinking or scission products form, leading to:
   - **Chalking** (in TiO₂ pigmented systems via redox cycle with Ti⁴⁺/Ti³⁺)
   - **Gloss loss** (surface roughening)
   - **Cracking** (embrittlement)
   - **Yellowing** (chromophore build-up)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The **Norrish reactions** (Type I: α-cleavage; Type II: γ-H abstraction with ketene formation) are favourite board questions in coatings chemistry derivations.

---

### Pillar 2: Hydrolytic Degradation

Water (liquid or vapor) attacks **ester, amide, urethane, and siloxane bonds** in the binder.

**Generic hydrolysis reaction:**

$$ \text{R}-\text{CO}-\text{O}-\text{R}' + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{R}-\text{COOH} + \text{R}'-\text{OH} $$

**Rate expression (Pseudo-first order):**

$$ -\frac{d[\text{Ester}]}{dt} = k_h \cdot [\text{Ester}] \cdot [\text{H}_2\text{O}] \approx k' \cdot [\text{Ester}] $$

where $k_h$ is the hydrolysis rate constant and $k' = k_h [\text{H}_2\text{O}]$ (pseudo-first order since water is in excess in humid conditions).

**Vulnerable resin chemistries (descending order of susceptibility):**

| Resin Type | Hydrolytic Stability | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Silicone / Fluoro-polymer | **Excellent** | Si-O-Si and C-F bonds resist hydrolysis |
| Epoxy (cured) | Very Good | Ether linkages are stable |
| Polyurethane (polyester-based) | Moderate | Ester groups vulnerable |
| Polyurethane (polyether-based) | Good | Ether linkages |
| Alkyd | Poor | Many ester groups, unsaturated fatty acids |
| Polyester | Poor-Moderate | Ester hydrolysis |

---

### Pillar 3: Thermal Degradation

At elevated temperatures, **chain scission, crosslinking, and oxidation** occur. For most coatings, ageing rate roughly **doubles for every 10 °C rise** (an approximation of the Arrhenius relationship).

---

## 2.2 Major Ageing Failure Modes — Engineering Description

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Topic:** Students must be able to **distinguish, identify, and explain** each failure mode with its root cause.

### 2.2.1 Chalking
- **Mechanism**: TiO₂ (rutile) catalytically degrades the surrounding binder via a **redox cycle**: Ti⁴⁺ + e⁻ → Ti³⁺; Ti³⁺ + O₂ → Ti⁴⁺ + O₂⁻•.
- The degraded binder leaves loose pigment particles on the surface — a **white powder** that rubs off.
- **Prevention**: Use **non-reactive Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, or SiO₂ surface-treated TiO₂**.

### 2.2.2 Cracking
- **Mechanism**: As the binder degrades, the film loses **elasticity** (T_g increases). Internal stress > tensile strength → cracks form.
- **Types**: 
  - *Mud cracking* (irregular, from over-thick application)
  - *Crazing* (fine network)
  - *Thermal cracking* (linear, from thermal cycling)

### 2.2.3 Blistering
- **Mechanism**: **Osmotic pressure** builds under the film as water-soluble species (salts, degradation products) draw in water through the semi-permeable coating.
- **Categories**:
  - *Osmotic blisters* (circular, on contaminated substrates)
  - *Corrosion blisters* (filled with rust-colored liquid)
  - *Solvent blisters* (from trapped solvent)

### 2.2.4 Yellowing
- **Mechanism**: Oxidation of aromatic amines, conjugated dienes, or formation of **polyene chromophores** (λ_max in visible range).
- **Most prone**: Aliphatic polyurethanes (aromatic isocyanates), alkyds, epoxies (amines).

### 2.2.5 Gloss Reduction
- **Mechanism**: Surface roughness increases due to:
  - Differential erosion of binder vs. pigment
  - Pigment bloom
  - Micro-cracking
- **Quantification**: **% Gloss Retention** at 60°

$$ \text{Gloss Retention (\%)} = \frac{G_t}{G_0} \times 100 $$

where $G_t$ = gloss after time $t$, and $G_0$ = initial gloss.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{Gloss Retention} = \dfrac{G_t}{G_0} \times 100$ | Loss of specular reflectance | % | Gloss ageing |
| $\Delta E = \sqrt{(\Delta L)^2 + (\Delta a)^2 + (\Delta b)^2}$ | CIELAB total color change | dimensionless | Color fading |
| $-\dfrac{dC}{dt} = k \cdot C$ | First-order degradation kinetics | concentration/time | Coating loss |
| $C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ | Exponential decay of property | varies | Property retention |
| $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Arrhenius rate constant | s⁻¹ | Accelerated ageing |
| $\text{AF} = \dfrac{t_{\text{field}}}{t_{\text{lab}}}$ | Acceleration factor | dimensionless | Test correlation |
| $t_{2} = t_{1} \cdot 2^{(T_2 - T_1)/10}$ | Rule-of-ten rule of thumb (Q10) | hours | Thermal acceleration |
| $P_{\text{osm}} = \dfrac{nRT}{V}$ (van't Hoff, dilute) | Osmotic pressure in blister | Pa | Blistering analysis |
| $\text{Chalking Rate} \propto I_{\text{UV}} \cdot [\text{TiO}_2] \cdot t$ | Empirical chalking trend | qualitative | UV exposure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Boundary Conditions / Limits:**
> - $\Delta E \le 1$: not perceptible to the human eye
> - $1 < \Delta E \le 2$: perceptible only on close observation (acceptable for most applications)
> - $\Delta E > 3$: clearly noticeable color change (typically a failure)
> - 60° gloss retention $> 50\%$ is generally acceptable for decorative coatings
> - QUV-B (313 nm) is more aggressive than QUV-A (340 nm) for most binders

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Critical Ageing Property | Typical Test Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive | Gloss retention, DOI, color (ΔE) | SAE J2527, SAE J1960 |
| Marine | Blistering, cathodic disbondment | ASTM B117, NACE TM0404 |
| Architectural | Chalking, color fade, dirt pickup | ASTM D4214, ASTM D1729 |
| Aerospace | Hydraulic fluid, Skydrol resistance | BMS 10-60 (Boeing) |
| Industrial maintenance | Salt spray, humidity | ISO 7253, ISO 6270 |
| Pipeline | Cathodic disbondment, soil stress | CSA Z245.20 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Project Hint:** A 14-mark question often asks: *"Explain how you would formulate a coating for 10-year exterior durability. Justify each additive choice."* This tests integration of **mechanism + prevention**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: First-Order Ageing Kinetics of a Coating Property

### Problem Setup
A coating property (e.g., gloss, flexibility, tensile strength) decays during ageing. Empirically, many properties follow **first-order kinetics** with respect to the property itself.

### Step 1: Write the Rate Law
Assume the property $C$ (concentration of "intact" binder linkages, or simply a measurable property like tensile strength) decreases with time:

$$ -\frac{dC}{dt} = k \cdot C $$

The negative sign indicates decay.

### Step 2: Separate Variables
Move all $C$ terms to one side and $t$ to the other:

$$ \frac{dC}{C} = -k \cdot dt $$

### Step 3: Integrate (with limits $C_0$ at $t = 0$ and $C_t$ at $t = t$)

$$ \int_{C_0}^{C_t} \frac{dC}{C} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt $$

$$ \ln(C_t) - \ln(C_0) = -k \cdot t $$

### Step 4: Solve for $C_t$

$$ \ln\left(\frac{C_t}{C_0}\right) = -k \cdot t $$

$$ C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t} $$

### Step 5: Express in Terms of Property Retention (%)

$$ \text{Retention (\%)} = \frac{C_t}{C_0} \times 100 = 100 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t} $$

### Step 6: Solve for Half-Life (time to 50% retention)

At $C_t = 0.5 \cdot C_0$:

$$ 0.5 = e^{-k \cdot t_{1/2}} $$

$$ \ln(0.5) = -k \cdot t_{1/2} $$

$$ t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln(2)}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k} $$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)
> *"A coating loses gloss according to first-order kinetics. After 500 hours of QUV exposure, gloss retention is 60%. Predict the gloss retention after 1000 hours."*

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** From the given data, find $k$.

$$ 60 = 100 \cdot e^{-k \cdot 500} $$

$$ 0.6 = e^{-500k} $$

$$ \ln(0.6) = -500k $$

$$ k = -\frac{\ln(0.6)}{500} = -\frac{(-0.5108)}{500} = 1.0216 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{h}^{-1} $$

**Step 2:** Predict at $t = 1000$ h.

$$ \text{Retention}_{1000} = 100 \cdot e^{-(1.0216 \times 10^{-3})(1000)} $$

$$ = 100 \cdot e^{-1.0216} $$

$$ = 100 \cdot (0.3600) = 36.0\% $$

**Interpretation:** The coating has lost 64% of its gloss in 1000 hours — likely a **failure** by industry standards.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Arrhenius-Based Acceleration Factor for Thermal Ageing

### Problem Setup
We want to predict **outdoor service life** from **elevated-temperature laboratory tests**.

### Step 1: Arrhenius Equation for Rate Constant

$$ k(T) = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} $$

where $A$ is the pre-exponential factor, $E_a$ is activation energy (J/mol), $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$, and $T$ is absolute temperature (K).

### Step 2: Take Ratio at Two Temperatures (T₂ > T₁)

$$ \frac{k(T_2)}{k(T_1)} = \frac{A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT_2}}{A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT_1}} = e^{\frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)} $$

### Step 3: Define Acceleration Factor (AF)
For a given property loss, time is inversely proportional to rate:

$$ \text{AF} = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{k(T_2)}{k(T_1)} = e^{\frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)} $$

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example
> *"A coating is tested at 60 °C and reaches 50% gloss loss in 800 h. If the activation energy is 70 kJ/mol, predict the time to 50% loss at 25 °C."*

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert temperatures to Kelvin.

$$ T_1 = 60 + 273 = 333 \; \text{K}, \quad T_2 = 25 + 273 = 298 \; \text{K} $$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the AF equation.

$$ \text{AF} = e^{\frac{70000}{8.314} \left( \frac{1}{333} - \frac{1}{298} \right)} $$

$$ \frac{1}{333} - \frac{1}{298} = 0.003003 - 0.003356 = -3.529 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{K}^{-1} $$

$$ \text{AF} = e^{\frac{70000 \times (-3.529 \times 10^{-4})}{8.314}} $$

$$ = e^{-2.971} = 0.0513 $$

**Step 3:** Predict outdoor life.

$$ t_{25°C} = \frac{t_{60°C}}{\text{AF}} = \frac{800}{0.0513} = 15{,}594 \; \text{h} \approx 1.78 \; \text{years} $$

**Interpretation:** The coating has only ~1.8 years of service life at 25 °C — clearly an **interior-grade** material.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: CIELAB Color Change (ΔE) Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question**: *"A coating shows color shift from L₁=80, a₁=5, b₁=10 to L₂=78, a₂=7, b₂=12 after 1000 h QUV. Calculate ΔE and comment on acceptability."*

**Step 1:** Compute individual differences.

$$ \Delta L = L_2 - L_1 = 78 - 80 = -2 $$

$$ \Delta a = a_2 - a_1 = 7 - 5 = +2 $$

$$ \Delta b = b_2 - b_1 = 12 - 10 = +2 $$

**Step 2:** Apply the ΔE formula.

$$ \Delta E = \sqrt{(-2)^2 + (2)^2 + (2)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 4 + 4} = \sqrt{12} = 3.46 $$

**Step 3:** Interpret.

$$ \Delta E = 3.46 > 3 \Rightarrow \text{Clearly noticeable color change.} $$

**Conclusion:** The coating has **failed** the color stability requirement for premium exterior applications.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting a Stabilizer Package

| Ageing Failure | Root Cause | Preventive Additive | Mechanism of Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Chalking | TiO₂ photocatalysis | Surface-treated TiO₂ (Al₂O₃/SiO₂/ZrO₂ coating) | Insulates pigment from binder |
| UV-induced chain scission | UV photon absorption | **UV Absorbers** (e.g., benzotriazoles, HALS) | Competitive absorption of UV → harmless thermal energy |
| Free-radical chain oxidation | Radical propagation | **HALS** (Hindered Amine Light Stabilizers) | Convert ROO• to stable species (Denisov cycle) |
| Thermal oxidation | Heat + O₂ | **Antioxidants** (hindered phenols, phosphites) | Scavenge R• and ROO• |
| Hydrolysis | Water + labile bonds | **Hydrolysis-resistant resins** (silicone, fluoropolymer) | Inherently stable bond chemistry |
| Yellowing | Chromophore build-up | **Aliphatic isocyanates**, **HALS**, **phenolic antioxidants** | Prevent conjugated diene formation |
| Gloss loss | Surface erosion | **Micronized waxes**, **proper binder selection** | Topcoat densification |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison: Accelerated vs. Natural Weathering

| Feature | Natural Weathering | Accelerated (QUV / Xenon Arc) |
|---|---|---|
| Duration | 1–10 years | 500–5000 hours |
| Cost | High (field site maintenance) | Low (one-time lab setup) |
| Spectral accuracy | Full solar spectrum | Approximated (limited) |
| Correlation with real life | **Excellent** | **Good to fair** (often rank-correct but not life-accurate) |
| Repeatability | Variable | High |
| KTU Exam Mention | "Emco Wheeling" / "Florida exposure" | QUV (ASTM G154), Xenon Arc (ASTM G155) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define chalking in paint films. Name two pigments that resist chalking and explain why.**

**Model Answer:**

Chalking is the formation of a **loose, powdery layer** on the surface of a paint film caused by the **photo-catalytic degradation of the binder** at the pigment-binder interface. It is most prominent in exterior coatings pigmented with untreated **titanium dioxide (rutile)**, where the Ti⁴⁺/Ti³⁺ redox cycle catalytically breaks down the surrounding polymer binder, leaving behind unbound pigment particles.

**Pigments that resist chalking:**
1. **Surface-treated TiO₂** (coated with Al₂O₃, SiO₂, ZrO₂, or combinations) — the inorganic coating insulates the pigment from the binder and deactivates surface sites.
2. **Inert pigments** such as **iron oxide (Fe₂O₃)**, **chromium oxide (Cr₂O₃)**, and **carbon black** — these do not exhibit photocatalytic activity and therefore do not promote chalking.

Chalking is **measured using ASTM D4214** (tape test, with ratings on a scale of 0–10, where 10 is no chalking).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**What is the QUV accelerated weathering test? Mention the standard used and two types of lamps employed.**

**Model Answer:**

The **QUV accelerated weathering test** is a laboratory method that simulates the damaging effects of sunlight (UV), dew, and rain on coatings in a compressed timeframe. It uses **fluorescent UV lamps** and a **condensation/water spray cycle** to accelerate ageing.

- **Standard**: **ASTM G154** (Standard Practice for Operating Fluorescent Ultraviolet (UV) Lamp Apparatus for Exposure of Materials)
- **Two common lamp types**:
  1. **UVA-340 lamps**: simulate sunlight in the 295–365 nm range (most realistic for outdoor solar simulation)
  2. **UVB-313 lamps**: emit shorter wavelength UV (centered at 313 nm) — more aggressive, used for quick screening and quality control (often gives "worst-case" results)

The test typically cycles between **UV exposure at elevated temperature (e.g., 60 °C)** and **condensation at lower temperature (e.g., 50 °C)**, with cycle times of 4–8 hours each. Total exposure is reported in hours (e.g., 1000 h, 2000 h, 5000 h).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the mechanism of **photo-oxidative degradation** of paint films with the help of the **Boltzman–Norrish free radical chain mechanism**. Mention the role of **HALS** in preventing this degradation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A coating exhibits first-order ageing kinetics. The gloss retention after 1000 hours of QUV exposure is **45%**. Calculate:
(i) The rate constant $k$ of degradation.
(ii) The time required for gloss retention to drop to **20%** of the original value.
(iii) Comment on the suitability of this coating for a **5-year outdoor service** (assume 12 h/day average sunlight, i.e., 4380 h/year × 5 = 21,900 h).

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Photo-oxidative Degradation Mechanism:**

Photo-oxidation is a **free-radical chain reaction** initiated by UV photon absorption by chromophores in the binder (C=O, C=C, aromatic groups, residual unsaturations).

**Initiation Step:**

$$ \text{RH} \xrightarrow{h\nu} \text{R}^\bullet + \text{H}^\bullet $$

A polymer C–H bond (or chromophore) absorbs a UV photon and cleaves homolytically, generating an alkyl radical and a hydrogen atom.

**Propagation Step 1:**

$$ \text{R}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{ROO}^\bullet $$

The carbon-centered radical rapidly reacts with atmospheric oxygen to form a peroxy radical.

**Propagation Step 2:**

$$ \text{ROO}^\bullet + \text{RH} \longrightarrow \text{ROOH} + \text{R}^\bullet $$

The peroxy radical abstracts a hydrogen atom from a neighboring polymer chain, forming a **hydroperoxide (ROOH)** and propagating a new radical center.

**Branching Step:**

$$ \text{ROOH} \xrightarrow{h\nu \; \text{or} \; \Delta} \text{RO}^\bullet + {}^\bullet\text{OH} $$

Hydroperoxides are photolytically and thermally labile — they decompose into alkoxy and hydroxyl radicals, which initiate **two new chain reactions**, hence "branching."

**Termination Step:**

$$ \text{ROO}^\bullet + \text{ROO}^\bullet \longrightarrow \text{ROOR} + \text{O}_2 $$

$$ \text{R}^\bullet + \text{R}^\bullet \longrightarrow \text{R-R} $$

Termination by radical combination or disproportionation ends the chains.

**Consequences:** Chain scission (low MW fragments) and crosslinking both occur, leading to embrittlement, cracking, chalking, gloss loss, and yellowing.

**Role of HALS (Hindered Amine Light Stabilizers):**

HALS (e.g., **Tinuvin 770, Tinuvin 292**) do **not** absorb UV. Instead, they are converted during ageing into stable **nitroxyl radicals (R₂NO•)** via the **Denisov Cycle**:

1. **HALS → Nitroxyl Radical** (by reaction with ROO• or by photo-oxidation of the amine):

$$ \text{R}_2\text{N-H} + \text{ROO}^\bullet \longrightarrow \text{R}_2\text{N}^\bullet + \text{ROOH} $$

2. **Nitroxyl Scavenges Alkyl Radical**:

$$ \text{R}_2\text{N-O}^\bullet + \text{R}^\bullet \longrightarrow \text{R}_2\text{N-O-R} $$

3. **Alkoxyamine Reacts with Peroxy Radical**:

$$ \text{R}_2\text{N-O-R} + \text{ROO}^\bullet \longrightarrow \text{R}_2\text{N-O}^\bullet + \text{ROOR} $$

The **nitroxyl radical is regenerated**, making HALS a true **catalytic** stabilizer. Even at very low concentrations (0.1–1%), HALS dramatically extend coating life — they are the most cost-effective and widely used light stabilizers in modern coatings.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- At $t = 1000$ h: Retention = 45% → $C_t / C_0 = 0.45$
- First-order kinetics: $C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$

**(i) Calculate rate constant $k$:**

$$ 0.45 = e^{-k \cdot 1000} $$

$$ \ln(0.45) = -1000k $$

$$ k = -\frac{\ln(0.45)}{1000} = -\frac{(-0.7985)}{1000} = 7.985 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{h}^{-1} $$

**(ii) Time for retention to drop to 20%:**

$$ 0.20 = e^{-k \cdot t} $$

$$ \ln(0.20) = -k \cdot t $$

$$ t = -\frac{\ln(0.20)}{k} = -\frac{(-1.6094)}{7.985 \times 10^{-4}} = 2015.5 \; \text{h} $$

**(iii) Suitability for 5-year outdoor service:**

**Total service exposure hours:**

$$ 21{,}900 \; \text{h} $$

**Calculate retention at $t = 21{,}900$ h:**

$$ \frac{C_t}{C_0} = e^{-(7.985 \times 10^{-4})(21{,}900)} = e^{-17.49} $$

$$ = 2.50 \times 10^{-8} \approx 0\% $$

**Conclusion:** The retention is essentially **zero** — the coating would have **completely failed** well before 5 years. In fact, even at $t = 2015$ h (≈84 days of constant QUV equivalent), gloss has dropped to 20%, which is already below the **50% retention acceptance criterion** for most exterior decorative coatings.

**Verdict:** This coating is **NOT suitable** for 5-year exterior service. It may be acceptable only for **interior or short-term temporary** applications.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various **ageing failure modes** of paint films (chalking, cracking, blistering, yellowing, gloss reduction). For each, mention the root cause and one preventive measure.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define the **Acceleration Factor (AF)** in ageing studies. A coating is tested at **80 °C** and reaches 50% gloss loss in **200 h**. If the activation energy of degradation is **80 kJ/mol**, calculate the time to 50% gloss loss at **30 °C** (typical tropical service temperature). Comment on the result.

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

| Failure Mode | Root Cause | Preventive Measure |
|---|---|---|
| **Chalking** | TiO₂ photocatalysis; Ti⁴⁺/Ti³⁺ redox cycle degrades binder at pigment surface | Use **surface-treated TiO₂** (Al₂O₃/SiO₂/ZrO₂ coating) or **inert pigments** (Fe₂O₃, Cr₂O₃) |
| **Cracking** | Loss of elasticity as binder Tg rises; internal stress > tensile strength; over-thick application | Use **flexible resins** (long-chain alkyds, polyurethanes), add **plasticizers**, control **DFT** to recommended range |
| **Blistering** | Osmotic pressure buildup from water-soluble species (salts, degradation products) under film; poor surface prep | **Thorough surface preparation** (solvent cleaning, abrasive blasting), use **osmosis-resistant primers** (e.g., epoxy with low water permeability) |
| **Yellowing** | Oxidation of aromatic amines, conjugated dienes, chromophore build-up; especially in alkyds, epoxies, aromatic PUs | Use **aliphatic isocyanates** (HDI, IPDI) instead of TDI/MDI; add **HALS** and **phenolic antioxidants**; use **acrylic** topcoats |
| **Gloss Reduction** | Surface roughening from differential binder/pigment erosion, chalking, micro-cracking | Use **micronized waxes**, **proper pigment dispersion**, **HALS/UV absorbers**, and select **resins with good UV resistance** (acrylic, fluoropolymer) |

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Definition:**

The **Acceleration Factor (AF)** is the ratio of the time to reach a specified failure (e.g., 50% gloss loss) under **service conditions** to the time to reach the same failure under **accelerated laboratory conditions**. Mathematically:

$$ \text{AF} = \frac{t_{\text{service}}}{t_{\text{lab}}} = \frac{k(T_{\text{lab}})}{k(T_{\text{service}})} $$

A higher AF means the lab test compresses more service time into a short test duration.

**Given:**
- $T_{\text{lab}} = 80 + 273 = 353 \; \text{K}$
- $T_{\text{service}} = 30 + 273 = 303 \; \text{K}$
- $t_{\text{lab}} = 200 \; \text{h}$ (time to 50% gloss loss at 80 °C)
- $E_a = 80{,}000 \; \text{J/mol}$
- $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$

**Step 1: Apply the Arrhenius-based AF equation.**

$$ \text{AF} = e^{\frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{\text{service}}} - \frac{1}{T_{\text{lab}}} \right)} $$

$$ \frac{1}{303} - \frac{1}{353} = 3.300 \times 10^{-3} - 2.833 \times 10^{-3} = 4.674 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{K}^{-1} $$

$$ \text{AF} = e^{\frac{80{,}000 \times 4.674 \times 10^{-4}}{8.314}} $$

$$ = e^{4.498} = 89.7 $$

**Step 2: Calculate service life at 30 °C.**

$$ t_{\text{service}} = \text{AF} \times t_{\text{lab}} = 89.7 \times 200 \; \text{h} $$

$$ = 17{,}940 \; \text{h} $$

**Step 3: Convert to years.**

$$ \frac{17{,}940}{8760 \; \text{h/year}} = 2.05 \; \text{years} $$

**Comment:** The coating's 50% gloss loss life at 30 °C tropical service is approximately **2 years**. This is **marginally acceptable** for interior applications but **inadequate** for premium exterior architectural use (typically 5–10 years required). The lab test at 80 °C compressed **~2 years of real-world exposure** into just 200 hours — a useful but limited prediction.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Ageing Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting units in $k$**: Always state $k$ in h⁻¹ or s⁻¹ explicitly. A numerical answer without units loses at least 1 mark.
> 
> 2. **Confusing QUV with Xenon Arc**: QUV uses **fluorescent lamps** (no IR heat); Xenon Arc uses **filtered xenon** (closer to solar spectrum with IR). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 
> 3. **Writing the Norrish mechanism without propagation steps**: Many students write only initiation. You **must** show propagation, branching, and termination for full marks.
> 
> 4. **Using ΔE formula with wrong sign**: $\Delta L = L_2 - L_1$ (not absolute value). Squaring makes the sign irrelevant, but examiners may dock marks if you write $|\Delta L|$ or use the wrong variable order.
> 
> 5. **Mixing up Arrhenius equation forms**: Some students write $k = A \cdot e^{+E_a/RT}$ (wrong sign). It is **always** $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$. The acceleration factor formula is the **inverse** for service life.
> 
> 6. **Omitting the role of HALS in the Denisov Cycle**: Simply stating "HALS scavenges radicals" is **insufficient** for 7 marks. You must show the **regeneration of the nitroxyl radical** — this is the catalytic aspect that examiners specifically look for.
> 
> 7. **Confusing chalking with cracking**: Chalking is **surface powdering**; cracking is **fissure formation**. Examiners will mark you down if you interchange the mechanisms.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ageing Properties**
> 
> **Core Definitions:**
> - Ageing = time-dependent degradation of coating properties under service exposure
> - Durability = ability to resist ageing
> - Weathering = combined effect of UV, moisture, heat, O₂
> 
> **The Three Pillars of Ageing:**
> - **Photo-oxidation** (UV-driven free-radical chain reaction) — most dominant outdoors
> - **Hydrolytic degradation** (water attacks ester, amide, urethane bonds) — most dominant in marine/humid
> - **Thermal degradation** (heat accelerates every reaction) — Arrhenius-controlled
> 
> **Five Visible Failure Modes to Remember (CHALK-PY GC mnemonic):**
> - **C**halking (TiO₂ photocatalysis)
> - **H**ydrolysis-driven blistering (osmotic pressure)
> - **A**lligatoring / Cracking (embrittlement)
> - **L**oss of gloss (surface erosion)
> - **K**aroshi / Yellowing (chromophore build-up)
> - **P**eeling (adhesion loss)
> - **Y**ellowing (oxidation)
> - **G**litter loss = Gloss loss
> - **C**olor fade (ΔE > 3 = failure)
> 
> **Stabilizer Package:**
> - UV absorbers (benzotriazoles) — competitive UV absorption
> - HALS — catalytic radical scavenger via Denisov cycle
> - Antioxidants (phenolic, phosphite) — chain-breaking
> - Surface-treated TiO₂ — pigment deactivation
> 
> **Critical Formulas:**
> - $C_t = C_0 e^{-kt}$ (first-order decay)
> - $t_{1/2} = 0.693 / k$
> - $\text{AF} = e^{(E_a/R)(1/T_{\text{service}} - 1/T_{\text{lab}})}$
> - $\Delta E = \sqrt{(\Delta L)^2 + (\Delta a)^2 + (\Delta b)^2}$
> - $\text{Gloss Retention (\%)} = (G_t / G_0) \times 100$
> 
> **Key Standards (Must Know for KTU):**
> - **ASTM G154** — QUV test
> - **ASTM G155** — Xenon Arc test
> - **ASTM B117** — Salt spray
> - **ISO 6270** — Humidity
> - **ASTM D4214** — Chalking rating
> - **ASTM D1729** — Color change
> - **ASTM D523** — Gloss measurement
> 
> **Acceptance Criteria (Industry Rule of Thumb):**
> - Gloss retention > 50% at end of test
> - $\Delta E \le 2$ for color stability
> - No chalking, cracking, blistering, or peeling

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Rheological Behaviour on Paint Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT404 - PAINTS & SURFACE COATINGS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Rheological Behaviour on Paint Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Rheology in Paint Science

> [!NOTE]
> **Rheology** is the branch of physics/physical chemistry that deals with the **flow and deformation of matter** under the influence of an applied force (stress). In the context of paints and surface coatings, rheology governs how a liquid paint *flows* during application (brushing, spraying, rolling, dipping) and how it *deforms* (or resists deformation) once on the substrate.

The **rheological behaviour of a paint** is determined by the interplay between:
1. The **binder (resin)** — controls intrinsic viscosity and elasticity.
2. The **pigments and extenders** — affect packing, flocculation, and inter-particle forces.
3. The **solvents / water** — control the dispersion medium viscosity.
4. The **additives** — rheology modifiers, thickeners, anti-sag agents, thixotropes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Hook:** Paints are *rarely* simple Newtonian liquids. They are almost always **complex non-Newtonian, viscoelastic fluids** whose viscosity changes with shear rate, time, and deformation history. Understanding this is central to Module 3.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Paint is Alive!"

Imagine you have a **bottle of honey** on a table.

- If you tilt the bottle gently, the honey barely moves — it has a **high yield stress**.
- If you shake the bottle vigorously, the honey flows rapidly — its viscosity has **dropped** under high shear.
- After you stop shaking, the honey gradually thickens back to its original state over time — this is **thixotropy**.

> **A paint behaves exactly like this honey!** During brushing or spraying, it is subjected to **high shear rates** (thousands of s⁻¹), so it must become **thin and free-flowing** for easy application. Once on the wall, the shear stops, and the paint must **recover its viscosity** so that it does not sag, drip, or run on a vertical surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** A perfect paint has a *time-dependent* and *shear-dependent* viscosity — **low at high shear (application)** and **high at low/rest shear (post-application)**. This dual personality is engineered through careful rheology design.

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Rheological Parameters

| Symbol | Parameter | SI Unit | Meaning in Paint |
|--------|-----------|---------|------------------|
| $\tau$ | Shear stress | **Pa** (Pascal) | Force per unit area dragging one layer of paint over another |
| $\dot{\gamma}$ | Shear rate | **s⁻¹** | Velocity gradient between two adjacent layers |
| $\eta$ | Dynamic viscosity | **Pa·s** | Resistance of paint to flow |
| $\tau_0$ | Yield stress | **Pa** | Minimum stress required to initiate flow |
| $G$ | Shear modulus | **Pa** | Elastic (solid-like) recovery component |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition Box:** Viscosity is formally defined as the **ratio of shear stress to shear rate**:
> $$\eta = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$
> A fluid with constant $\eta$ at all shear rates is called a **Newtonian fluid**.

---

## 1.4 Shear Rate Ranges Encountered in Paint Operations

| Operation | Typical Shear Rate ($\dot{\gamma}$) |
|-----------|-----------------------------------|
| Sedimentation / Storage (very low) | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ s⁻¹ |
| Leveling (after application) | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{1}$ s⁻¹ |
| Brushing / Rolling | $10^{1}$ to $10^{3}$ s⁻¹ |
| Spraying | $10^{3}$ to $10^{5}$ s⁻¹ |
| High-speed industrial coating | $10^{5}$ to $10^{6}$ s⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A paint must show **low viscosity at brushing/shear rates (10²–10⁴ s⁻¹)** for smooth application, and **high viscosity at low/resting shear rates (< 1 s⁻¹)** to resist sagging and pigment settling. The rheology curve must be carefully tuned.

---

## 1.5 Why Rheology Dictates Paint Performance — The "Big Picture"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single Most Critical Insight:** *Every* practical defect a paint shows on a wall — brush marks, sag, poor leveling, orange peel, pigment settling, cratering, floating — has a **rheological root cause**. Therefore, **rheology = performance**.

The following paint performance attributes are directly controlled by rheology:

1. **Brushability / Sprayability** — depends on high-shear viscosity.
2. **Leveling** — depends on low-shear viscosity and surface tension.
3. **Sag resistance** — depends on yield stress and thixotropic recovery.
4. **Pigment settling** — depends on low-shear (rest) viscosity.
5. **Brush mark / flow pattern retention** — depends on viscoelastic balance.
6. **Spatter resistance** — depends on elastic recovery (high $G'$).
7. **Storage stability** — depends on yield stress and thixotropy.
8. **Film build and edge coverage** — depends on yield stress.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Flow Behaviour

Paint rheology is best understood by classifying fluids based on the **shape of their $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ curve** (or equivalently, $\eta$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ curve).

### 2.1.1 Newtonian Fluids

A fluid obeying **Newton's law of viscosity**:
$$\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

- $\eta$ is **constant** for all $\dot{\gamma}$.
- The $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ plot is a **straight line** through the origin.
- *Examples:* Water, mineral oil, simple solvents.
- **Very few paints are Newtonian.** Only some very dilute solutions or binders (like mineral spirit + pure resin without pigments/additives) approximate this.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pure binders and solvents are typically Newtonian**, but the moment you add pigments, fillers, and rheology modifiers, the formulation becomes **non-Newtonian**.

---

### 2.1.2 Non-Newtonian Fluids — The Real World of Paints

#### A) Pseudoplastic (Shear-Thinning) Fluids

- Viscosity **decreases** with increasing shear rate.
- The most common rheology in well-formulated paints.
- **Mechanism:** At rest, pigment particles form a loose **flocculated network** held by van der Waals forces. High shear breaks this network, freeing the liquid to flow.
- *Paint examples:* Most latex/emulsion paints, alkyds with proper additives.

#### B) Dilatant (Shear-Thickening) Fluids

- Viscosity **increases** with increasing shear rate.
- **Mechanism:** At rest, particles are closely packed with minimum liquid between them. Under shear, the structure expands, requiring more liquid to lubricate — viscosity rises.
- *Paint examples:* Highly pigmented dispersions, some PVC-rich primers, defoamer-induced defects.
- **Usually undesirable in paints** because it causes poor sprayability and brush drag.

#### C) Plastic (Bingham) Fluids

- Flow only **begins after a yield stress $\tau_0$ is exceeded**.
- After yielding, flow is linear (Newtonian-like).
- Equation:
$$\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_{pl} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

- *Paint examples:* Highly pigmented industrial coatings, some putties, primers with high filler loading.

#### D) Thixotropic Fluids

- **Time-dependent shear thinning**: viscosity decreases under shear, but **recovers gradually** when shear is removed.
- A thixotropic loop on a rheometer shows a hysteresis between the up-curve and down-curve.
- *Paint examples:* Almost all architectural emulsion paints, gel-coats, anti-sag mastics.

#### E) Rheopectic Fluids

- **Time-dependent shear thickening**: viscosity **increases** with time under constant shear.
- Rare in paints, but seen in some specialized lithographic inks and certain gypsum-based coatings.

#### F) Viscoelastic Fluids

- Exhibit both **viscous flow** and **elastic recovery**.
- Modelled by the **complex modulus** $G^* = G' + iG''$
  - $G'$ = storage modulus (elastic, solid-like character).
  - $G''$ = loss modulus (viscous, liquid-like character).
- High $G'$ → better anti-sag, anti-spatter, better edge build.
- High $G''$ → better leveling, smoother flow.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important** reference for solving any KTU 2024 numerical or theoretical question on paint rheology.

| Model | Equation | Viscosity Expression | Key Features | Typical Paint Use |
|-------|----------|----------------------|--------------|-------------------|
| **Newtonian** | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | $\eta = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | Constant $\eta$ | Solvents, pure resin solutions |
| **Power Law (Ostwald–de Waele)** | $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | $\eta = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | $n<1$: pseudoplastic; $n>1$: dilatant | Latex paints, inks |
| **Bingham Plastic** | $\tau = \tau_0 + \eta_{pl} \dot{\gamma}$ | $\eta = \tau_0/\dot{\gamma} + \eta_{pl}$ | Linear flow after yield | Highly filled primers |
| **Casson Model** | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_0} + \sqrt{\eta_\infty \dot{\gamma}}$ | $\eta = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | Better fit at high $\dot{\gamma}$ | Printing inks, some paints |
| **Herschel–Bulkley** | $\tau = \tau_0 + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | $\eta = \tau_0/\dot{\gamma} + K\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Yield + power-law flow | Most non-Newtonian paints |
| **Thixotropy Index (TI)** | $TI = \eta_{\text{low shear}} / \eta_{\text{high shear}}$ | ratio | Quantifies thixotropy | Architectural paints |
| **Levelling Index (LI)** | $LI = \eta_{\infty} / \eta_0$ | ratio | Indicates flow vs. holdout | Coatings |
| **Sag Resistance Criterion** | $\tau_0 > \rho g h \sin\theta$ | — | Prevents sag on vertical surface | All paints on walls |
| **Leveling Time** | $t \propto \eta \lambda / \gamma_{LV}$ | — | Time to flow out brush marks | Brushed paints |
| **Stokes Settling Velocity** | $v = \frac{2 r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_l) g}{9 \eta}$ | — | Pigment settling rate | Storage stability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Units Reminder:** All $\tau$ in **Pa**, $\dot{\gamma}$ in **s⁻¹**, $\eta$ in **Pa·s** (or mPa·s = 0.001 Pa·s). KTU answers must carry consistent SI units to earn full marks.

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Flow Type — Mechanistic Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pseudoplasticity in Paints — The Dominant Mechanism:**
> 1. **At rest:** Pigment particles are weakly flocculated (held by London–van der Waals forces + adsorbed polymer layers). A continuous 3-D network exists, so viscosity is high.
> 2. **During brushing/spraying (high shear):** Hydrodynamic forces overcome the weak inter-particle attractions, **breaking the floc network**. Particles align in the flow direction. Viscosity drops 10–100×.
> 3. **After application (low/rest shear):** Brownian motion and particle re-attraction slowly **rebuild the floc network**, restoring high viscosity. This **time-dependent recovery is thixotropy**.

> **Real-world utility:** This is why a properly formulated emulsion paint feels "thin" when brushed on (shear-thinning) but does not drip from the brush edge or sag on a vertical wall (rapid recovery). The same paint, if formulated incorrectly, will either **run on the wall (too little recovery)** or be **impossible to brush out (too much structure)**.

---

## 2.4 Critical Rheological Parameters and Their Paint Performance Impact

| Rheology Parameter | Performance Attribute | Desired Trend |
|--------------------|----------------------|---------------|
| High-shear viscosity ($\eta_{\infty}$) | Sprayability, brush drag | **Low** for easy application |
| Low-shear viscosity ($\eta_0$) | Leveling, sagging | **Moderate** (balance needed) |
| Yield stress ($\tau_0$) | Anti-sag, edge build, anti-settling | **Sufficiently high** to prevent sag |
| Thixotropic index | Brush mark retention, recovery | **Moderate** to avoid "telegraphing" |
| Storage modulus ($G'$) | Spatter resistance, structure | **High** at low frequency |
| Loss modulus ($G''$) | Leveling, smoothness | **Higher than $G'$** at application frequency |

---

## 2.5 Yield Stress and Sag — Quantitative Criterion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sag Criterion Equation:** A paint will **not sag** on a vertical surface if its yield stress exceeds the gravitational shear stress acting on the wet film:
> $$\tau_0 \geq \rho g h \sin\theta$$
> where $\rho$ = paint density (kg/m³), $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $h$ = wet film thickness (m), $\theta$ = angle from horizontal (90° for a wall, so $\sin\theta = 1$).

> **Numerical Insight:** A typical 100 µm wet film of an architectural emulsion with $\rho \approx 1300 \text{ kg/m}^3$ experiences a gravitational shear stress of about **1.27 Pa**. Therefore, the paint's yield stress must be **at least 1.3–2.0 Pa** to remain sag-free.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Apparent Viscosity from Shear Stress–Shear Rate Data

> **Problem Context:** During a KTU problem, you are given experimental $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ data and asked to identify the flow model and compute the apparent viscosity.

### Step 1: Define Apparent Viscosity

For a **non-Newtonian** fluid, the apparent viscosity is the slope of the line from origin to a chosen data point on the $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ curve:

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Power Law

The power-law (Ostwald–de Waele) model states:

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

Therefore, the apparent viscosity is:

$$\eta_{app} = \frac{K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}}{\dot{\gamma}} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

### Step 3: Take the Logarithm to Find K and n

Taking $\log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$\log \tau = \log K + n \cdot \log \dot{\gamma}$$

This is a **straight line** with slope $n$ and intercept $\log K$. From two data points, we obtain $K$ and $n$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

> **Problem:** A latex paint gives the following rheometer data:
> | $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹) | $\tau$ (Pa) |
> |---------------------|-------------|
> | 10 | 25 |
> | 100 | 95 |
> | 1000 | 360 |
>
> **(a)** Identify the flow model.
> **(b)** Calculate the power-law constants $K$ and $n$.
> **(c)** Find the apparent viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 500 \text{ s}^{-1}$.

### Step 1: Check if Power Law Fits

The power law on a log–log scale should be linear.

Using $\log \dot{\gamma} = 1, 2, 3$ for $\dot{\gamma} = 10, 100, 1000$:

$$\log \tau = \log 25 = 1.398, \quad \log 95 = 1.978, \quad \log 360 = 2.556$$

**Slope between first two points:**

$$n_1 = \frac{1.978 - 1.398}{2 - 1} = 0.580$$

**Slope between last two points:**

$$n_2 = \frac{2.556 - 1.978}{3 - 2} = 0.578$$

Slopes are nearly equal → confirms **Power Law behaviour**.

### Step 2: Average n

$$n = \frac{0.580 + 0.578}{2} = 0.579 \approx 0.58$$

Since $n < 1$, the paint is **pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**. ✔

### Step 3: Solve for K

Using $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ at $\dot{\gamma} = 100$ s⁻¹:

$$95 = K \cdot (100)^{0.58}$$

Compute $(100)^{0.58}$:

$$100^{0.58} = 10^{2 \times 0.58} = 10^{1.16} \approx 14.45$$

So:

$$K = \frac{95}{14.45} \approx 6.57 \text{ Pa·s}^n$$

### Step 4: Apparent Viscosity at $\dot{\gamma} = 500$ s⁻¹

$$\eta_{app} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1} = 6.57 \cdot (500)^{-0.42}$$

Compute $(500)^{-0.42}$:

$$\log(500^{-0.42}) = -0.42 \times 2.699 = -1.134$$

$$500^{-0.42} = 10^{-1.134} \approx 0.0735$$

Therefore:

$$\eta_{app} = 6.57 \times 0.0735 \approx 0.483 \text{ Pa·s} = 483 \text{ mPa·s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** At 500 s⁻¹ (typical brushing shear rate), this paint has a viscosity of ~483 mPa·s — well within the range for smooth brushability. At rest, viscosity will be much higher (shear-thinning recovery).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Herschel–Bulkley Model and Sag Prevention

### Step 1: Generalised Yield + Power Law

The Herschel–Bulkley model is the **most general three-parameter** model:

$$\tau = \tau_0 + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

- $\tau_0$ = yield stress.
- $K$ = consistency index.
- $n$ = flow index.

### Step 2: Special Cases

- If $n = 1$ → reduces to **Bingham plastic**.
- If $\tau_0 = 0$ → reduces to **Power law**.
- If $\tau_0 = 0$ and $n = 1$ → **Newtonian**.

### Step 3: Quantifying Sag Prevention

From Section 2.5, the gravitational stress on a vertical wet film of thickness $h$ and density $\rho$ is:

$$\tau_{grav} = \rho g h$$

For sag-free application:

$$\tau_0 \geq \rho g h$$

Rearranging for **maximum sag-free wet film thickness**:

$$h_{max} = \frac{\tau_0}{\rho g}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Illustration

A primer has $\tau_0 = 3$ Pa, $\rho = 1400$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

$$h_{max} = \frac{3}{1400 \times 9.81} = \frac{3}{13734} \approx 2.18 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 218 \text{ µm}$$

> **Engineering meaning:** You can apply up to ~218 µm of wet film thickness on a vertical wall without sagging. This is the **practical limit of single-coat application**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Stokes' Law and Pigment Settling

For a spherical pigment particle of radius $r$ settling under gravity in a paint of viscosity $\eta$, the terminal settling velocity is given by **Stokes' Law**:

$$v = \frac{2 r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_l) g}{9 \eta}$$

### Step 1: Set Up Force Balance

At terminal velocity, gravitational force = drag force + buoyancy:

$$\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_p g = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_l g + 6 \pi \eta r v$$

### Step 2: Rearrange

$$6 \pi \eta r v = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 (\rho_p - \rho_l) g$$

### Step 3: Solve for v

$$v = \frac{\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 (\rho_p - \rho_l) g}{6 \pi \eta r} = \frac{2 r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_l) g}{9 \eta}$$

### Step 4: Apply to Pigment Settling Prevention

To **prevent settling** during storage, the paint's **low-shear (rest) viscosity** must be high enough to keep $v$ negligibly small. For example, if $r = 5$ µm, $\rho_p - \rho_l = 1500$ kg/m³, then to achieve $v < 1$ mm/day:

$$\eta_0 > \frac{2 r^2 (\rho_p - \rho_l) g}{9 v} = \frac{2 \times (5 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times 1500 \times 9.81}{9 \times (1 \times 10^{-3}/86400)}$$

$$\eta_0 > \frac{2 \times 25 \times 10^{-12} \times 1500 \times 9.81}{9 \times 1.157 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$\eta_0 > \frac{7.36 \times 10^{-7}}{1.04 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 7.1 \text{ Pa·s}$$

> **Conclusion:** The paint needs a low-shear viscosity of **at least ~7 Pa·s** to prevent visible settling of a 5-µm pigment particle over a 1-day storage period. This is achieved through rheology modifiers (HEC, HASE, HEUR, fumed silica, organoclays, etc.).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Rheology Parameter → Performance Mapping

| Performance Attribute | Dominant Rheology Parameter | Equation / Criterion | Engineering Target |
|----------------------|------------------------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| **Brushability** | High-shear viscosity $\eta_{\infty}$ | $\eta_{\infty} \approx 0.1 - 0.5$ Pa·s | Low, smooth drag |
| **Sprayability** | High-shear viscosity $\eta_{\infty}$ | $\eta_{\infty} \approx 0.05 - 0.15$ Pa·s | Very low |
| **Leveling** | Low-shear viscosity $\eta_0$, surface tension $\gamma$ | $t_{level} \propto \eta_0 / \gamma$ | Moderate $\eta_0$, high $\gamma$ |
| **Sag resistance** | Yield stress $\tau_0$ | $\tau_0 \geq \rho g h$ | $\tau_0 > 1 - 5$ Pa typically |
| **Anti-settling** | Low-shear viscosity $\eta_0$ | $v \propto 1/\eta_0$ (Stokes) | $\eta_0 > 5$ Pa·s at rest |
| **Spatter resistance** | Storage modulus $G'$ (high) | $G' > G''$ at low $\omega$ | High $G'$ for structure |
| **Brush-mark retention** | Thixotropy index TI | $TI = \eta_{low}/\eta_{high}$ | Moderate TI for "designer look" |
| **Edge build** | Yield stress $\tau_0$ | Higher $\tau_0$ = better | $\tau_0 > 3$ Pa for sharp edges |
| **Film build** | Low-shear viscosity $\eta_0$ | High $\eta_0$ holds thickness | High at low shear |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Trade-off:** Increasing $\eta_0$ and $\tau_0$ improves sag and settling resistance but **worsens leveling**. Increasing $G'$ improves spatter resistance but may cause brush marks. The art of paint formulation is balancing these competing demands.
