<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **Destructive** and **Non-Destructive** testing of paints. Give two examples of each.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Destructive Testing (DT) | Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Specimen State | **Damaged / Unusable** | **Intact / Serviceable** |
> | Purpose | Determine ultimate properties and failure limits | Evaluate current in-service condition |
> | Cost | Higher per test | Lower per test |
> | **Examples** | Cross-cut adhesion, Taber abrasion, Pull-off, Salt spray, PIG | Magnetic thickness, Gloss, Holiday detection, Color measurement |
>
> **Examples:**
> - **DT:** Taber abrasion test, Pencil hardness test
> - **NDT:** Magnetic film thickness gauge, Glossmeter

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is the principle of **Holiday Detection**? Mention the standards used.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Holiday detection identifies **pinholes, voids, or thin spots** in a coating that can lead to **premature corrosion** of the substrate.
> - **High-Voltage Method:** A probe applies **$500$–$30{,}000$ V DC**; a spark jumps to the conductive substrate at any discontinuity, triggering an alarm. Used for thick film coatings on pipelines.
> - **Low-Voltage Wet Sponge Method:** A water-soaked sponge is dragged over the coating; a current flows at any holiday, completing a circuit. Used for thin film coatings up to **$500$ $\mu$m**.
> - **Standards:** **ASTM D5162, NACE SP0188, ISO 29601**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Adhesion and Pull-Off Test Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Cross-Cut Adhesion Test** as per **ASTM D3359**. Discuss the rating scale and factors affecting the result. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dolly of **$25$ mm diameter** is bonded to a polyurethane coating on a steel substrate. The pull-off test records a maximum force of **$4{,}905$ N** at failure. Calculate the **pull-off adhesion strength in MPa** and comment on the adhesion quality. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**
> 1. **Principle (1 mark):** A lattice pattern is cut through the coating down to the substrate using a sharp blade. Pressure-sensitive tape is applied and rapidly pulled at $180°$. The percentage of coating removed is rated visually.
> 2. **Procedure (3 marks):**
>    - Surface is cleaned and a lattice of **$6 \times 6$ cuts** is made at **$1$ mm or $2$ mm spacing**.
>    - Pressure-sensitive tape (e.g., **3M #250**) is firmly pressed and removed in **$0.5$–$1$ second**.
>    - The cut area is examined under good lighting and compared to the standard rating scale.
> 3. **Rating Scale (2 marks):**
>    - **5B:** 0% removed (no peeling)
>    - **4B:** Less than 5% removed
>    - **3B:** 5–15% removed
>    - **2B:** 15–35% removed
>    - **1B:** 35–65% removed
>    - **0B:** Greater than 65% removed (poorest)
> 4. **Factors Affecting (1 mark):** Substrate prep, blade sharpness, tape quality, operator pressure, coating thickness, ambient humidity.

> **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**
>
> **Step 1:** Identify the formula.
>
> $$P_a = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{F}{\pi r^2}$$
>
> **Step 2:** Find the radius.
>
> $$r = \dfrac{25 \text{ mm}}{2} = 12.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0125 \text{ m}$$
>
> **Step 3:** Compute the area.
>
> $$A = \pi r^2 = \pi \times (0.0125)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$
>
> **Step 4:** Compute the adhesion strength.
>
> $$P_a = \dfrac{4{,}905 \text{ N}}{4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2} = 9{,}990{,}629 \text{ Pa} \approx 9.99 \text{ MPa}$$
>
> **Step 5:** Comment on quality.
> A pull-off strength of **$\approx 10$ MPa** is considered **excellent adhesion** for polyurethane coatings, indicating that the coating will perform well under mechanical and thermal stress.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Hardness, Impact, and NDT Focus (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Describe the **Pencil Hardness Test (ASTM D3363)** and the **Pendulum Hardness Test (König)** in detail. Compare their applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **principle, working, and applications** of the following Non-Destructive Testing methods: **(i) Magnetic Induction Thickness Gauge (ISO 2178)**, **(ii) High-Voltage Holiday Detector (ASTM D5162)**. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**
> **Pencil Hardness (3.5 marks):**
> - Pencils graded **6B (softest) to 6H (hardest)** are used.
> - Each pencil is sanded flat and pushed at **$45°$** under a **$1$ kg load**.
> - The **hardest pencil that does NOT scratch or gouge** the coating is the rated hardness.
> - **Applications:** Quick field QC, automotive OEM, comparative ranking.
>
> **Pendulum Hardness / König (3.5 marks):**
> - A pendulum with stainless steel balls rests on the coating.
> - The time (in seconds) for the swing to dampen from **$6°$ to $3°$** is recorded.
> - **Glass reference** gives a baseline of **$250 \pm 10$ s**.
> - **Applications:** Laboratory testing of lacquers, varnishes, automotive clear coats, and compliance with ISO 1522.
> - **Comparison:** Pencil is qualitative and quick; König is quantitative and reproducible.

> **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**
>
> **(i) Magnetic Induction Gauge (3.5 marks):**
> - **Principle:** A magnetic field generated by the probe interacts with the **ferrous substrate**.
> - The coating acts as an **air gap**, altering magnetic flux.
> - The change in reluctance is directly proportional to **coating thickness**.
> - **Working:** Probe is placed on the coating; the instrument displays thickness in **$\mu$m or mils**.
> - **Applications:** Steel pipelines, ship hulls, structural steel, automotive body panels.
> - **Standard:** **ISO 2178 / ASTM B499**.
> - **Range:** Typically **$0$–$2000$ $\mu$m**.
>
> **(ii) High-Voltage Holiday Detector (3.5 marks):**
> - **Principle:** A high DC voltage is applied across the coating.
> - At any **pinhole, void, or thin spot**, the electric field exceeds the dielectric strength, causing a **spark** that triggers an alarm.
> - **Working:** A wire brush or conductive rubber electrode is moved over the coating while voltage is applied. The detector is calibrated using the formula:
>
> $$V_{\text{test}} = k \cdot \sqrt{d}$$
>
> where $d$ = DFT in $\mu$m and $k$ is a constant (typically **$1250$ for $1$ mm DFT**).
> - **Applications:** **Pipeline coatings (FBE, 3LPE), tank linings, ship hulls** where coating integrity is critical.
> - **Standard:** **ASTM D5162 / NACE SP0274**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–2 marks):** Always convert mm to m when computing stress in Pa or MPa. $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2$ — use this shortcut to save time.
> 2. **Confusing DT with NDT (–1 mark):** The Cross-Cut Test is **DT** (it cuts the film). Gloss and thickness measurement are **NDT**.
> 3. **Skipping Formula Derivation (–2 marks):** In Part B, always write the **formula → substitution → unit conversion → final answer** chain. Do not jump steps.
> 4. **Forgetting Standards (–1 mark):** KTU examiners reward **standard numbers** (ASTM D3359, ISO 2178, etc.). Always mention them.
> 5. **Wrong Holiday Detector Voltage (–1 mark):** High-voltage = thick film ($>500$ $\mu$m); Low-voltage = thin film. Do not mix them up.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION NOTES — Read Before Exam**

- **DT methods damage** the coating; **NDT methods preserve** it.
- **Cross-Cut Test:** ASTM D3359, **5B to 0B** rating (5B = perfect).
- **Pull-Off Test:** $P_a = F / (\pi r^2)$, **MPa or psi** is the unit.
- **Pencil Hardness:** Range **6B (soft) to 6H (hard)**, **$1$ kg load at $45°$**, ASTM D3363.
- **König Pendulum:** Damping time in **seconds**; higher = harder.
- **Taber Abrasion:** Weight loss in **mg per 1000 cycles**, ASTM D4060, CS-10/CS-17 wheels.
- **Impact Resistance:** $E_i = mgh$, units **J or in·lb**, ASTM D2794.
- **Mandrel Bend:** Smallest diameter without cracking = best flexibility, ASTM D522.
- **Ericksen Cupping:** Depth in **mm** at first crack, ISO 1520.
- **Salt Spray:** $5\%$ NaCl, $35°C$, ASTM B117 — **scribe creep** in mm.
- **MEK Rub Test:** ASTM D5402 — solvent resistance.
- **Magnetic Thickness Gauge:** ISO 2178, **ferrous only**.
- **Eddy Current Gauge:** ISO 2360, **non-ferrous (Al, Cu)**.
- **Glossmeter Angles:** $60°$ (general), $20°$ (high gloss), $85°$ (matte), ISO 2813.
- **Color:** CIE L\*a\*b\* via spectrophotometer, ASTM E308.
- **Holiday Detector:** High-V = thick film; Low-V = thin film; ASTM D5162.
- **PIG (Paint Inspection Gauge):** Cuts wedge through coating → **destructive** thickness measurement.
- **WFT to DFT:** $DFT = WFT \times VS\%$.
- **Always write standards** — ASTM, ISO, IS numbers carry 1–2 marks each.
- **Use MPa and $\mu$m** — KTU expects SI units.
- **Mention field application** — Pipeline, marine, automotive, aerospace, construction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Paint Film Defects, Surface Cleaning Treatments, and Paint Application Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT404 - PAINTS & SURFACE COATINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Paint Film Defects, Surface Cleaning Treatments, and Paint Application Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Paint Film Defects — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Paint Film Defects** are defined as any discontinuities, irregularities, or failures observed in the dried/cured paint film that compromise its protective, decorative, or functional properties relative to the substrate and intended service environment (KTU PBPOT404, Module 4, 2024 Scheme).

Paint film defects arise from a complex interplay of:
- **Substrate-related causes** (inadequate surface preparation, contamination, moisture)
- **Paint formulation issues** (incorrect pigment/binder ratio, solvent imbalance)
- **Application errors** (wrong film thickness, poor technique, unsuitable conditions)
- **Curing/environmental stress** (temperature, humidity, UV exposure)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wallpaper Problem"

Imagine you are applying wallpaper to a damp, dusty wall. You paste it carefully, but within weeks:
- It starts **bubbling up** (blistering) due to trapped moisture
- **Cracks** appear because the paste dried too fast
- The **edges lift** (peeling) because the wall was greasy
- The **color fades unevenly** (chalking) due to sunlight

A paint film behaves exactly like this wallpaper — it is a thin polymer layer "glued" to a substrate. Any flaw in the substrate, glue (binder), or environment manifests as a defect. Understanding the defect is essentially **forensic engineering of the paint system**.

### 1.3 Surface Cleaning Treatments — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Surface Cleaning / Surface Preparation** is the systematic removal of contaminants (rust, scale, oil, grease, old paint, salts, dust) from the substrate to create a chemically and physically receptive surface for paint adhesion. Standard reference: **ISO 8501-1** (visual assessment of surface cleanliness) and **SSPC-SP** (Steel Structures Painting Council) standards.

The goal is to achieve a defined surface profile (roughness) and cleanliness grade before coating application.

### 1.4 Paint Application Methods — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Paint Application Methods** are the engineered techniques used to transfer a liquid coating formulation from a container onto a substrate as a uniform, defect-free wet film of controlled thickness, which subsequently dries/cures to form a continuous protective film.

The four classical industrial methods are: **Brushing, Rolling, Spraying (air/airless/electrostatic), and Dipping**, with modern variants like **flow coating, curtain coating, roller coating, and powder coating**.

### 1.5 Intuitive Analogy for Surface Preparation & Application

Think of painting like **planting a garden on a wall**:
- **Surface preparation** = clearing the ground, removing weeds, tilling the soil (you cannot grow plants on rocky, oily ground)
- **Primer** = fertilizer/soil base for roots to grip
- **Application method** = choosing between a watering can (brushing), a hose (spraying), or flooding (dipping)
- **Defects** = wilting plants, peeling leaves, discoloration — symptoms telling you something went wrong upstream

### 1.6 Classification Snapshot (KTU Syllabus Map)

| Category | Key Sub-Topics |
|----------|----------------|
| **Paint Film Defects** | Cracking, alligatoring, blistering, peeling, chalking, flaking, checking, crazing, pinholes, cratering, orange peel, runs/sags, wrinkling, fish-eyes, blooming, yellowing, mildew |
| **Surface Cleaning** | Mechanical (hand/power tools, sandblasting), Chemical (acid pickling, solvent wiping, alkali cleaning), Flame cleaning, Water-jetting |
| **Application Methods** | Brushing, Rolling, Spray (air/airless/electrostatic), Dip, Flow/curtain, Roller coating, Powder coating |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Paint Film Defects — Detailed Taxonomy

#### 2.1.1 Cracking & Crazing
- **Definition**: A pattern of fractures in the paint film ranging from fine hairline **checking/crazing** (surface only) to deep **cracking/alligatoring** (penetrating to substrate).
- **Mechanism**: Internal stress > cohesive strength of film. Caused by:
  - Over-application (excessive film thickness)
  - Topcoat too rigid over flexible undercoat
  - Solvent entrapment beneath skin
  - Substrate movement (thermal expansion, vibration)

> [!NOTE]
> **Checking** = shallow, fine cracks (< 1 mm). **Alligatoring** = large, scale-like deep cracks resembling reptile skin. **Crazing** = fine, interconnected network of surface cracks.

#### 2.1.2 Blistering
- **Definition**: Dome-shaped swellings (bubbles) in the paint film, often containing liquid or gas.
- **Mechanism**: **Osmotic or vapor-pressure driven** — water/solvent permeates through the film and accumulates at the substrate interface.
- **Causes**: Moisture in substrate, soluble salts (sulphates, chlorides), solvent entrapment, cathodic disbondment.

#### 2.1.3 Peeling & Flaking
- **Definition**: Loss of adhesion resulting in paint detaching from substrate (peeling) or self-detaching as flakes.
- **Mechanism**: Adhesive failure at substrate-paint interface due to:
  - Poor surface preparation (oil, rust, mill scale)
  - Moisture ingress
  - Incompatible primer/topcoat
  - Substrate expansion (wood grain)

#### 2.1.4 Chalking
- **Definition**: Formation of a loose, powdery layer on the paint surface due to binder degradation.
- **Mechanism**: UV radiation degrades the polymer binder, releasing pigment particles.
- **Indicator**: Wipe finger across the surface — white powder comes off.

#### 2.1.5 Other Critical Defects

| Defect | Visual Description | Root Cause |
|--------|-------------------|------------|
| **Pinholes** | Tiny holes through film | Solvent pop, porous substrate outgassing |
| **Orange Peel** | Textured surface like orange skin | Poor spray atomization, high viscosity |
| **Runs/Sags** | Drips and curtains | Over-application, low viscosity |
| **Wrinkling** | Wrinkled skin texture | Surface skins before underneath cures |
| **Fish-eyes** | Crater-like depressions | Silicone/oil contamination |
| **Blooming** | Whitish haze on surface | Moisture during drying (esp. lacquers) |
| **Yellowing** | Yellow discoloration | Alkyd resin oxidation, amine curing agents |
| **Mildew** | Black/grey biological growth | High humidity, organic nutrients |

### 2.2 Surface Cleaning Treatments — Engineering Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Mechanical Cleaning

**A. Hand Tool Cleaning (SSPC-SP-2 / St 2)**
- Tools: Wire brushes, scrapers, sandpaper, chipping hammers
- Use: Light rust, small areas, maintenance
- **Surface standard**: Removes loose rust/scale; visible bare metal in 60% of area

**B. Power Tool Cleaning (SSPC-SP-3 / St 3)**
- Tools: Rotary wire brushes, needle guns, sanders, grinders
- Use: Moderate rust, repair work
- **Surface standard**: Very thorough; visible bare metal in 95% of area

**C. Abrasive Blast Cleaning (Sandblasting)**
- Grades per **ISO 8501-1**:
  - **Sa 1** — Light blast
  - **Sa 2** — Thorough blast (commercial)
  - **Sa 2½** — Very thorough blast (most common industrial standard)
  - **Sa 3** — White metal blast (highest cleanliness)
- Abrasives: Sand, steel grit, garnet, aluminum oxide, glass beads, plastic media
- **Surface profile**: 50–100 μm typical, measured by **ASTM D4417** (comparator) or **ASTM D7127** (replica tape)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sa 2½** is the industry benchmark for most protective coating systems on steel. It removes all rust, scale, and old coatings, leaving a uniform matte finish with surface profile 50–75 μm.

#### 2.2.2 Chemical Cleaning

**A. Acid Pickling**
- Uses dilute **H₂SO₄ (5–10%)** or **HCl** to dissolve rust/scale
- Must be followed by thorough water rinse and passivation
- Common in galvanizing pre-treatment

**B. Alkali Cleaning**
- Hot NaOH/Na₂CO₃ solutions remove oils, greases, drawing compounds
- Used in automotive pre-treatment lines

**C. Solvent Cleaning (SSPC-SP-1)**
- Wipe with mineral spirits, xylene, or acetone
- **Mandatory first step** before any mechanical cleaning
- Removes oil, grease, soluble salts

#### 2.2.3 Flame Cleaning
- Oxy-acetylene torch rapidly heats surface
- Thermal shock spalls off mill scale, rust
- Old method, rarely used today; risk of heat distortion

#### 2.2.4 Water Jetting
- **SSPC-SP WJ-1** (clean to white metal, no flash rust)
- **SSPC-SP WJ-2/3/4** (varying degrees)
- High-pressure (700+ bar) ultra-high-pressure water (UHP, >1700 bar)
- Environmentally friendly, no abrasive waste

### 2.3 Paint Application Methods — Engineering Analysis

#### 2.3.1 Brushing
- **Transfer efficiency**: 60–80%
- **Suitable for**: Small areas, touch-up, primers on rough surfaces, edges, corners
- **Film build**: 25–50 μm per coat typical
- **Limitations**: Slow, labor-intensive, brush marks visible

#### 2.3.2 Rolling
- **Transfer efficiency**: 70–85%
- **Suitable for**: Large flat surfaces (walls, decks, ship hulls)
- **Types**: Short nap (smooth), long nap (rough/textured)
- **Film build**: 50–100 μm

#### 2.3.3 Spray Application

| Sub-Method | Atomization | Transfer Efficiency | Typical Use |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Conventional Air Spray** | Compressed air | 25–40% | Automotive, finishing |
| **Airless Spray** | Hydraulic pressure (no air) | 50–70% | Heavy industrial, marine |
| **HVLP** (High Volume Low Pressure) | Soft air jet | 65–80% | Wood finishing, low VOC areas |
| **Electrostatic Spray** | Charged droplets attracted to grounded substrate | 70–95% | Conductive substrates, low waste |

**Key spray parameters**:
- Pressure: 3–7 bar (air spray), 100–500 bar (airless)
- Nozzle orifice: 0.2–1.5 mm
- Spray distance: 15–30 cm
- Spray angle: 10°–80° (fan width)

#### 2.3.4 Dip Coating
- Substrate immersed in paint tank, withdrawn at controlled rate
- **Withdrawal speed** controls film thickness (per Landau-Levich equation, see Section 3)
- **Transfer efficiency**: ~95%
- Used for mass production of small parts (nuts, bolts, frames)

#### 2.3.5 Flow Coating & Curtain Coating
- **Flow coating**: Paint flows over the object via a hose/nozzle; excess drains back to tank
- **Curtain coating**: Object passes through a falling sheet (curtain) of paint
- High efficiency, used for flat panels (doors, furniture)

#### 2.3.6 Roller Coating (Industrial)
- Paint picked up by a rubber roller, transferred to a metering roller, then to the substrate
- Continuous, high-speed application on coil coating lines

#### 2.3.7 Powder Coating
- **No solvent** — dry powder (epoxy, polyester, acrylic) electrostatically sprayed or fluidized-bed dipped
- Cured at 160–200°C
- **VOC = 0**, near-100% utilization
- Used in appliances, automotive, outdoor furniture

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas use SI units. Constants in bold.**

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $WFT = \dfrac{DFT}{VS \times (1 - \rho_v)}$ | Wet Film Thickness from Dry Film Thickness | μm |
| $SPR = \dfrac{A_{actual}}{T_{theoretical}}$ | Spreading Rate (area covered per liter) | m²/L |
| $T_{theoretical} = \dfrac{V_{solid} \times 10}{DFT}$ | Theoretical DFT needed (in microns) | μm |
| $h = 0.94 \cdot \dfrac{(\eta \cdot U)^{2/3}}{\gamma^{1/6} \cdot (\rho g)^{1/2}}$ | Landau-Levich dip coating thickness | m |
| $TE = \dfrac{m_{deposit}}{m_{sprayed}} \times 100\%$ | Transfer Efficiency | % |
| $V_{VOC} = \dfrac{\rho_{VOC} \times m_{paint}}{10^6}$ | VOC content of a paint | g/L |
| $P_{profile} = \dfrac{m_{abrasive} \times v_{impact}^2}{2 \times A}$ | Blast cleaning impact energy density | J/m² |
| $R_a = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_0^L \vert Z(x) \vert \, dx$ | Average surface roughness | μm |

Where:
- **WFT** = Wet Film Thickness, **DFT** = Dry Film Thickness, **VS** = Volume Solids (fraction)
- $\rho_v$ = Volatile fraction, $\eta$ = viscosity, $U$ = withdrawal speed, $\gamma$ = surface tension, $\rho$ = density, $g$ = gravity
- **TE** = Transfer Efficiency, $m_{deposit}$ = mass deposited on substrate, $m_{sprayed}$ = total mass sprayed

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Marine industry**: Blast cleaning to **Sa 2½** + epoxy coating extends ship hull life from 2 to 15+ years
- **Automotive**: Electrostatic spray + powder topcoat achieves 60–80 μm DFT with <5% waste
- **Structural steel**: Cost of surface preparation ≈ 30–50% of total painting cost — quality directly controls service life
- **Aerospace**: Defects like pinholes cause corrosion-fatigue failure; trigger X-ray/visual NDT inspection
- **Civil infrastructure**: Bridges use Zn-rich primer + epoxy + polyurethane system; defects cause catastrophic coating failure within 2 years
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Wet Film Thickness from Dry Film Thickness

This is the **most frequently used formula in KTU examinations** for paint calculations.

**Given**:
- Required Dry Film Thickness (DFT) = 75 μm
- Volume Solids (VS) = 50% (i.e., 0.50)
- Volatile fraction ($\rho_v$ implicit) is already accounted for in VS

**Step 1**: Understand the volume balance

When paint is applied wet, it contains solids + volatile solvents. After drying, only solids remain as the dry film.

$$
V_{wet} = V_{solids} + V_{volatile}
$$

**Step 2**: Express volume solids fraction

$$
VS = \dfrac{V_{solids}}{V_{wet}}
$$

Since $V_{solids}$ becomes the dry film:

$$
DFT = VS \times WFT
$$

**Step 3**: Solve for WFT

$$
WFT = \dfrac{DFT}{VS}
$$

**Step 4**: Numerical Evaluation (DFT = 75 μm, VS = 0.50)

$$
\begin{aligned}
WFT &= \dfrac{75 \, \mu m}{0.50} \\
    &= 150 \, \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5**: Account for volatile fraction explicitly (alternative form)

If the volatile fraction is given separately as $\rho_v$ (e.g., 0.40 means 40% volatiles by volume), then:

$$
VS = 1 - \rho_v
$$

So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WFT &= \dfrac{DFT}{1 - \rho_v} \\
    &= \dfrac{75}{1 - 0.40} \\
    &= \dfrac{75}{0.60} \\
    &= 125 \, \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note**: Students frequently confuse "VS by volume" vs "VS by weight". The formula $WFT = DFT / VS$ uses **volume solids (decimal fraction)**, NOT weight solids. The conversion is $VS_{vol} = VS_{wt} \times \rho_{binder}/\rho_{paint}$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Spreading Rate (Coverage)

**Given**:
- 1 liter of paint
- DFT required = 50 μm
- Volume solids = 60%

**Step 1**: Volume of solids in 1 L

$$
V_{solids} = 1 \, L \times 0.60 = 0.60 \, L = 6 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3
$$

**Step 2**: Area covered at 50 μm DFT

$$
A = \dfrac{V_{solids}}{DFT} = \dfrac{6 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3}{50 \times 10^{-6} \, m}
$$

**Step 3**: Numerical evaluation

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{6 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-5}} \, m^2 \\
  &= 12 \, m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4**: General formula

$$
SPR = \dfrac{VS \times 10}{DFT} \quad \text{(m}^2\text{/L)}
$$

(DFT in μm, factor 10 converts L·μm to m²·10⁻³ L units)

### 3.3 Derivation: Landau-Levich Equation (Dip Coating)

The film thickness deposited during dip coating is governed by viscous drag balancing gravity. For a Newtonian fluid withdrawn vertically at speed $U$:

**Step 1**: Force balance on the meniscus

Viscous force pulling up = Gravity pulling down:

$$
\eta \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy} \sim \eta \cdot \dfrac{U}{h}
$$

**Step 2**: Capillary pressure from meniscus curvature

$$
\Delta P \sim \dfrac{\gamma}{R}
$$

where $R \sim \sqrt{\gamma / (\rho g)}$ (capillary length scale)

**Step 3**: Solve the momentum equation for the meniscus region

Dimensional analysis yields:

$$
h = 0.94 \cdot (\eta U)^{2/3} \cdot \gamma^{-1/6} \cdot (\rho g)^{-1/2}
$$

**Step 4**: Interpretation

- Higher withdrawal speed $U$ → thicker film (more drag)
- Higher viscosity $\eta$ → thicker film
- Higher surface tension $\gamma$ → thinner film (capillary resists)
- Higher density $\rho$ or gravity $g$ → thinner film (gravity drains)

**Step 5**: Worked example

Given: $\eta = 0.1 \, Pa \cdot s$, $U = 0.1 \, m/s$, $\gamma = 0.03 \, N/m$, $\rho = 1000 \, kg/m^3$, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= 0.94 \times (0.1 \times 0.1)^{2/3} \times (0.03)^{-1/6} \times (1000 \times 9.81)^{-1/2} \\
  &= 0.94 \times (0.01)^{0.667} \times (0.03)^{-0.167} \times (9810)^{-0.5} \\
  &= 0.94 \times 0.0464 \times 1.402 \times 0.01010 \\
  &= 6.17 \times 10^{-4} \, m \\
  &\approx 617 \, \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Defect → Cause → Remedy

| Defect | Visual Cue | Primary Cause | Corrective Action |
|--------|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Cracking** | Fine to deep cracks | Film too thick, rigid topcoat | Sand, recoat at correct DFT |
| **Blistering** | Bubbles (small to large) | Osmotic water, salts | Remove, neutralize salts, prime |
| **Peeling** | Sheet-like detachment | Poor surface prep, oil | Strip to substrate, re-prep |
| **Chalking** | Powdery surface | UV degradation of binder | Apply UV-resistant topcoat |
| **Orange Peel** | Bumpy texture | High viscosity, bad atomization | Thin paint, adjust spray |
| **Runs/Sags** | Drips, curtains | Over-thick application | Sand, recoat properly |
| **Pinholes** | Small holes | Solvent pop, gas release | Adjust thinner, substrate prep |
| **Fish-eyes** | Craters | Silicone contamination | Solvent wipe, add defoamer |
| **Wrinkling** | Wrinkled skin | Surface dries before bulk | Use slower solvent, thinner coat |
| **Blooming** | Hazy film | Humid drying conditions | Control environment, add retarder |

### 3.5 Comparative Matrix — Application Methods

| Parameter | Brushing | Rolling | Air Spray | Airless Spray | Electrostatic | Dip |
|-----------|----------|---------|-----------|---------------|---------------|-----|
| **Transfer Efficiency** | 60–80% | 70–85% | 25–40% | 50–70% | 70–95% | 95–100% |
| **Film Build (μm)** | 25–50 | 50–100 | 15–30 | 50–150 | 20–60 | 15–40 |
| **Speed** | Slow | Medium | Fast | Fast | Fast | Fast |
| **Suitable Surface** | Any | Flat | Any | Any | Conductive | Small parts |
| **Skill Level** | Medium | Low | High | High | High | Low |
| **VOC Waste** | Low | Low | High | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Capital Cost** | Very Low | Low | Medium | High | Very High | Medium |

### 3.6 Surface Preparation Standards Cross-Reference

| Standard | Description | Visual Reference | Typical Use |
|----------|-------------|------------------|-------------|
| **SSPC-SP-1** | Solvent cleaning | N/A (mandatory first) | Oil/grease removal |
| **SSPC-SP-2 / St 2** | Hand tool cleaning | Loose rust removed | Touch-up |
| **SSPC-SP-3 / St 3** | Power tool cleaning | Very thorough | Repair |
| **SSPC-SP-6 / Sa 2** | Commercial blast | 2/3 of each area clean | Mild environment |
| **SSPC-SP-10 / Sa 2½** | Near-white blast | 95% clean | Heavy industrial |
| **SSPC-SP-5 / Sa 3** | White metal blast | 100% clean | Immersion service |
| **SSPC-SP WJ-1** | Water jet to white metal | 100% clean, no flash rust | Maintenance |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a) Differentiate between checking and alligatoring in paint films.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Parameter | Checking | Alligatoring |
|-----------|----------|--------------|
| **Depth** | Shallow, surface only | Deep, through entire film to substrate |
| **Pattern** | Fine, short, irregular cracks | Large, interconnected scale-like pattern |
| **Severity** | Cosmetic, minor | Severe, complete film failure |
| **Cause** | Aging, mild stress | Excessive film build, incompatible coats |
| **Remedy** | Topcoat renewal | Complete removal and recoat |

*(1 mark for each correct distinction, 3 marks total for comprehensive table)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(b) Explain the term "chalking" in paint films. State its cause and prevention.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

**Definition** (1 mark): Chalking is the formation of a loose, powdery chalk-like layer on the paint surface caused by **UV degradation of the binder matrix**, releasing unbound pigment particles.

**Cause** (1 mark): Prolonged exposure to sunlight (UV radiation), use of low-quality exterior paints, or insufficient binder content in formulation.

**Prevention** (1 mark): Use **UV-resistant binders** (acrylic, fluoropolymer, polyurethane), apply a UV-stable topcoat (e.g., polyurethane over epoxy), select light-stable pigments (TiO₂, metal oxides), and follow proper maintenance recoating intervals.

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

## QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**Q. (a) Classify paint film defects with suitable examples. Discuss in detail the causes, mechanisms, and remedies for the following defects:**
   **(i) Blistering (7 marks)**
   **(ii) Cracking and Alligatoring (7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### (i) Blistering (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Blistering is the formation of dome-shaped swellings or bubbles in the paint film, typically filled with liquid (water/solvent) or gas, caused by localized loss of adhesion between the film and substrate.

**Classification (2 marks):**
- **Osmotic blisters** — Small (1–3 mm), filled with liquid; caused by soluble salts
- **Vapor blisters** — Larger; caused by thermal expansion of trapped moisture
- **Corrosion blisters** — Contain rusty liquid; associated with active corrosion
- **Solvent blisters** — Occur during drying; contain trapped solvent

**Mechanism (2 marks):**
Water or solvent permeates through the semi-permeable paint film. At the substrate interface, soluble salts (chlorides, sulphates) create an osmotic gradient. Water migrates to dilute the salt solution, generating pressure that lifts the film forming a blister. Cathodic blistering occurs at cathodic sites on metal substrates during corrosion.

**Causes and Remedies (2 marks):**

| Cause | Remedy |
|-------|--------|
| Surface contamination (oil, salts) | Solvent wipe (SSPC-SP-1) + water rinse |
| Soluble salts in substrate | Salt testing (Bresle test), high-pressure water wash |
| Moisture in substrate | Dry substrate below 4% moisture; check dew point |
| Incompatible primer | Use compatible, breathable coating system |
| Excessive DFT | Apply per specification; multi-coat thin films |

#### (ii) Cracking and Alligatoring (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Cracking is the appearance of breaks or splits in the paint film. Alligatoring is a severe form where the cracks form a pattern resembling alligator/crocodile scales, penetrating the entire film to the substrate.

**Mechanism (2 marks):**
Internal stresses develop within the paint film exceeding its tensile/cohesive strength. Sources of stress include:
- **Thermal cycling** (differential expansion between paint and substrate)
- **Film shrinkage** during curing (especially solvent-borne coatings)
- **Substrate movement** (wood grain, vibration)
- **Age-related embrittlement** of the binder

**Stages of cracking (2 marks):**
1. **Checking** — Fine, shallow cracks on the surface; cosmetic only
2. **Crazing** — Random network of fine cracks
3. **Cracking** — Cracks penetrating deeper but not to substrate
4. **Alligatoring** — Deep, wide cracks in scaly pattern, exposing substrate
5. **Flaking** — Cracks lead to complete detachment of scales

**Causes and Remedies (2 marks):**

| Cause | Remedy |
|-------|--------|
| Excessive film thickness (over-application) | Apply at specified DFT; use multi-coat system |
| Rigid topcoat over flexible primer | Use compatible flexible systems (e.g., polyurethane over epoxy) |
| Solvent entrapment | Use recommended thinner; allow flash-off time |
| Substrate movement | Use elastomeric coatings on flexible substrates |
| Aging and embrittlement | Scheduled maintenance recoating |

---

## QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style — ALTERNATIVE]

**Q. (a) Describe the various methods of surface preparation for steel before painting. Explain the ISO 8501-1 grades in detail.**
   **(7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the different methods of paint application with their relative advantages, limitations, and typical areas of application.**
   **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### (a) Surface Preparation Methods (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Surface preparation is the most critical step in achieving coating performance. Industry data shows **70% of coating failures** are attributable to inadequate surface preparation.

**Methods (5 marks):**

**1. Solvent Cleaning (SSPC-SP-1)**
- Removes oil, grease, wax, soluble salts
- Uses mineral spirits, xylene, or acetone
- **Mandatory first step** before any other preparation
- Cannot remove rust or scale

**2. Hand Tool Cleaning (SSPC-SP-2 / St 2)**
- Tools: wire brushes, scrapers, sandpaper, chipping hammers
- Removes loose rust and loose mill scale
- Suitable for maintenance, small areas
- Produces rough surface profile

**3. Power Tool Cleaning (SSPC-SP-3 / St 3)**
- Tools: rotary wire brushes, needle scalers, grinders, sanders
- More thorough than hand tools
- Suitable for repair work and moderate contamination
- Risk of polishing the surface (reduces adhesion)

**4. Abrasive Blast Cleaning (Sandblasting)**
- **Sa 1 (Light blast):** Removes loose rust/scale; light surface visible
- **Sa 2 (Commercial):** ~70% clean; suitable for mild environments
- **Sa 2½ (Very thorough / Near-white):** ~95% clean; **industry standard** for most protective coatings
- **Sa 3 (White metal):** 100% clean; for immersion service and highest specifications
- Abrasives: sand, steel grit, garnet, aluminum oxide
- Achieves controlled surface profile (50–100 μm)

**5. Flame Cleaning**
- Oxy-acetylene torch removes mill scale by thermal shock
- Old method; risk of warping thin sections
- Mostly obsolete

**6. Water Jetting / UHP**
- High-pressure (700 bar) or ultra-high-pressure (1700+ bar) water
- Environmentally friendly, no dust
- Grades: WJ-1, WJ-2, WJ-3, WJ-4 per SSPC-SP WJ
- No abrasive waste disposal issues

**Standards Cross-Reference (1 mark):**
- **ISO 8501-1** — Visual assessment
- **SSPC-SP** — North American standards
- **NACE** — National Association of Corrosion Engineers

#### (b) Paint Application Methods (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Method | Mechanism | Transfer Efficiency | Advantages | Limitations |
|--------|-----------|--------------------:|-----------|------------|
| **Brushing** | Manual brushing with bristles | 60–80% | Low cost; reaches corners; minimal waste; no equipment | Slow; brush marks; labor intensive; limited film build |
| **Rolling** | Cylinder with nap transfers paint | 70–85% | Fast on flat surfaces; uniform; minimal skill | Edges need brushing; texture depends on nap; not for complex shapes |
| **Air Spray** | Compressed air atomizes paint | 25–40% | High quality finish; versatile | High overspray loss; needs skilled operator; booth required |
| **Airless Spray** | Hydraulic pressure atomizes paint | 50–70% | High film build; fast; less overspray | Equipment cost; high pressure hazard |
| **HVLP Spray** | Soft air at low pressure | 65–80% | High efficiency; meets VOC regulations | Slower; needs specialized equipment |
| **Electrostatic Spray** | Charged droplets attracted to grounded part | 70–95% | Wrap-around; minimum waste; uniform | Conductive substrate only; safety from high voltage |
| **Dip Coating** | Immersion and withdrawal | 95–100% | Best for small parts; uniform; low labor | Tank size limits part size; drainage marks; sagging |
| **Flow Coating** | Paint flows over object | 90–95% | Continuous; minimal waste | Limited to simple shapes |
| **Curtain Coating** | Object passes through paint sheet | 95%+ | Excellent uniformity; high speed | Flat objects only; precise leveling required |
| **Powder Coating** | Dry powder electrostatically applied, then cured | ~99% | Zero VOC; no solvent; thick films | Requires oven curing; conductive substrate; limited colors |

**Selection Criteria (2 marks):**
- **Production volume**: Dip/electrostatic for high volume; brushing for low
- **Part geometry**: Brushing/rolling for simple; spray for complex
- **Finish quality**: Electrostatic/air spray for premium
- **Environmental compliance**: Powder/HVLP for VOC limits
- **Film thickness**: Airless for thick; spray/brush for thin

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Volume Solids confusion**: Students write $WFT = DFT \times VS$ instead of $WFT = DFT / VS$. This is a **common 1-mark loss** in Part B calculations.
>
> 2. **Sa 2½ vs Sa 3**: Many students confuse these. **Sa 2½** is "near-white metal" (95% clean); **Sa 3** is "white metal" (100% clean). Use correct terminology in KTU answers.
>
> 3. **Defect drawings**: When asked to "explain" a defect in KTU exams, examiners expect **both cause AND remedy**, not just description. Loss of 2 marks if remedies are missing.
>
> 4. **Application methods**: Don't just list methods — always include **transfer efficiency, advantages, limitations**. Examiners award 1 mark specifically for quantitative comparisons.
>
> 5. **Surface preparation order**: Students forget that **SSPC-SP-1 (solvent cleaning) is mandatory FIRST** before mechanical/abrasive cleaning. Mark awarded for correct sequence.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU PBPOT404 Module 4**

#### A. Paint Film Defects (Most Tested)
- ✅ **Cracking family**: Checking → Crazing → Cracking → Alligatoring → Flaking (in order of severity)
- ✅ **Blistering**: Osmotic, vapor, corrosion, solvent — mechanism is pressure under film
- ✅ **Peeling vs Flaking**: Peeling = substrate-side detachment; Flaking = self-detachment
- ✅ **Chalking**: UV degrades binder → pigment release → powdery surface
- ✅ **Orange Peel**: Spray atomization issue, high viscosity
- ✅ **Fish-eyes**: Silicone contamination — single most diagnostic test
- ✅ **Runs/Sags**: Gravity vs viscosity; over-application
- ✅ **Pinholes**: Solvent pop or gas release from porous substrate

#### B. Surface Preparation Standards (Always Memorize)
- ✅ **SSPC-SP-1** = Solvent cleaning (mandatory first step)
- ✅ **SSPC-SP-2** = Hand tool, **SP-3** = Power tool
- ✅ **Sa 2½** = Near-white blast = **industry benchmark** (95% clean)
- ✅ **Sa 3** = White metal blast (100% clean, for immersion)
- ✅ **ISO 8501-1** = Visual assessment reference
- ✅ Surface profile: **50–100 μm** typical for blast cleaning

#### C. Application Methods (Recall Table)
- ✅ **Transfer Efficiency**: Brushing ~70%, Rolling ~75%, Air Spray ~35%, Airless ~60%, HVLP ~75%, Electrostatic ~85%, Dip ~95%, Powder ~99%
- ✅ **Powder coating** = 0% VOC, requires oven curing
- ✅ **Electrostatic** = needs conductive substrate
- ✅ **Airless spray** = high pressure (100–500 bar), high film build
- ✅ **Dip coating** film thickness follows Landau-Levich equation

#### D. Critical Formulas (Practice Solving)
- ✅ $WFT = DFT / VS$ (most important)
- ✅ $SPR = (VS \times 10) / DFT$ in m²/L
- ✅ $TE = (m_{deposit} / m_{sprayed}) \times 100\%$
- ✅ Landau-Levich: $h = 0.94 (\eta U)^{2/3} \gamma^{-1/6} (\rho g)^{-1/2}$

#### E. KTU Frequently Asked Concepts
- ✅ Difference between **checking and alligatoring** (asked in every cycle)
- ✅ **Sa 2½ grade** explanation (asked almost every exam)
- ✅ **Causes and remedies of blistering and cracking** (7-mark favorite)
- ✅ **Transfer efficiency comparison table** (7-mark favorite)
- ✅ **Selection of application method** based on criteria (14-mark question type)

#### F. One-Line Memory Triggers
- 🔑 *"Brush cheap, spray fast, electrostatic efficient, powder green"* (Application methods)
- 🔑 *"Solvent first, then blast, finally inspect"* (Surface prep sequence)
- 🔑 *"DFT = WFT × VS"* (Core relationship)
- 🔑 *"Blister = pressure; Crack = stress; Peel = adhesion; Chalk = UV"* (Defect memory)

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT404 - PAINTS & SURFACE COATINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Project-Based Learning: Polymer-Based Paint Formulation, Component Analysis, Pigment Dispersion Equipment Design, and Manufacturing of Water and Solvent Based Paints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Polymer-Based Paint Formulation"?

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
Polymer-based paint formulation is the engineering process of selecting, quantifying, and combining a polymeric binder (resin), pigments, extenders (fillers), solvents/diluents, and functional additives in precise ratios to produce a homogeneous coating system that meets specified performance criteria — including opacity, gloss, durability, chemical resistance, drying time, and film integrity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A paint is **NOT** just colored liquid. It is a *multi-phase colloidal system* in which a continuous polymer phase (binder) holds discrete pigment particles, all suspended in a volatile carrier that evaporates during film formation.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Concrete Recipe" Intuition

Imagine you are baking a cake:
- **Polymer (Binder)** = Flour + Eggs → This is the structural backbone that holds everything together once dry.
- **Pigments** = Chocolate chips → These give the color and "body" to the cake.
- **Solvents/Water** = Milk → This makes the batter pourable, but evaporates during baking.
- **Additives** = Baking powder, salt, vanilla → Tiny amounts that dramatically change the final performance.
- **Extenders (Fillers)** = Oats or crushed nuts → Cheap bulk that adjusts texture, opacity, and reduces cost.

Just as a baker adjusts ingredient ratios to make a sponge cake vs. a pound cake, a paint formulator adjusts PVC (Pigment Volume Concentration), binder type, and additive package to design a **matte interior wall paint** vs. a **glossy automotive enamel**.

## 1.2 What is "Pigment Dispersion Equipment Design"?

**Definition:**
Pigment dispersion equipment design refers to the engineering selection, sizing, and configuration of mechanical equipment — high-speed dispersers (HSD), sand mills, ball mills, and three-roll mills — used to break down pigment agglomerates into primary particles and uniformly distribute them within the binder/solvent medium. Design considerations include **tip speed (m/s)**, **agitator power (kW)**, **tank geometry (H:D ratio)**, and **media selection (size, density, chemistry)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in Dispersion Design:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Density of steel media: **ρ_steel ≈ 7800 kg/m³**
> - Density of zirconia media: **ρ_ZrO₂ ≈ 6100 kg/m³**
> - Density of glass beads: **ρ_glass ≈ 2500 kg/m³**
> - Standard HSD tip speed range: **15–25 m/s** for most pigments.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Grinder" Intuition

Think of instant coffee powder. If you just stir coffee powder into water, you get clumps and a gritty drink. But if you first pass the coffee through a grinder (mechanical shear), then stir it in, you get a smooth, dark, uniform beverage. **Pigment dispersion is exactly this process** — except the "powder" (pigment agglomerates, often 50–100 µm) must be reduced to **5–20 µm primary particles** for proper color development, opacity, and gloss.

## 1.3 What is "Manufacturing of Water-Based vs. Solvent-Based Paints"?

| Aspect | Water-Based Paint | Solvent-Based Paint |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous Phase | Water + Co-solvent | Organic solvent (e.g., xylene, mineral spirits) |
| Binder Type | Emulsion/latex polymer (acrylic, VAE) | Solution polymer (alkyd, epoxy, PU) |
| VOC Content | **< 50 g/L** (low-VOC) | **300–500 g/L** (high-VOC) |
| Drying Mechanism | Coalescence + water evaporation | Solvent evaporation + oxidative cure |
| Equipment | Stainless steel (corrosion risk) | Carbon steel acceptable |
| Safety | Lower flammability risk | Flammable vapors, requires ATEX |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **CO4** — *Formulate paints and design associated manufacturing processes for industrial-scale production.*

# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Paint Formulation

A complete paint formulation rests on **four engineering decisions**:

1. **Binder Selection** — Determines chemical resistance, flexibility, hardness, and outdoor durability.
2. **Pigment + Extender System** — Determines color, opacity (hiding power), gloss, and rheology.
3. **Solvent / Water System** — Determines application viscosity, drying profile, and VOC.
4. **Additive Package** — Determines wetting, defoaming, sag resistance, UV stability, and shelf life.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Component Selection Logic

**Step 1 — Define End-Use Requirements**
Ask: Interior or exterior? Gloss level? Substrate (concrete/steel/wood)? Chemical exposure? Cost ceiling?

**Step 2 — Choose the Binder**
- **Acrylic emulsions** → Exterior wall paints, flexible, UV-stable.
- **Alkyds (long/medium/short oil)** → Decorative enamels, industrial primers.
- **Epoxy** → High chemical resistance, marine, floor coatings.
- **Polyurethane** → High gloss, abrasion resistance, automotive.
- **Vinyl acetates (PVA/VAE)** → Cheap interior emulsions.

**Step 3 — Calculate Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC)**

$$PVC = \frac{V_{pigment} + V_{extender}}{V_{pigment} + V_{extender} + V_{binder}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Compare with CPVC**

$$CPVC = \text{Critical PVC at which binder just fills inter-particle voids}$$

**Step 5 — Decide PVC vs. CPVC Position**
- **PVC < CPVC** → Glossy, dense film (binder-rich).
- **PVC ≈ CPVC** → Matte, balanced properties.
- **PVC > CPVC** → Highly porous, matte, "breathable" — used in interior flat paints.

**Step 6 — Calculate Oil Absorption (OA) of Pigment/Extender Blend**

$$OA_{blend} = \sum_{i} x_i \cdot OA_i$$

where $x_i$ is the weight fraction of component $i$ in the pigment/extender mixture, and $OA_i$ is in **g of oil per 100 g of pigment** (ASTM D281).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are essential for KTU Part B numerical questions on formulation.**

| # | Formula | Symbols & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $PVC = \frac{V_p + V_e}{V_p + V_e + V_b} \times 100$ | $V_p$ = pigment volume, $V_e$ = extender vol., $V_b$ = binder vol. (all in cm³ or L) | Determines gloss, porosity, film integrity |
| 2 | $CPVC \approx \frac{1}{1 + \sum \frac{OA_i \cdot \rho_i}{93.5}}$ | $OA_i$ = oil absorption, $\rho_i$ = density (g/cm³) | Predicts CPVC from oil absorption data |
| 3 | $V_{solids} = V_p + V_e + V_b$ | All in mL | Total solid volume in wet paint |
| 4 | $VS = \frac{V_{solids}}{V_{total\,wet}} \times 100\%$ | % Volume Solids | Film thickness prediction |
| 5 | $WFT = \frac{DFT}{VS}$ | WFT = Wet Film Thickness, DFT = Dry Film Thickness (microns) | Application thickness control |
| 6 | $P_{HSD} = \frac{N_p \cdot \rho \cdot D^5 \cdot N^3}{g_c \cdot 10^9}$ | $N_p$ = power number (≈ 5–6 for disc), $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $D$ = impeller diameter (m), $N$ = rpm | HSD motor power sizing |
| 7 | $v_{tip} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ | $v_{tip}$ in m/s, $D$ in m, $N$ in rev/s | Tip speed for dispersion |
| 8 | $t_{grind} \approx \frac{k \cdot OA_{blend}}{E_{mill}}$ | $k$ = empirical constant, $E_{mill}$ = mill efficiency factor | Grinding time estimation |
| 9 | $VOC = \frac{m_{volatile\,org}}{V_{paint}} \times 1000$ | g/L | Regulatory compliance check |
| 10 | $Q_{cooling} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat removal in sand mill (kW) | Process thermal management |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions to Memorize:**
> - HSD tip speed **must be 15–25 m/s** for proper wetting; > 25 m/s causes solvent evaporation.
> - Sand mill media fill: **50–60% of grinding chamber volume** (optimal).
> - CPVC of TiO₂ (rutile): typically **40–45%**.
> - CPVC of carbon black: typically **15–20%** (very high OA).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The equations above are used in:
- **Architectural paint factories** (Berger, Asian Paints) — for batch formulation and cost optimization.
- **Automotive OEM lines** — for matching DFT/WFT to robotic spray parameters.
- **Industrial maintenance coatings** — for chemical plant specifications.
- **R&D labs** — for prototype evaluation before pilot-plant scale-up.

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Determining PVC and CPVC Position

**Problem Setup:**
A formulator designs an interior flat wall paint using:
- TiO₂ (rutile): 200 g, density 4.2 g/cm³, OA = 22 g/100g
- CaCO₃ (extender): 150 g, density 2.7 g/cm³, OA = 18 g/100g
- Acrylic binder: 250 g, density 1.05 g/cm³
- Water + additives: 400 g

**Step 1 — Compute volume of each component**

$$V_{TiO_2} = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{200}{4.2} = 47.62\ cm^3$$

$$V_{CaCO_3} = \frac{150}{2.7} = 55.56\ cm^3$$

$$V_{binder} = \frac{250}{1.05} = 238.10\ cm^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute total solid volume (V_solids)**

$$V_{solids} = 47.62 + 55.56 + 238.10 = 341.28\ cm^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute PVC**

$$PVC = \frac{V_{TiO_2} + V_{CaCO_3}}{V_{solids}} \times 100$$

$$PVC = \frac{47.62 + 55.56}{341.28} \times 100 = \frac{103.18}{341.28} \times 100$$

$$PVC = 30.24\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute CPVC using oil absorption blend**

$$OA_{blend} = \frac{(200 \times 22) + (150 \times 18)}{200 + 150}$$

$$OA_{blend} = \frac{4400 + 2700}{350} = \frac{7100}{350} = 20.29\ g/100g$$

**Step 5 — Apply CPVC formula**

$$CPVC = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{OA_{blend} \cdot \bar{\rho}_{pigment}}{93.5}}$$

where $\bar{\rho}_{pigment}$ (weight-averaged) is:

$$\bar{\rho}_{pigment} = \frac{200 \times 4.2 + 150 \times 2.7}{350} = \frac{840 + 405}{350} = 3.557\ g/cm^3$$

Therefore:

$$CPVC = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{20.29 \times 3.557}{93.5}}$$

$$CPVC = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{72.17}{93.5}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.772}$$

$$CPVC = \frac{1}{1.772} = 0.5643 = 56.43\%$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result**

Since $PVC = 30.24\% < CPVC = 56.43\%$, the formulation is **well below CPVC**, which means:
- Binder completely fills the inter-particle voids.
- Film is **continuous and dense** (suitable for semi-gloss, not flat).
- For a true **flat interior paint**, the formulator would *increase* the CaCO₃ or reduce binder so that PVC approaches or slightly exceeds CPVC (~55–60%).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This calculation is the *heart* of paint formulation. A 5% shift in PVC can change a paint from semi-gloss to matte. This is why the KTU board examiner will test this concept with numerical data.

## 3.2 Derivation: High-Speed Disperser (HSD) Power Requirement

**Given:**
- Impeller diameter: $D = 0.5\ m$
- Impeller speed: $N = 1500\ rpm = 25\ rev/s$
- Slurry density: $\rho = 1400\ kg/m^3$
- Power number for flat-blade disc: $N_p = 5.5$
- Gravitational constant: $g_c = 9.81\ m/s^2$

**Power Number Correlation (Rushton-type):**

$$P = \frac{N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5}{g_c}$$

**Substituting values:**

$$P = \frac{5.5 \times 1400 \times (25)^3 \times (0.5)^5}{9.81}$$

**Compute $N^3$:**

$$N^3 = 25^3 = 15625$$

**Compute $D^5$:**

$$D^5 = 0.5^5 = 0.03125$$

**Substitute:**

$$P = \frac{5.5 \times 1400 \times 15625 \times 0.03125}{9.81}$$

**Numerator:**

$$5.5 \times 1400 = 7700$$

$$7700 \times 15625 = 120{,}312{,}500$$

$$120{,}312{,}500 \times 0.03125 = 3{,}759{,}765.6$$

**Divide by $g_c$:**

$$P = \frac{3{,}759{,}765.6}{9.81} \approx 383{,}259\ W$$

$$P \approx 383\ kW$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This is the theoretical power required under turbulent (Newtonian) conditions. In practice, paints exhibit **non-Newtonian (shear-thinning)** behavior, and form correction factors of 0.4–0.7 are applied. The actual motor is oversized by 1.2–1.5× for startup loads.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Equipment Selection Decision Tree

| Goal / Material | Recommended Equipment | Tip Speed / Energy | Media | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wetting + initial dispersion of dry pigments | **High-Speed Disc (Cowles blade)** | 15–25 m/s | None | First stage in any paint manufacture |
| Fine dispersion of TiO₂, color pigments | **Sand Mill (vertical)** | 8–12 m/s peripheral | Zirconia or silica beads, 0.4–1.2 mm | Industry standard for architectural paints |
| Ultra-fine dispersion (automotive, industrial) | **Bead Mill / Horizontal Mill** | 10–15 m/s | 0.2–0.5 mm zirconia | Highest energy density |
| Carbon black dispersion | **Three-Roll Mill** | N/A (compression shear) | None (rolls themselves) | Highest shear; for pastes & inks |
| Low-viscosity tinting pastes | **Attritor / Pearl Mill** | 5–8 m/s | 1.5–3 mm glass or steel | Batch process |
| Pre-mix only | **Slow-speed paddle mixer** | 0.5–2 m/s | None | Just homogenization |

## 3.4 Process Flow: Water-Based Paint Manufacturing

The process is divided into two distinct stages — **GRIND STAGE** and **LET-DOWN STAGE**:

**Stage 1 — Grind (in High-Speed Disperser or Sand Mill):**
1. Charge water + co-solvent (e.g., 5% glycol) + dispersant + defoamer.
2. Add wetting agent + surfactant.
3. Premix at slow speed (5 m/s tip) for 5 min.
4. Add pigments and extenders slowly under high speed (18–22 m/s).
5. Grind to Hegman gauge 6–7 (≈ 25–30 µm).
6. Transfer to let-down tank.

**Stage 2 — Let-Down (in slow-speed paddle):**
1. Add thickener (HEC) pre-mixed in water.
2. Add coalescing agent (Texanol) for latex films.
3. Add defoamer and pH adjuster (AMP-95).
4. Add biocide and in-can preservative.
5. Add acrylic emulsion binder (last) under gentle stirring.
6. Adjust pH to 8.5–9.5.
7. Filter (200 mesh) and fill.

## 3.5 Process Flow: Solvent-Based Paint Manufacturing

1. Charge solvent (xylene/mineral spirits) in enclosed mixer.
2. Add resin (alkyd, epoxy, PU) — dissolve at 60–80°C with reflux.
3. Charge pigment + extender dispersant.
4. High-speed disperse at 20 m/s tip speed.
5. Sand-mill to Hegman 7–8 (≈ 15–20 µm).
6. Cool to 30°C in let-down tank.
7. Add driers (Co, Mn, Pb naphthenates for alkyds).
8. Add anti-skin agent (MEKO) and UV stabilizer.
9. Adjust viscosity with solvent.
10. Filter and pack in sealed metal containers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern Note:** When asked to "compare water-based and solvent-based manufacturing," students often forget to mention **stainless steel requirement for water-based** (to avoid rust contamination) and **explosion-proof (ATEX) motors for solvent-based** (to prevent ignition of vapors).

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Pigment Volume Concentration (PVC) and Critical Pigment Volume Concentration (CPVC). Explain what happens to a paint film when PVC exceeds CPVC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**PVC** is the percentage of the total dry film volume occupied by pigments and extenders:

$$PVC = \frac{V_{pigment} + V_{extender}}{V_{pigment} + V_{extender} + V_{binder}} \times 100\%$$

**CPVC** is the specific PVC value at which the binder just barely fills all the inter-particle voids between pigment particles. Beyond this point, there is insufficient binder to fully wet and bind the pigment, leading to a porous, non-continuous film.

**When PVC > CPVC:**
- The film becomes **porous and discontinuous**.
- Air voids form where binder is deficient.
- Properties affected: **gloss drops sharply**, opacity may increase, permeability rises, stain resistance falls, and the film becomes chalky.
- This is deliberately exploited in **interior flat/matte wall paints** to achieve very low gloss (60° gloss < 5 units).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any six functional additives used in paint formulation and state the role of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — table format expected):**

| Additive | Function |
|---|---|
| 1. Wetting agent (surfactant) | Reduces surface tension; helps pigment particles mix with vehicle |
| 2. Dispersant | Prevents re-agglomeration of pigment particles in the can |
| 3. Defoamer | Eliminates air bubbles introduced during mixing and application |
| 4. Thickener (rheology modifier) | Controls sag, leveling, and brush-drag; sets viscosity |
| 5. Biocide / Preservative | Prevents bacterial/fungal growth in water-based paints |
| 6. Anti-skin agent (e.g., MEKO) | Prevents oxidative gelling of solvent-based paints in the can |
| 7. UV stabilizer (HALS) | Protects binder from photodegradation in exterior paints |
| 8. Coalescent (e.g., Texanol) | Aids film formation in latex paints by softening polymer particles |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, describe the **complete manufacturing process of a water-based acrylic emulsion wall paint**. List the raw materials used and explain the role of each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A paint is formulated with the following composition. Calculate (i) PVC, (ii) % Volume Solids, and (iii) state whether the film will be glossy or matte. Given: density of TiO₂ = 4.0 g/cm³, CaCO₃ = 2.65 g/cm³, acrylic binder = 1.04 g/cm³, water + additives total volume = 450 mL. **(7 marks)**

| Component | Mass (g) |
|---|---|
| TiO₂ | 250 |
| CaCO₃ | 100 |
| Acrylic binder | 200 |
| Water + additives | 350 |

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Process Flow (7 marks):**

The manufacturing process consists of two distinct stages:

**Stage A — Grind Stage (5 marks breakdown: 1 mark diagram + 4 marks explanation):**
1. Charge water (deionized), co-solvent (ethylene glycol or propylene glycol, ~3–5%), and dispersant into a stainless-steel high-speed disperser.
2. Add defoamer and wetting agent.
3. Premix at low speed (tip speed ~5 m/s) for 5 minutes to wet the dispersant.
4. Slowly add TiO₂, calcium carbonate, and any other extenders.
5. Increase tip speed to 18–22 m/s and disperse for 15–20 minutes.
6. Check Hegman gauge — target 6–7 (≈ 25–30 µm).
7. Transfer grind paste to let-down tank.

**Stage B — Let-Down Stage (1 mark):**
1. Add thickener (HEC or associative thickener) pre-dissolved in water.
2. Add coalescing solvent (Texanol, ~2–4%).
3. Add acrylic latex emulsion under slow stirring.
4. Adjust pH to 8.5–9.5 with AMP-95.
5. Add biocide, in-can preservative, and additional defoamer.
6. Final filtration through 200-mesh screen.
7. Pack in plastic containers.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute volumes:**

$$V_{TiO_2} = \frac{250}{4.0} = 62.5\ cm^3$$

$$V_{CaCO_3} = \frac{100}{2.65} = 37.74\ cm^3$$

$$V_{binder} = \frac{200}{1.04} = 192.31\ cm^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute total solid volume:**

$$V_{solids} = 62.5 + 37.74 + 192.31 = 292.55\ cm^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute total wet volume (approximation: water density ≈ 1 g/cm³):**

$$V_{wet} = V_{solids} + V_{water} = 292.55 + 350 = 642.55\ cm^3$$

**Step 4 — Compute PVC:**

$$PVC = \frac{62.5 + 37.74}{292.55} \times 100 = \frac{100.24}{292.55} \times 100 = 34.26\%$$

**Step 5 — Compute % Volume Solids:**

$$VS = \frac{292.55}{642.55} \times 100 = 45.53\%$$

**Step 6 — Interpret CPVC position:**
For typical TiO₂/CaCO₃ blends, CPVC is around 50–55%. Since $PVC = 34.26\% < CPVC \approx 50\%$, the film is **glossy or semi-glossy** (binder-rich, continuous film).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. Forgetting to convert **mass to volume** using density (using grams as if they were cm³). Always divide by density!
> 2. Mixing up the formula — PVC is **V_pigment / V_total_solids**, NOT V_pigment / V_total_wet.
> 3. Forgetting to state the CPVC position conclusion — a numerical answer without interpretation loses 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **working principle, construction, and design considerations of a vertical sand mill** used in paint manufacturing. Discuss the role of grinding media and tip speed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **manufacturing processes, equipment, safety considerations, and environmental impact** of water-based vs. solvent-based paints in a tabular format with at least 6 parameters. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

**Part (a) — Sand Mill Design (7 marks):**

**Construction (2 marks):**
- Cylindrical grinding chamber (jacketed for cooling).
- Central shaft with discs or pins (rotor).
- Separator screen at top (keeps media inside).
- Bottom feed / top discharge (or vice versa for closed design).
- Cooling water jacket connected to chiller.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
The mill operates on the principle of **inter-particle attrition**. The slurry is fed from the bottom, lifted by rotating discs through a bed of grinding media (zirconia or glass beads, 0.4–1.2 mm). The beads collide with each other and with pigment agglomerates, applying both **impact and shear forces**, breaking agglomerates into primary particles.

**Design Considerations (3 marks):**
- **Media fill**: 50–60% of chamber volume.
- **Tip speed**: 8–12 m/s peripheral velocity.
- **Media size**: smaller beads → finer grind, but higher viscosity and heat.
- **Media density**: zirconia (6100 kg/m³) > glass (2500 kg/m³) for harder pigments.
- **Flow rate**: residence time of 1–5 minutes typical; lower flow = finer grind.
- **Cooling**: $Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ must remove grinding heat.
- **Viscosity of feed**: 50–100 KU (Krebs units) optimal.

**Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Water-Based | Solvent-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Binder | Acrylic/styrene-acrylic latex | Alkyd, epoxy, PU solution |
| Carrier | Water + co-solvent | Mineral spirits, xylene, toluene |
| Drying | Coalescence + water evaporation | Solvent evaporation + oxidation |
| Equipment material | Stainless steel (rust) | Carbon steel acceptable |
| Motor spec | Standard (TEFC) | Explosion-proof (ATEX) |
| VOC content | < 50 g/L | 300–500 g/L |
| Fire hazard | Low | High (flash point of xylene ≈ 25°C) |
| Cleaning solvent | Water | Mineral spirits or acetone |
| Odor | Low | Strong |
| Cost of solvent | Low | Higher (volatile, regulated) |
| Environmental | Preferred (low VOC) | Restricted in many regions |
| Film quality | Breathable, lower gloss | Higher gloss, tougher |

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — High-Yield Revision Points (Read before exam):**

- **PVC < CPVC** → Glossy, dense, continuous film (binders, automotive, industrial).
- **PVC ≈ CPVC** → Balanced properties.
- **PVC > CPVC** → Matte, porous, breathable (interior wall flat paints).
- **CPVC depends on oil absorption** of the pigment/extender blend — higher OA = lower CPVC.
- **HSD tip speed**: 15–25 m/s. Above 25 m/s, solvent evaporates; below 15 m/s, poor dispersion.
- **Sand mill media fill**: 50–60% of grinding chamber. Zirconia > glass in density and durability.
- **Three-roll mill** = highest shear, used for carbon black, inks, and pastes — NOT for water-based latex.
- **Cowles blade** = standard HSD impeller; flat-blade disc; $N_p \approx 5.5$.
- **Water-based paint** requires **stainless steel tanks** (rust contamination risk) and **alkaline pH (8.5–9.5)**.
- **Solvent-based paint** requires **ATEX motors** (explosion-proof) and **inert gas blanketing** of storage.
- **Hegman gauge** measures grind fineness: 0 = 100 µm, 8 = 0 µm. Architectural paints: Hegman 6–7. Automotive: Hegman 7–8.
- **Volume Solids (VS)** determines WFT from DFT: $WFT = DFT / VS$. Critical for coating thickness specs.
- **VOC < 50 g/L** → "low-VOC" / "green" label compliant.
- **Two-stage manufacturing** (GRIND + LET-DOWN) is universal — *never* add the binder in the grind stage.
- **Coalescing agent (Texanol)** is essential in latex paints; without it, the film cracks at low temperatures.
- **Biocide** is essential in water-based paints to prevent microbial spoilage (in-can preservation).
- **Anti-skin agent (MEKO)** is essential in solvent-based alkyd paints to prevent oxidative gelling.
- **Project deliverables** in PBL typically include: formulation table, calculation sheet, process flow diagram, equipment selection justification, and cost analysis.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls to Avoid in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Using grams directly in volume formulas** — always divide mass by density.
> 2. **Confusing PVC with weight-% pigment** — they are *not* the same; PVC is volume-based.
> 3. **Omitting the let-down stage** in process diagrams — guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Stating CPVC as a fixed number** — CPVC depends on the specific pigment blend's oil absorption.
> 5. **Recommending carbon steel tanks for water-based** — wrong material; corrosion will occur.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rev/s** in HSD power formula — $N$ in $N^3$ MUST be in rev/s, not rpm.
